research_study_list.title: Breaking  Certified  Defenses:  Semantic  Adversarial  Examples  With  Spoofed  Robustness  Certificates
research_study_list.abstract:
To deflect adversarial attacks, a range of "certified" classifiers have been
proposed. In addition to labeling an image, certified classifiers produce (when
possible) a certificate guaranteeing that the input image is not an
$\ell_p$-bounded adversarial example. We present a new attack that exploits not
only the labelling function of a classifier, but also the certificate
generator. The proposed method applies large perturbations that place images
far from a class boundary while maintaining the imperceptibility property of
adversarial examples. The proposed "Shadow Attack" causes certifiably robust
networks to mislabel an image and simultaneously produce a "spoofed"
certificate of robustness.
research_study_list.full_text: BREAKING CERTIFIED DEFENSES : S EMANTIC ADVER - SARIAL EXAMPLES WITH SPOOFED ROBUSTNESS CER - TIFICATES Amin Ghiasi вҲ—, Ali ShafahiвҲ—& Tom Goldstein University of Maryland {amin,ashafahi,tomg}@cs.umd.edu ABSTRACT To deп¬Ӯect adversarial attacks, a range of вҖңcertiп¬ҒedвҖқ classiп¬Ғers have been proposed. In addition to labeling an image, certiп¬Ғed classiп¬Ғers produce (when possible) a certiп¬Ғcate guaranteeing that the input image is not an в„“p-bounded adversarial ex- ample. We present a new attack that exploits not only the labelling function of a classiп¬Ғer, but also the certiп¬Ғcate generator. The proposed method applies large perturbations that place images far from a class boundary while maintaining the imperceptibility property of adversarial examples. The proposed вҖңShadow At- tackвҖқ causes certiп¬Ғably robust networks to mislabel an image and simultaneously produce a вҖңspoofedвҖқ certiп¬Ғcate of robustness. 1 I NTRODUCTION Conventional training of neural networks has been shown to produce classiп¬Ғers that are highly sensi- tive to adversarial perturbations (Szegedy et al., 2013; Biggio et al., 2013), вҖңnatural lookingвҖқ images that have been manipulated to cause misclassiп¬Ғcation by a neural network (Figure 1). While a wide range of defenses exist that harden neural networks against such attacks (Madry et al., 2017; Shafahi et al., 2019), defenses based on heuristics and tricks are often easily breakable Athalye et al. (2018). This has motivated work on certiп¬Ғably secure networks вҖ” classiп¬Ғers that produce a label for an image, and also (when possible) a rigorous guarantee that the input is not adversarially manipulated (Cohen et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2019b). Natural macaw(0.6%) в„“вҲһ: 8/255 bucket(1.1%) Semantic bucket(0.6%) Shadow Attack bucket(0.6%) Figure 1: All adversarial examples have the goal of fooling classiп¬Ғers while looking вҖңnaturalвҖқ. Standard attacks limit the в„“p-norm of the perturbation, while semantic attacks have large в„“p-norm while still producing natural looking images. Our attack produces large but visually subtle seman- tic perturbations that not only cause misclassiп¬Ғcation, but also cause a certiп¬Ғed defense to issue a вҖңspoofedвҖқ high-conп¬Ғdence certiп¬Ғcate. In this case, a certiп¬Ғed Gaussian smoothing classiп¬Ғer misla- bels the image, and yet issues a certiп¬Ғcate with radius 0.24, which is a larger certiп¬Ғed radius than its corresponding unmodiп¬Ғed ImageNet image which is 0.13. вҲ—equal contribution 1 arXiv:2003.08937v1  [cs.LG]  19 Mar 2020To date, all work on certiп¬Ғable defenses has focused on deп¬Ӯecting в„“p-bounded attacks, where p= 2 or вҲһ(Cohen et al., 2019; Gowal et al., 2018; Wong et al., 2018). After labelling an image, these defenses then check whether there exists an image of a different label withinПөunits (in theв„“pmetric) of the input, where Пөis a security parameter chosen by the user. If the classiп¬Ғer assigns all images within the Пөball the same class label, then a certiп¬Ғcate is issued, and the input image known not to be an в„“p adversarial example. In this work, we demonstrate how a system that relies on certiп¬Ғcates as a measure of label security can be exploited. We present a new class of adversarial examples that target not only the classiп¬Ғer output label, but also the certiп¬Ғcate. We do this by adding adversarial perturbations to images that are large in the в„“p norm (larger than the Пө used by the certiп¬Ғcate generator), and produce attack images that are surrounded by a large ball exclusively containing images of the same label. The resulting attacks produce a вҖңspoofedвҖқ certiп¬Ғcate with a seemingly strong security guarantee despite being adversarially manipulated. Note that the statement made by the certiп¬Ғcate (i.e., that the input image is not an Пөadversarial example in the chosen norm) is still technically correct, however in this case the adversary is hiding behind a certiп¬Ғcate to avoid detection by a certiп¬Ғable defense. In summary, we consider methods that attack a certiп¬Ғed classiп¬Ғer in the following sense: вҖў Imperceptibility: the adversarial example вҖңlooks likeвҖқ its corresponding natural base ex- ample, вҖў Misclassiп¬Ғcation: the certiп¬Ғed classiп¬Ғer assigns an incorrect label to the adversarial exam- ple, and вҖў Strongly certiп¬Ғed: the certiп¬Ғed classiп¬Ғer provides a strong/large-radius certiп¬Ғcate for the adversarial example. While the existence of such an attack does not invalidate the certiп¬Ғcates produced by certiп¬Ғable systems, it should serve as a warning that certiп¬Ғable defenses are not inherently secure, and one should take caution when relying on certiп¬Ғcates as an indicator of label correctness. BACKGROUND In the white-box setting, where the attacker knows the victimвҖҷs network and parameters, adversarial perturbation are often constructed using п¬Ғrst-order gradient information (Carlini & Wagner, 2017; Kurakin et al., 2016; Moosavi-Dezfooli et al., 2016) or using approximations of the gradient (Uesato et al., 2018; Athalye et al., 2018). The prevailing formulation for crafting attacks uses an additive adversarial perturbation, and perceptibility is minimized using an в„“p-norm constraint. For example, в„“вҲһ-bounded attacks limit how much each pixel can move, while в„“0 adversarial attacks limit the number of pixels that can be modiп¬Ғed, without limiting the size of the change to each pixel (Wiyatno & Xu, 2018). It is possible to craft imperceptible attacks without using в„“p bounds (Brown et al., 2018). For example, Hosseini & Poovendran (2018) use shifting color channels, Wong et al. (2019) use the Wasserstein ball/distance, and Engstrom et al. (2017) use rotation and translation to craft вҖңseman- ticвҖқ adversarial examples. In Figure 1, we produce semantic adversarial examples using the method of Hosseini & Poovendran (2018) which is a greedy approach that transforms the image into HSV space, and then, while keeping V constant, tries to п¬Ғnd the smallest S perturbation causing mis- classiп¬Ғcation1. Other variants use generative models to construct natural looking images causing misclassiп¬Ғcation (Song et al., 2018; Dunn et al., 2019). In practice, many of the defenses which top adversarial defense leader-board challenges are non- certiп¬Ғed defenses (Madry et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2019a; Shafahi et al., 2019). The majority of these defenses make use of adversarial training, in which attack images are crafted during training and injected into the training set. These non-certiп¬Ғed defenses are mostly evaluated against PGD- based attacks, resulting in an upper-bound on robustness. Certiп¬Ғed defenses, on the other-hand, provably make networks resist в„“p-bounded perturbations of a certain radius. For instance, randomized smoothing (Cohen et al., 2019) is a certiп¬Ғable defense against в„“2-norm bounded attacks, and CROWN-IBP (Zhang et al., 2019b) is a certiп¬Ғable defense 1In п¬Ғg. 1, the adversarial example has saturation=0 2against lвҲһ-norm bounded perturbations. Both of these defenses produce a class label, and also a guarantee that the image could not have been crafted by making small perturbations to an image of a different label. Certiп¬Ғed defenses can also beneп¬Ғt from adversarial training. For example, Salman et al. (2019) recently improved the certiп¬Ғed radii of randomized smoothing (Cohen et al., 2019) by training on adversarial examples generated for the smoothed classiп¬Ғer. To the best of our knowledge, prior works have focused on making adversarial examples that sat- isfy the imperceptibility and misclassiп¬Ғcation conditions, but none have investigated manipulating certiп¬Ғcates, which is our focus here. The reminder of this paper is organized as follows. In section 2 we introduce our new approach Shadow Attack for generating adversarial perturbations. This is a hybrid model that allows various kinds of attacks to be compounded together, resulting in perturbations of large radii. In section 3 we present an attack on вҖңrandomized smoothingвҖқ certiп¬Ғcates (Cohen et al., 2019). Section 4 shows an ablation study which illustrates why the elements of the Shadow Attack are important for successfully manipulating certiп¬Ғed models. In section 5 we generate adversarial examples for вҖңCROWN-IBPвҖқ (Zhang et al., 2019b). Finally, we discuss results and wrap up in section 6. 2 T HE SHADOW ATTACK Because certiп¬Ғcate spooп¬Ғng requires large perturbations (larger than the в„“p ball of the certiп¬Ғcate), we propose a simple attack that ensembles numerous modes to create large perturbations. Our attack can be seen as the generalization of the well-known PGD attack, which creates adversarial images by modifying a clean base image. Given a loss function Land an в„“p-norm bound Пөfor some pвүҘ0, PGD attacks solve the following optimization problem: max Оҙ L(Оё,x + Оҙ) (1) s.t. вҲҘОҙвҲҘp вүӨПө, (2) where Оё are the network parameters and Оҙ is the adversarial perturbation to be added to the clean input image x. Constraint 2 promotes imperceptibility of the resulting perturbation to the human eye by limiting the perturbation size. In the shadow attack, instead of solving the above constrained optimization problem, we solve the following problem with a range of penalties: max Оҙ L(Оё,x + Оҙ) вҲ’О»cC(Оҙ) вҲ’О»tvTV (Оҙ) вҲ’О»sDissim(Оҙ), (3) where О»c,О»tv,О»s are scalar penalty weights. Penalty TV (Оҙ) forces the perturbation Оҙto have small total variation (TV ), and so appear more smooth and natural. Penalty C(Оҙ) limits the perturbation Оҙ globally by constraining the change in the mean of each color channel c. This constraint is needed since total variation is invariant to constant/scalar additions to each color channel, and it is desirable to suppress extreme changes in the color balances of images. Penalty Dissim(Оҙ) promotes perturbations Оҙ that assume similar values in each color channel. In the case of an RGB image of shape 3 Г—W Г—H, if Dissim(Оҙ) is small, the perturbations to red, green, and blue channels are similar, i.e., ОҙR,w,h вүҲОҙG,w,h вүҲОҙB,w,h,вҲҖ(w,h) вҲҲW Г—H. This amounts to making the pixels darker/lighter, without changing the color balance of the image. Later, in section 3, we suggest two ways of enforcing such similarity between RGB channels and we п¬Ғnd both of them effective: вҖў 1-channel attack strictly enforces ОҙR,i вүҲОҙG,i вүҲОҙB,i,вҲҖi by using just one array to si- multaneously represent each color channel ОҙWГ—H.On the forward pass, we duplicate Оҙto make a 3-channel image. In this case, Dissim(Оҙ) = 0, and the perturbation is greyscale. вҖў 3-channel attack uses a 3-channel perturbationОҙ3Г—WГ—H, along with the dissimilarity met- ric Dissim(Оҙ) = вҲҘОҙR вҲ’ОҙBвҲҘp + вҲҘОҙR вҲ’ОҙGвҲҘp + вҲҘОҙB вҲ’ОҙGвҲҘp. All together, the three penalties minimize perceptibility of perturbations by forcing them to be (a) small, (b) smooth, and (c) without dramatic color changes (e.g. swapping blue to red). At the same time, these penalties allow perturbations that are very large in в„“p-norm. 32.1 C REATING UNTARGETED ATTACKS We focus on spooп¬Ғng certiп¬Ғcates for untargeted attacks, in which the attacker does not specify the class into which the attack image moves. To achieve this, we generate an adversarial perturbation for all possible wrong classes ВҜyand choose the best one as our strong attack: max ВҜyМё=y,Оҙ вҲ’L(Оё,x + ОҙвҲҘВҜy) вҲ’О»cC(Оҙ) вҲ’О»tvTV (Оҙ) вҲ’О»sDissim(Оҙ) (4) where yis the true label/class for the clean image x, and Lis a spooп¬Ғng loss that promotes a strong certiп¬Ғcate. We examine different choices for Lfor different certiп¬Ғcates below. 3 A TTACKS ON RANDOMIZED SMOOTHING The Randomized Smoothing method, п¬Ғrst proposed by Lecuyer et al. (2018) and later improved by Li et al. (2018), is an adversarial defense againstв„“2-norm bounded attacks. Cohen et al. (2019) prove a tight robustness guarantee under the в„“2 norm for smoothing with Gaussian noise. Their study was the п¬Ғrst certiп¬Ғable defense for the ImageNet dataset (Deng et al., 2009). The method constructs certiп¬Ғcates by п¬Ғrst creating many copies of an input image contaminated with random Gaussian noise of standard deviation Пғ. Then, it uses a base classiп¬Ғer (a neural net) to make a prediction for all of the images in the randomly augmented batch. Depending on the level of the consensus of the class labels at these random images, a certiп¬Ғed radius is calculated that can be at most 4Пғ (in the case of perfect consensus). Intuitively, if the image is far away from the decision boundary, the base classiп¬Ғer should predict the same label for each noisy copy of the test image, in which case the certiп¬Ғcate is strong. On the other hand, if the image is adjacent to the decision boundary, the base classiп¬ҒerвҖҷs predictions for the Gaussian augmented copies may vary. If the variation is large, the smoothed classiп¬Ғer abstains from making a prediction. To spoof strong certiп¬Ғcates (large certiп¬Ғed radius) for an incorrect class, we must make sure that the majority of a batch of noisy images around the adversarial image are assigned the same (wrong) label. We do this by minimizing the cross entropy loss relative to a chosen (incorrect) label, averaged over a large set of randomly perturbed images. To this end, we minimize equation 4, where Lis chosen to be the average cross-entropy over a batch of Gaussian perturbed copies. This method is analogous to the technique presented by Shafahi et al. (2018) for generating universal perturbations that are effective when added to a large number of different images. RESULTS Cohen et al. (2019) show the performance of the Gaussian smoothed classiп¬Ғer on CIFAR-10 (Krizhevsky et al.) and ImageNet (Deng et al., 2009). To attack the CIFAR-10 and ImageNet smoothed classiп¬Ғers, we use 400 randomly sampled Gaussian images, О»tv = 0 .3, О»c = 1 .0, and perform 300 steps of SGD with learning rate 0.1. We choose the functional regularizers C(Оҙ) and TV (Оҙ) to be C(Оҙ) = вҲҘAvg(|ОҙR|),Avg(|ОҙG|),Avg(|ОҙB|)вҲҘ2 2, and TV (Оҙi,j) = anisotropic-TV(Оҙi,j)2, where |В·| is the element-wise absolute value operator, and Avg computes the average. For the Dissim regularizer, we experiment with both the 1-Channel attack that ensures Dissim(Оҙ) = 0 , and the 3-Channel attack by setting Dissim(Оҙ) = вҲҘ(ОҙR вҲ’ОҙG)2,(ОҙR вҲ’ОҙB)2,(ОҙG вҲ’ОҙB)2вҲҘ2 and О»s = 0 .5. For the validation examples on which the smoothed classiп¬Ғer does not abstain (see Cohen et al. (2019) for more details), the less-constrained 3-channel attack is always able to п¬Ғnd an adversarial example while the 1-channel attack also performs well, achieving 98.5% success. 2 In section 4 we will discuss in more detail other differences between 1-channel and 3-channel attacks. The results are summarized in Table 1. For the various base-models and choices of Пғ, our adversarial examples are able to produce certiп¬Ғed radii that are on average larger than the certiп¬Ғed radii produced for natural images. The adversarial perturbation is smooth and natural looking even-though it is large when measured using в„“p-metrics. Also see Figure 16 in the appendix. Figure 2 depicts a sample adversarial example built for the smoothed ImageNet classiп¬Ғer that pro- duces a strong certiп¬Ғcate. The adversarial perturbation causes the batch of Gaussian augmented black swan images to get misclassiп¬Ғed as hooks. For more, see appendix 16. 4 A BLATION STUDY OF THE ATTACK PARAMETERS In this section we perform an ablation study on the parameters of the Shadow Attack to evaluate (i) the number of SGD steps needed, (ii) the importance of О»s (or alternatively using 1-channel attacks), and (iii) the effect of О»tv. The default parameters for all of the experiments are as follows unless explicitly mentioned: We use 30 SGD steps with learning rate 0.1 for the optimization. All experiments except part (ii) use 1-channel attacks for the sake of simplicity and efп¬Ғciency (since it has less parameters). We assume О»tv = 0.3, О»c = 20,and use batch-size 50. We present results using the п¬Ғrst example from each class of the CIFAR-10 validation set. Figure 3 shows how the adversarial example evolves during the п¬Ғrst few steps of optimization (See appendix 13 for more examples). Also, п¬Ғgures 4, 5, and 6 show the evolution of L(Оҙ), TV (Оҙ), and C(Оҙ), respectively (Note that we use 1-channel attacks, so Dissim(Оҙ) is always 0). See table 15 for more images, and п¬Ғgure 8 for the impact of parameters on TV (Оҙ). 5 A TTACKS ON CROWN-IBP Interval Bound Propagation (IBP) methods have been recently studied as a defense against в„“вҲһ- bounded attacks. Many recent studies such as Gowal et al. (2018); Xiao et al. (2018); Wong et al. (2018); Mirman et al. (2018) have investigated IBP methods to train provably robust networks. To the best of our knowledge, the CROWN-IBP method by Zhang et al. (2019b) achieves state-of- the-art performance for MNIST (LeCun & Cortes, 2010), Fashion-MNIST (Xiao et al., 2017), and CIFAR-10 datasets among certiп¬Ғableв„“вҲһdefenses. In this section we focus on attacking Zhang et al. (2019b) using CIFAR-10. IBP methods (over)estimate how much a small в„“вҲһ-bounded noise in the input can impact the clas- siп¬Ғcation layer. This is done by propagating errors from layer to layer, computing bounds on the maximum possible perturbation to each activation. During testing, the user chooses an в„“вҲһ per- turbation bound Пө,and error propagation is used to bound the magnitude of the largest achievable perturbation in network output. If the output perturbation is not large enough to п¬Ӯip the image la- bel, then a certiп¬Ғcate is produced. If the output perturbation is large enough to п¬Ӯip the image label, 0.0 0.03 0.06 0.09 0.12 0.15 0.18 0.21 0.24 0.27 0.30 original Figure 12: The visual effect of О»tv on the on imperceptibility of the perturbations. The п¬Ғrst row shows the value of О»tv 7Table 2: вҖңRobust errorвҖқ for natural images, and вҖңattack errorвҖқ for Shadow Attack images using the CIFAR-10 dataset, and CROWN-IBP models. Smaller is better. Пө(lвҲһ) Model Family Method Robustness Errors Min Mean Max 2/255 9 small models CROWN-IPB 52.46 57.55 60.67 Shadow Attack 45.90 53.89 65.74 8 large models CROWN-IBP 52.52 53.9 56.05 Shadow Attack 46.21 49.77 51.79 8/255 9 small models CROWN-IBP 71.28 72.15 73.66 Shadow Attack 63.43 66.94 71.02 8 large models CROWN-IBP 70.79 71.17 72.29 Shadow Attack 64.04 67.32 71.16 then a certiп¬Ғcate is not produced. During network training, IBP methods include a term in the loss function that promotes tight error bounds that result in certiп¬Ғcates. Our attack directly uses the loss function term used during IBP network training in addition to the Shadow Attack penalties; we search for an image that is visually similar to the base image, while producing a bound on the output perturbation that is too small to п¬Ӯip the image label. Note that there is a subtle difference between crafting conventional adversarial examples which ultimately targets misclassiп¬Ғcation and our adversarial examples which aim to produce adversarial examples which cause misclassiп¬Ғcation and produce strong certiп¬Ғcates. In the former case, we only need to attack the cross-entropy loss. If we use our Shadow Attack to craft adversarial examples based on the cross-entropy loss, the robust- ness errors are on average roughly 50% larger than those reported in table 2 (i.e., simple adversarial examples produce weaker certiп¬Ғcates.) We attack 4 classes of networks released by Zhang et al. (2019b) for CIFAR-10. There are two classes of IBP architectures, one of them consists of 9 small models and the other consists of 8 larger models. For each class of architecture, there are two sets of pre-trained models: one for Пө = 2/255 and one for Пө = 8/255. We use О»tv = 0.000009, О»c = 0.02, C(Оҙ) = вҲҘОҙвҲҘ2 and set the learning rate to 200 and for the rest of the regularizers and hyper-paramters we use the same hyper- parameters and regularizers as in 3. For the sake of efп¬Ғciency, we only do 1-channel attacks. We attack the 4 classes of models and for each class, we report the min, mean, and max of the robustness errors and compare them with those of the CROWN-IBP paper. To quantify the success of our attack, we deп¬Ғne two metrics of error. For natural images, we report the rate of вҖңrobust errors,вҖқ which are images that are either (i) incorrectly labeled, or (ii) correctly labelled but without a certiп¬Ғcate. In contrast, for attack images, we report the rate of вҖңattack errors,вҖқ which are either (i) correctly classiп¬Ғed or (ii) incorrectly classiп¬Ғed but without a certiп¬Ғcate. Table 2 shows the robust error on natural images, and the attack error on Shadow Attack images for the CROWN-IBP models. With Пө = 2/255, our attack п¬Ғnds adversarial examples that certify roughly 15% of the time (i.e., attack error <85%). With Пө = 8/255, our attack п¬Ғnds adversarial examples that are incorrectly classiп¬Ғed, and yet certify even more often than natural images. 6 CONCLUSION We demonstrate that it is possible to produce adversarial examples with вҖңspoofedвҖқ certiп¬Ғed ro- bustness by using large-norm perturbations. Our adversarial examples are built using our Shadow Attack that produces smooth and natural looking perturbations that are often less perceptible than those of the commonly used в„“p-bounded perturbations, while being large enough in norm to escape the certiп¬Ғcation regions of state of the art principled defenses. This work suggests that the certiп¬Ғ- cates produced by certiп¬Ғably robust classiп¬Ғers, while mathematically rigorous, are not always good indicators of robustness or accuracy. Dataset Пғ(l2) Adversarially Trained Randomized Smoothed Shadow Attack Mean STD Mean STD CIFAR-10 0.5 0.60 0.34 0.65 0.16 IMAGE NET RESULTS Many of the recent studies have explored the semantic attacks. Semantic attacks are powerful for attacking defenses (Engstrom et al., 2017; Hosseini & Poovendran, 2018; Laidlaw & Feizi, 2019). Many of semantic attacks are applicable to Imagenet, however, none of them consider increasing the radii of the certiп¬Ғcates generated by the certiп¬Ғable defenses. Some other works focus on using generative models to generate adversarial examples (Song et al., 2018), but unfortunately none of the GANвҖҷs are expressive enough to capture the manifold of the ImageNet. Figure 16 illustrates some of our successful examples generated by Shadow Attack to attack Ran- domized Smoothed classiп¬Ғers for ImageNet. B C ERTIFICATE SPOOFING ATTACKS ON ADVERSARIALLY TRAINED SMOOTH CLASSIFIERS Recently, Salman et al. (2019) signiп¬Ғcantly improved the robustness of Gaussian smoothed classi- п¬Ғers by generating adversarial examples for the smoothed classiп¬Ғer and training on them. In this section, we use our Shadow Attack to generate adversarial examples using the loss in eq. 4 for the SmoothAdv classiп¬Ғer 4. Due to computation limitations, we attack a sample of 40% of the same validation images used for evaluating the randomized smooth classiп¬Ғer in section 3. The results are summarized in table 3. Comparing the results of table 1 to table 3, we can see that the SmoothAdv classiп¬Ғer, does produce stronger certiп¬Ғed radii for natural examples (many of the examples in fact have the maximum radii) compared to the original randomized smoothing classiп¬Ғer. This can be associate to the excessive invariance introduced as a result of adversarial training. However, table 3 empirically veriп¬Ғes that its certiп¬Ғcates are still subject to attacks and the certiп¬Ғcate should not be used as a measure for robustness. 4We use the ofп¬Ғcial models released at https://github.com/Hadisalman/smoothing-adversarial for attacking the SmoothAdv classiп¬Ғer. 15Figure 16: Natural looking Imperceptible ImageNet adversarial images which produce large certi- п¬Ғed radii for the ImageNet Gaussian smoothed classiп¬Ғer. 16

llm_extracted_info.main_contributions: Introduces Shadow Attack, a semantic adversarial method that simultaneously (i) causes misclassification and (ii) вҖңspoofsвҖқ certified robustness by making certified defenses output large/strong certificates for adversarial inputs. Demonstrates attacks against two state-of-the-art certified defensesвҖ”Randomized Smoothing (в„“2) and CROWN-IBP (в„“вҲһ)вҖ”on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet, showing adversarial examples can receive larger certified radii than their natural counterparts. Provides ablation studies and extends results to adversarially trained smoothed classifiers (SmoothAdv), again showing spoofed certificates.
llm_extracted_info.methodology: Formulates adversarial example generation as unconstrained optimization with perceptual penalties: maximize L(Оё, x+Оҙ) вҲ’ О»c C(Оҙ) вҲ’ О»tv TV(Оҙ) вҲ’ О»s Dissim(Оҙ). Penalties: TV(Оҙ) for smoothness; C(Оҙ) to limit per-channel mean shifts; Dissim(Оҙ) to encourage similar perturbations across RGB channels. Two variants: (1) 1-channel attack (enforces ОҙRвүҲОҙGвүҲОҙB via a single shared map; Dissim=0), and (2) 3-channel attack with Dissim(Оҙ)=||ОҙRвҲ’ОҙB||p+||ОҙRвҲ’ОҙG||p+||ОҙBвҲ’ОҙG||p. Untargeted setting: optimize over all incorrect labels and pick the best. For Randomized Smoothing, L is the average cross-entropy over a batch of Gaussian-perturbed copies of x+Оҙ to enforce high consensus for a wrong label. For CROWN-IBP, incorporates the IBP training-time loss that bounds output perturbations, seeking adversarial images that both misclassify and still satisfy the certifiability condition. Uses SGD to optimize Оҙ with specified hyperparameters.
llm_extracted_info.experimental_setup: Datasets and models: (a) Randomized Smoothing (Cohen et al., 2019) on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet; (b) CROWN-IBP (Zhang et al., 2019b) on CIFAR-10 (9 small and 8 large model families, with ОөвҲҲ{2/255, 8/255}); (c) SmoothAdv (Salman et al., 2019) on CIFAR-10. RS attack settings: 400 Gaussian samples per step; 300 SGD steps; lr=0.1; О»tv=0.3; О»c=1.0; C(Оҙ)=||Avg(|ОҙR|),Avg(|ОҙG|),Avg(|ОҙB|)||^2; TV is anisotropic-TV; 3-channel Dissim uses squared differences with О»s=0.5; both 1-channel and 3-channel tested. Results (RS): On CIFAR-10 and ImageNet for various Пғ (e.g., ПғвҲҲ{0.12,0.25,0.30,0.50,1.0}), adversarial images typically receive larger certified radii than natural images; 3-channel achieves near 100% success on non-abstaining samples; 1-channel achieves 98.5% success. For ImageNet, due to compute, targeted wrong labels limited to class IDs {100,200,...,1000}. Ablations: Tiny-CIFAR-10 subset (1 image per class), default 30 steps, batch size 50; varied О»s (channel similarity) and О»tv (smoothness) and tracked L, TV, C, and resulting certificate radii histograms. CROWN-IBP attack settings: 1-channel attack, О»tv=9e-6, О»c=0.02, C(Оҙ)=||Оҙ||2, lr=200; other settings similar. Metrics: for natural images, вҖңrobust errorвҖқ = misclassified or correctly classified without certificate; for attack images, вҖңattack errorвҖқ = correctly classified or misclassified without certificate. Results (CROWN-IBP): For Оө=2/255, attack errors around mid-50% (min/mean/max ranges reported), implying many adversarial images still certify; for Оө=8/255, adversarial images certify even more often than natural images (mean attack error ~67% vs robust error ~71вҖ“72%). SmoothAdv (Пғ=0.5 on CIFAR-10): Natural mean certified radius 0.60 (std 0.34) vs Shadow Attack mean 0.65 (std 0.16), showing spoofing persists under adversarially trained smoothing.
llm_extracted_info.limitations: Assumes white-box access to models and certification procedures. Computationally intensive (e.g., 400 Gaussian samples, 300 optimization steps). ImageNet experiments attack only a subset of target classes due to compute limits. Focuses on untargeted attacks; targeted spoofing not fully explored. Perceptual imperceptibility is enforced via heuristic penalties (TV, channel constraints) without user studies or formal perceptual metrics. Evaluations primarily on в„“2-smoothed and в„“вҲһ-IBP certified defenses; generalization to other certification frameworks or modalities is untested. Does not propose defenses or detection methods. Some RS results exclude abstaining cases; physical-world robustness not evaluated.
llm_extracted_info.future_research_directions: - Develop certification frameworks robust to large, semantic perturbations and off-manifold shifts, e.g., manifold-aware or perceptual-metric-based certificates. - Combine certified defenses with distribution shift detection and input filtering to flag potential certificate spoofing. - Adversarial training regimes explicitly targeting certificate-spoofing resistance (e.g., adversarially training on spoof attempts). - Extend spoofing analyses to other modalities (NLP, audio), tasks (detection/segmentation), and physical-world settings. - Investigate targeted certificate spoofing, black-box threat models, and query-efficient variants. - Incorporate stronger perceptual similarity models (learned perceptual metrics, human studies) to better quantify imperceptibility. - Design auditing and calibration methods to measure when certificates correlate with true correctness, and diagnostics for abstention policies.
llm_extracted_info.experimental_code:
# Extracted code relevant to the requested method aspects present in this repository
# - Optimization of a perturbation delta by SGD to maximize (untargeted) loss
# - Randomized Smoothing loss over multiple Gaussian-perturbed copies (multi-noise vectors)
# NOTE: The repository does NOT contain implementations of TV(Оҙ), per-channel mean penalty C(Оҙ),
#       or cross-channel Dissimilarity(Оҙ) penalties, nor a 1-channel perturbation variant, nor CROWN-IBP integration.

# File: code/attacks.py (relevant parts)
from abc import ABCMeta, abstractmethod
import numpy as np
import torch
from torch.autograd import Variable
from torch.nn import CrossEntropyLoss
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F
import torch.optim as optim
from typing import Optional

class Attacker(metaclass=ABCMeta):
    @abstractmethod
    def attack(self, inputs, targets):
        raise NotImplementedError

# PGD with L2 constraint and support for multi-noise smoothing loss
class PGD_L2(Attacker):
    def __init__(self,
                 steps: int,
                 random_start: bool = True,
                 max_norm: Optional[float] = None,
                 device: torch.device = torch.device('cpu')) -> None:
        super(PGD_L2, self).__init__()
        self.steps = steps
        self.random_start = random_start
        self.max_norm = max_norm
        self.device = device

    def attack(self, model: nn.Module, inputs: torch.Tensor, labels: torch.Tensor,
               noise: torch.Tensor = None, num_noise_vectors=1, targeted: bool = False, no_grad=False) -> torch.Tensor:
        if num_noise_vectors == 1:
            return self._attack(model, inputs, labels, noise, targeted)
        else:
            if no_grad:
                with torch.no_grad():
                    return self._attack_mutlinoise_no_grad(model, inputs, labels, noise, num_noise_vectors, targeted)
            else:
                return self._attack_mutlinoise(model, inputs, labels, noise, num_noise_vectors, targeted)

    # single-noise variant (standard CE), untargeted by default
    def _attack(self, model: nn.Module, inputs: torch.Tensor, labels: torch.Tensor,
               noise: torch.Tensor = None, targeted: bool = False) -> torch.Tensor:
        if inputs.min() < 0 or inputs.max() > 1: raise ValueError('Input values should be in the [0, 1] range.')
        batch_size = inputs.shape[0]
        multiplier = 1 if targeted else -1
        delta = torch.zeros_like(inputs, requires_grad=True)
        optimizer = optim.SGD([delta], lr=self.max_norm/self.steps*2)

        for i in range(self.steps):
            adv = inputs + delta
            if noise is not None:
                adv = adv + noise
            logits = model(adv)
            ce_loss = F.cross_entropy(logits, labels, reduction='sum')
            loss = multiplier * ce_loss

            optimizer.zero_grad()
            loss.backward()
            grad_norms = delta.grad.view(batch_size, -1).norm(p=2, dim=1)
            delta.grad.div_(grad_norms.view(-1, 1, 1, 1))
            if (grad_norms == 0).any():
                delta.grad[grad_norms == 0] = torch.randn_like(delta.grad[grad_norms == 0])
            optimizer.step()

            delta.data.add_(inputs)
            delta.data.clamp_(0, 1).sub_(inputs)
            delta.data.renorm_(p=2, dim=0, maxnorm=self.max_norm)
        return inputs + delta

    # multi-noise smoothing loss: log of mean softmax across noise copies, then NLL with labels
    def _attack_mutlinoise(self, model: nn.Module, inputs: torch.Tensor, labels: torch.Tensor,
               noise: torch.Tensor = None, num_noise_vectors: int = 1, targeted: bool = False) -> torch.Tensor:
        if inputs.min() < 0 or inputs.max() > 1: raise ValueError('Input values should be in the [0, 1] range.')
        batch_size = labels.shape[0]
        multiplier = 1 if targeted else -1
        delta = torch.zeros((len(labels), *inputs.shape[1:]), requires_grad=True, device=self.device)
        optimizer = optim.SGD([delta], lr=self.max_norm/self.steps*2)

        for i in range(self.steps):
            adv = inputs + delta.repeat(1,num_noise_vectors,1,1).view_as(inputs)
            if noise is not None:
                adv = adv + noise
            logits = model(adv)

            # average probabilities across noise and use NLL on log-averaged softmax
            softmax = F.softmax(logits, dim=1)
            average_softmax = softmax.reshape(-1, num_noise_vectors, logits.shape[-1]).mean(1, keepdim=True).squeeze(1)
            logsoftmax = torch.log(average_softmax.clamp(min=1e-20))
            ce_loss = F.nll_loss(logsoftmax, labels)
            loss = multiplier * ce_loss

            optimizer.zero_grad()
            loss.backward()
            grad_norms = delta.grad.view(batch_size, -1).norm(p=2, dim=1)
            delta.grad.div_(grad_norms.view(-1, 1, 1, 1))
            if (grad_norms == 0).any():
                delta.grad[grad_norms == 0] = torch.randn_like(delta.grad[grad_norms == 0])
            optimizer.step()

            delta.data.add_(inputs[::num_noise_vectors])
            delta.data.clamp_(0, 1).sub_(inputs[::num_noise_vectors])
            delta.data.renorm_(p=2, dim=0, maxnorm=self.max_norm)
        return inputs + delta.repeat(1,num_noise_vectors,1,1).view_as(inputs)

    # multi-noise, approximate no-grad trick variant
    def _attack_mutlinoise_no_grad(self, model: nn.Module, inputs: torch.Tensor, labels: torch.Tensor,
               noise: torch.Tensor = None, num_noise_vectors: int = 1,targeted: bool = False) -> torch.Tensor:
        if inputs.min() < 0 or inputs.max() > 1: raise ValueError('Input values should be in the [0, 1] range.')
        batch_size = labels.shape[0]
        delta = torch.zeros((len(labels), *inputs.shape[1:]), requires_grad=True, device=self.device)
        optimizer = optim.SGD([delta], lr=self.max_norm/self.steps*2)

        for i in range(self.steps):
            adv = inputs + delta.repeat(1,num_noise_vectors,1,1).view_as(inputs)
            if noise is not None:
                adv = adv + noise
            logits = model(adv)

            softmax = F.softmax(logits, dim=1)
            grad = F.nll_loss(softmax,  labels.unsqueeze(1).repeat(1,1,num_noise_vectors).view(batch_size*num_noise_vectors), 
                            reduction='none').repeat(*noise.shape[1:],1).permute(3,0,1,2)*noise
            grad = grad.reshape(-1, num_noise_vectors, *inputs.shape[1:]).mean(1)            
            grad_norms = grad.view(batch_size, -1).norm(p=2, dim=1)
            grad.div_(grad_norms.view(-1, 1, 1, 1))
            if (grad_norms == 0).any():
                grad[grad_norms == 0] = torch.randn_like(grad[grad_norms == 0])

            delta = delta + grad*self.max_norm/self.steps*2
            delta.data.add_(inputs[::num_noise_vectors])
            delta.data.clamp_(0, 1).sub_(inputs[::num_noise_vectors])
            delta.data.renorm_(p=2, dim=0, maxnorm=self.max_norm)
        return inputs + delta.repeat(1,num_noise_vectors,1,1).view_as(inputs)

# DDN with multi-noise smoothing loss (averaged softmax) and L2 norm decoupling
class DDN(Attacker):
    def __init__(self,
                 steps: int,
                 gamma: float = 0.05,
                 init_norm: float = 1.,
                 quantize: bool = True,
                 levels: int = 256,
                 max_norm: Optional[float] = None,
                 device: torch.device = torch.device('cpu'),
                 callback: Optional = None) -> None:
        super(DDN, self).__init__()
        self.steps = steps
        self.gamma = gamma
        self.init_norm = init_norm
        self.quantize = quantize
        self.levels = levels
        self.max_norm = max_norm
        self.device = device
        self.callback = callback

    def attack(self, model: nn.Module, inputs: torch.Tensor, labels: torch.Tensor,
               noise: torch.Tensor = None, num_noise_vectors=1, targeted: bool = False, no_grad=False) -> torch.Tensor:
        if num_noise_vectors == 1:
            return self._attack(model, inputs, labels, noise, targeted)
        else:
            if no_grad:
                raise NotImplementedError
            else:
                return self._attack_mutlinoise(model, inputs, labels, noise, num_noise_vectors, targeted)

    def _attack(self, model: nn.Module, inputs: torch.Tensor, labels: torch.Tensor,
               noise: torch.Tensor = None, targeted: bool = False) -> torch.Tensor:
        if inputs.min() < 0 or inputs.max() > 1: raise ValueError('Input values should be in the [0, 1] range.')
        batch_size = inputs.shape[0]
        multiplier = 1 if targeted else -1
        delta = torch.zeros_like(inputs, requires_grad=True)
        norm = torch.full((batch_size,), self.init_norm, device=self.device, dtype=torch.float)
        worst_norm = torch.max(inputs, 1 - inputs).view(batch_size, -1).norm(p=2, dim=1)
        optimizer = optim.SGD([delta], lr=1)
        scheduler = optim.lr_scheduler.CosineAnnealingLR(optimizer, T_max=self.steps, eta_min=0.01)

        best_l2 = worst_norm.clone()
        best_delta = torch.zeros_like(inputs)
        adv_found = torch.zeros(inputs.size(0), dtype=torch.uint8, device=self.device)

        for i in range(self.steps):
            scheduler.step()
            l2 = delta.data.view(batch_size, -1).norm(p=2, dim=1)
            adv = inputs + delta
            if noise is not None:
                adv = adv + noise
            logits = model(adv)
            ce_loss = F.cross_entropy(logits, labels, reduction='sum')
            loss = multiplier * ce_loss

            pred_labels = logits.argmax(1)
            is_adv = (pred_labels == labels) if targeted else (pred_labels != labels)
            is_smaller = l2 < best_l2
            is_both = is_adv * is_smaller
            adv_found[is_both] = 1
            best_l2[is_both] = l2[is_both]
            best_delta[is_both] = delta.data[is_both]

            optimizer.zero_grad()
            loss.backward()
            grad_norms = delta.grad.view(batch_size, -1).norm(p=2, dim=1)
            delta.grad.div_(grad_norms.view(-1, 1, 1, 1))
            if (grad_norms == 0).any():
                delta.grad[grad_norms == 0] = torch.randn_like(delta.grad[grad_norms == 0])

            optimizer.step()

            norm.mul_(1 - (2 * is_adv.float() - 1) * self.gamma)
            norm = torch.min(norm, worst_norm)

            delta.data.mul_((norm / delta.data.view(batch_size, -1).norm(2, 1)).view(-1, 1, 1, 1))
            delta.data.add_(inputs)
            if self.quantize:
                delta.data.mul_(self.levels - 1).round_().div_(self.levels - 1)
            delta.data.clamp_(0, 1).sub_(inputs)

        if self.max_norm is not None:
            best_delta.renorm_(p=2, dim=0, maxnorm=self.max_norm)
            if self.quantize:
                best_delta.mul_(self.levels - 1).round_().div_(self.levels - 1)
        return inputs + best_delta

    # multi-noise smoothing loss inside DDN
    def _attack_mutlinoise(self, model: nn.Module, inputs: torch.Tensor, labels: torch.Tensor,
               noise: torch.Tensor = None, num_noise_vectors: int = 1,targeted: bool = False) -> torch.Tensor:
        if inputs.min() < 0 or inputs.max() > 1: raise ValueError('Input values should be in the [0, 1] range.')
        batch_size = labels.shape[0]
        multiplier = 1 if targeted else -1
        delta = torch.zeros((len(labels), *inputs.shape[1:]), requires_grad=True, device=self.device)
        norm = torch.full((batch_size,), self.init_norm, device=self.device, dtype=torch.float)
        worst_norm = torch.max(inputs[::num_noise_vectors], 1 - inputs[::num_noise_vectors]).view(batch_size, -1).norm(p=2, dim=1)

        optimizer = optim.SGD([delta], lr=1)
        scheduler = optim.lr_scheduler.CosineAnnealingLR(optimizer, T_max=self.steps, eta_min=0.01)

        best_l2 = worst_norm.clone()
        best_delta = torch.zeros_like(inputs[::num_noise_vectors])
        adv_found = torch.zeros(inputs[::num_noise_vectors].size(0), dtype=torch.uint8, device=self.device)

        for i in range(self.steps):
            scheduler.step()

            l2 = delta.data.view(batch_size, -1).norm(p=2, dim=1)
            adv = inputs + delta.repeat(1,num_noise_vectors,1,1).view_as(inputs)
            if noise is not None:
                adv = adv + noise
            logits = model(adv)

            softmax = F.softmax(logits, dim=1)
            average_softmax = softmax.reshape(-1, num_noise_vectors, logits.shape[-1]).mean(1, keepdim=True).squeeze(1)
            logsoftmax = torch.log(average_softmax.clamp(min=1e-20))
            ce_loss = F.nll_loss(logsoftmax, labels)
            loss = multiplier * ce_loss

            pred_labels = logits.argmax(1).reshape(-1, num_noise_vectors).mode(1)[0]
            is_adv = (pred_labels == labels) if targeted else (pred_labels != labels)
            is_smaller = l2 < best_l2
            is_both = is_adv * is_smaller
            adv_found[is_both] = 1
            best_l2[is_both] = l2[is_both]
            best_delta[is_both] = delta.data[is_both]

            optimizer.zero_grad()
            loss.backward()
            grad_norms = delta.grad.view(batch_size, -1).norm(p=2, dim=1)
            delta.grad.div_(grad_norms.view(-1, 1, 1, 1))
            if (grad_norms == 0).any():
                delta.grad[grad_norms == 0] = torch.randn_like(delta.grad[grad_norms == 0])

            optimizer.step()

            norm.mul_(1 - (2 * is_adv.float() - 1) * self.gamma)
            norm = torch.min(norm, worst_norm)
            delta.data.mul_((norm / delta.data.view(batch_size, -1).norm(2, 1)).view(-1, 1, 1, 1))

            delta.data.add_(inputs[::num_noise_vectors])
            if self.quantize:
                delta.data.mul_(self.levels - 1).round_().div_(self.levels - 1)
            delta.data.clamp_(0, 1).sub_(inputs[::num_noise_vectors])

        if self.max_norm is not None:
            best_delta.renorm_(p=2, dim=0, maxnorm=self.max_norm)
            if self.quantize:
                best_delta.mul_(self.levels - 1).round_().div_(self.levels - 1)
        return inputs + best_delta.repeat(1,num_noise_vectors,1,1).view_as(inputs)

# File: code/core.py (Randomized Smoothing wrapper for prediction/certification)
from math import ceil
import numpy as np
from scipy.stats import norm, binom_test
from statsmodels.stats.proportion import proportion_confint
import torch

class Smooth(object):
    ABSTAIN = -1
    def __init__(self, base_classifier: torch.nn.Module, num_classes: int, sigma: float):
        self.base_classifier = base_classifier
        self.num_classes = num_classes
        self.sigma = sigma

    def certify(self, x: torch.tensor, n0: int, n: int, alpha: float, batch_size: int) -> (int, float):
        self.base_classifier.eval()
        counts_selection = self._sample_noise(x, n0, batch_size)
        cAHat = counts_selection.argmax().item()
        counts_estimation = self._sample_noise(x, n, batch_size)
        nA = counts_estimation[cAHat].item()
        pABar = self._lower_confidence_bound(nA, n, alpha)
        if pABar < 0.5:
            return Smooth.ABSTAIN, 0.0
        else:
            radius = self.sigma * norm.ppf(pABar)
            return cAHat, radius

    def predict(self, x: torch.tensor, n: int, alpha: float, batch_size: int) -> int:
        self.base_classifier.eval()
        counts = self._sample_noise(x, n, batch_size)
        top2 = counts.argsort()[::-1][:2]
        count1 = counts[top2[0]]
        count2 = counts[top2[1]]
        if binom_test(count1, count1 + count2, p=0.5) > alpha:
            return Smooth.ABSTAIN
        else:
            return top2[0]

    def _sample_noise(self, x: torch.tensor, num: int, batch_size) -> np.ndarray:
        with torch.no_grad():
            counts = np.zeros(self.num_classes, dtype=int)
            from math import ceil
            for _ in range(ceil(num / batch_size)):
                this_batch_size = min(batch_size, num)
                num -= this_batch_size
                batch = x.repeat((this_batch_size, 1, 1, 1))
                noise = torch.randn_like(batch, device='cuda') * self.sigma
                predictions = self.base_classifier(batch + noise).argmax(1)
                counts += self._count_arr(predictions.cpu().numpy(), self.num_classes)
            return counts

    def _count_arr(self, arr: np.ndarray, length: int) -> np.ndarray:
        counts = np.zeros(length, dtype=int)
        for idx in arr:
            counts[idx] += 1
        return counts

    def _lower_confidence_bound(self, NA: int, N: int, alpha: float) -> float:
        return proportion_confint(NA, N, alpha=2 * alpha, method="beta")[0]

# File: code/predict.py (excerpt showing how multi-noise smoothing loss is used during attack)
import argparse, os
from architectures import get_architecture
from attacks import Attacker, PGD_L2, DDN 
from core import Smooth
from datasets import get_dataset, DATASETS, get_num_classes
import torch
from train_utils import requires_grad_

# [...] argparse definitions omitted for brevity

# Usage: construct attacker and perform attack using num_noise_vec Gaussian copies
checkpoint = torch.load(args.base_classifier)
base_classifier = get_architecture(checkpoint["arch"], args.dataset)
base_classifier.load_state_dict(checkpoint['state_dict'])
base_classifier.eval()
requires_grad_(base_classifier, False)
smoothed_classifier = Smooth(base_classifier, get_num_classes(args.dataset), args.sigma)

if args.attack == 'PGD':
    attacker = PGD_L2(steps=args.num_steps, device='cuda', max_norm=args.epsilon)
elif args.attack == 'DDN':
    attacker = DDN(steps=args.num_steps, device='cuda', max_norm=args.epsilon, 
                   init_norm=args.init_norm_DDN, gamma=args.gamma_DDN)

# Iterate dataset and craft adversarial examples with m = args.num_noise_vec noisy copies
(x, label) = dataset[i]
x = x.unsqueeze(0).cuda(); label = torch.Tensor([label]).cuda().long()
if args.attack in ['PGD', 'DDN']:
    x = x.repeat((args.num_noise_vec, 1, 1, 1))
    noise = (1 - int(args.base_attack))* torch.randn_like(x, device='cuda') * args.sigma
    x = attacker.attack(base_classifier, x, label, 
                        noise=noise, num_noise_vectors=args.num_noise_vec,
                        no_grad=args.no_grad_attack)
    x = x[:1]
# prediction under randomized smoothing
prediction = smoothed_classifier.predict(x.squeeze(), args.N, args.alpha, args.batch)
llm_extracted_info.experimental_info:
Scope of extracted implementation vs. described method
- Present: Untargeted optimization of perturbation delta via SGD (PGD_L2) or DDN, including a multi-noise smoothing loss that averages probabilities across Gaussian-perturbed copies of x+delta. This enforces consensus under Randomized Smoothing while crafting adversarial examples.
- Not present in repository: Perceptual penalties TV(Оҙ), per-channel mean shift penalty C(Оҙ), cross-channel dissimilarity penalty Dissim(Оҙ), a 1-channel shared-perturbation attack, or CROWN-IBP loss integration. Consequently, there is no О» weighting or penalty terms in the objective here.

How the implemented components map to the method
- Objective (implemented): maximize L(Оё, x+Оҙ) where L is either standard CE (single-noise) or smoothing-style NLL on the log of the mean softmax across m noisy copies. For multi-noise, the code computes
  L_smooth = NLL(log(mean_j softmax(f(x+Оҙ+Оө_j))))
  with Оө_j ~ N(0, Пғ^2 I), then ascends this loss w.r.t. Оҙ using SGD steps, projecting/clamping Оҙ to satisfy pixel range and optional L2 max norm.
- Untargeted mode: default targeted=False in attack(), so the multiplier is -1 and the optimization increases misclassification loss (untargeted).
- Optimizer: SGD on Оҙ with step size = (max_norm/steps)*2 for PGD_L2; DDN uses cosine LR schedule and decouples direction/norm, with optional max_norm.

Key experimental settings and hyperparameters (from predict.py and train_pgd.py)
- Dataset: cifar10 or imagenet (and imagenet32 for data handling); base models include ResNet-50 (ImageNet) and CIFAR ResNet-110.
- Randomized Smoothing parameters for predict/certify:
  вҖў sigma: Gaussian noise std added to inputs (float)
  вҖў N0 (selection samples) and N (estimation samples), alpha (failure prob) in certify; N and alpha in predict
  вҖў batch: batch size used for MC evaluation
- Attack parameters (predict-time and train-time):
  вҖў attack: 'PGD' or 'DDN'
  вҖў epsilon: L2 max-norm on Оҙ; in scripts given in 0вҖ“255 units and internally scaled by 256 (epsilon /= 256.0)
  вҖў num-steps: number of SGD/DDN steps
  вҖў num-noise-vec: m (number of noisy copies used during attack loss); for multi-noise smoothing loss
  вҖў base-attack: if set, attack runs without adding Gaussian noise during attack (noise = 0); otherwise, Gaussian noise with std sigma is added to each copy inside the attacker
  вҖў no-grad-attack: for PGD_L2 multi-noise, optional approximate gradient-free trick (see _attack_mutlinoise_no_grad)
  вҖў PGD step size is implicitly max_norm/steps*2; gradients normalized to unit L2 per sample each step; Оҙ is renormalized to L2 <= epsilon after each step
  вҖў DDN-specific: init-norm-DDN (init_norm, scaled by 256), gamma-DDN (norm growth/decay factor). If epsilon > 0 in predict.py, gamma is set to 1 - (3/510/epsilon)^(1/num_steps)
- Training-time (train_pgd.py):
  вҖў adv-training: when set, adversarial examples are generated on-the-fly via attacker and used for training (with Gaussian noise augmentation applied as well)
  вҖў warmup: over first warmup epochs, attacker.max_norm and init_norm are linearly increased from 0 to epsilon
  вҖў num-noise-vec (m_train): number of noisy copies used during attack at train time
  вҖў train-multi-noise: when set, the network is optimized with losses over all noisy copies; otherwise, only one sub-sampled copy per original is used in loss
  вҖў use-unlabelled and self-training-weight: optional semi-supervised self-training using a Tiny-Images derived dataset
  вҖў noise_sd: Gaussian augmentation added to inputs during training/eval
  вҖў Optimizer: SGD with momentum and weight decay; StepLR scheduler with lr_step_size and gamma; standard cross-entropy loss
- Certification (certify.py) settings: sigma, N0, N, alpha, batch, split, skip/max control scanning of dataset, outputs certified radius per sample.

Typical command patterns
- Predict-time adversarial evaluation with smoothing loss (multi-noise):
  python code/predict.py cifar10 path/to/checkpoint.pth.tar 0.25 data/predict --attack PGD --epsilon 255 --num-steps 10 --num-noise-vec 8 --N 100000 --alpha 0.001

- Adversarial training with multi-noise smoothing loss in the inner attack:
  python code/train_pgd.py cifar10 cifar_resnet110 out/exp --epochs 90 --batch 256 \
    --adv-training --attack PGD --epsilon 255 --num-steps 10 --warmup 1 \
    --num-noise-vec 4 --train-multi-noise --noise_sd 0.25

- Certification:
  python code/certify.py cifar10 path/to/checkpoint.pth.tar 0.25 data/certify/out.tsv --N0 100 --N 100000 --alpha 0.001 --batch 1000

Limitations relative to the described method
- No TV(Оҙ), per-channel mean penalty C(Оҙ), or cross-channel dissimilarity term Dissim(Оҙ) implemented.
- No 1-channel perturbation variant; Оҙ is full RGB.
- No CROWN-IBP loss integration; only standard training and adversarial training are provided.
- Loss across classes for вҖңoptimize over all incorrect labels and pick the bestвҖқ is not explicitly implemented; the attacks are untargeted and maximize misclassification without iterating wrong labels.

research_study_list.title: Second-Order Provable Defenses against Adversarial Attacks
research_study_list.abstract:
A robustness certificate is the minimum distance of a given input to the
decision boundary of the classifier (or its lower bound). For {\it any} input
perturbations with a magnitude smaller than the certificate value, the
classification output will provably remain unchanged. Exactly computing the
robustness certificates for neural networks is difficult since it requires
solving a non-convex optimization. In this paper, we provide
computationally-efficient robustness certificates for neural networks with
differentiable activation functions in two steps. First, we show that if the
eigenvalues of the Hessian of the network are bounded, we can compute a
robustness certificate in the $l_2$ norm efficiently using convex optimization.
Second, we derive a computationally-efficient differentiable upper bound on the
curvature of a deep network. We also use the curvature bound as a
regularization term during the training of the network to boost its certified
robustness. Putting these results together leads to our proposed {\bf
C}urvature-based {\bf R}obustness {\bf C}ertificate (CRC) and {\bf
C}urvature-based {\bf R}obust {\bf T}raining (CRT). Our numerical results show
that CRT leads to significantly higher certified robust accuracy compared to
interval-bound propagation (IBP) based training. We achieve certified robust
accuracy 69.79\%, 57.78\% and 53.19\% while IBP-based methods achieve 44.96\%,
44.74\% and 44.66\% on 2,3 and 4 layer networks respectively on the
MNIST-dataset.
research_study_list.full_text: Second-Order Provable Defenses against Adversarial Attacks Sahil Singla 1 Soheil Feizi 1 Abstract A robustness certiп¬Ғcate is the minimum distance of a given input to the decision boundary of the classiп¬Ғer (or its lower bound). For any in- put perturbations with a magnitude smaller than the certiп¬Ғcate value, the classiп¬Ғcation output will provably remain unchanged. Exactly com- puting the robustness certiп¬Ғcates for neural net- works is difп¬Ғcult since it requires solving a non- convex optimization. In this paper, we provide computationally-efп¬Ғcient robustness certiп¬Ғcates for neural networks with differentiable activation functions in two steps. First, we show that if the eigenvalues of the Hessian of the network are bounded, we can compute a robustness certiп¬Ғ- cate in the l2 norm efп¬Ғciently using convex opti- mization. Second, we derive a computationally- efп¬Ғcient differentiable upper bound on the curva- ture of a deep network. We also use the curvature bound as a regularization term during the train- ing of the network to boost its certiп¬Ғed robust- ness. Putting these results together leads to our proposed Curvature-based Robustness Certiп¬Ғcate (CRC) and Curvature-based Robust Training (CRT). Our numerical results show that CRT leads to signiп¬Ғcantly higher certiп¬Ғed robust ac- curacy compared to interval-bound propagation (IBP) based training. We achieve certiп¬Ғed ro- bust accuracy 69.79%, 57.78% and 53.19% while IBP-based methods achieve 44.96%, 44.74% and 44.66% on 2,3 and 4 layer networks respectively on the MNIST-dataset. 1. Introduction Modern neural networks achieve high accuracy on tasks such as image classiп¬Ғcation and speech recognition, but are 1Department of Computer Science, University of Maryland, College Park. Correspondence to: Sahil Singla <ssingla@cs.umd.edu>, Soheil Feizi <sfeizi@cs.umd.edu>. Proceedings of the 37 th International Conference on Machine Learning, Vienna, Austria, PMLR 108, 2020. Copyright 2020 by the author(s). known to be brittle to small, adversarially chosen pertur- bations of their inputs (Szegedy et al., 2014). A classiп¬Ғer which correctly classiп¬Ғes an image x, can be fooled by an adversary to misclassify an adversarial example x +Оҙ, such that x +Оҙis indistinguishable from x to a human. Adversar- ial examples can also fool systems when they are printed out on a paper and photographed with a smart phone (Kurakin et al., 2016a). Even in a black box threat model, where the adversary has no access to the model parameters, attackers could target autonomous vehicles by using stickers or paint to create an adversarial stop sign that the vehicle would interpret as a yield or another sign (Papernot et al., 2016). This trend is worrisome and suggests that adversarial vulner- abilities need to be appropriately addressed before neural networks can be deployed in security critical applications. In this work, we propose a new approach for developing provable defenses against в„“2-bounded adversarial attacks as well as computing robustness certiп¬Ғcations of pre-trained deep networks with differentiable activations. In contrast to the existing certiп¬Ғcates (Weng et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2018b) that use the п¬Ғrst-order information (upper and lower bounds on the slope), our approach is based on the second- order information (upper and lower bounds on curvature val- ues i.e. eigenvalues of the Hessian). Our approach is based on two key theoretically-justiп¬Ғed steps: First, in Theorems 1 and 2, we show that if the eigenvalues of the Hessian of the network (curvatures of the network) are bounded (globally or locally), we can efп¬Ғciently compute a robustness cer- tiп¬Ғcate and develop a defense method against в„“2-bounded adversarial attacks using convex optimization. Second, in Theorem 4, we derive a computationally-efп¬Ғcient differen- tiable bound on the curvature (eigenvalues of the Hessian) of a deep network. We derive this bound by explicitly char- acterizing the Hessian of a deep network in Lemma 1. Although the problem of п¬Ғnding the closest adversarial ex- ample to a given point for deep nets leads to a non-convex optimization problem, our proposed Curvature-based Ro- bustness Certiп¬Ғcate (CRC), under some veriп¬Ғable condi- tions, is able to compute points on the decision boundary that are provably closest to the input. That is, it provides the tightest certiп¬Ғcate in those cases. For example, for a 2,3,4 layer networks trained on MNIST, we can п¬Ғnd prov- ably closest adversarial points for 44.17%, 22.59%, 19.53% cases, respectively (Table 2). To the best of our knowledge, arXiv:2006.00731v1  [cs.LG]  1 Jun 2020Second-Order Provable Defenses against Adversarial Attacks our method is the п¬Ғrst approach that can efп¬Ғciently com- pute provably closest adversarial examples for a signiп¬Ғcant fraction of examples in non-trivial neural networks. We note that un-regularized networks, specially deep ones, can obtain large curvature bounds which can lead to small robustness certiп¬Ғcates. However, by using the derived curva- ture bound as a regularizer during training, we signiп¬Ғcantly decrease curvature values of the network, with little or no decrease in its performance (Table 5, Figure 1). Using this technique, our method signiп¬Ғcantly outperforms interval- bound propagation (IBP) (Wong et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2019a) and achieves state of the art certiп¬Ғed accuracy (Ta- bles 3 and 4). In particular, our method achieves certiп¬Ғed robust accuracy 69.79%, 57.78% and 53.19% while IBP- based methods achieve 44.96%, 44.74% and 44.66% on 2,3 and 4 layer networks, respectively, on the MNIST-dataset (similar results for Fashion-MNIST). Other recent works (e.g. Moosavi Dezfooli et al. (2019); Qin et al. (2019)) empirically show that using an estimate of curvature at inputs as a regularizer leads to empirical robustness on par with the adversarial training. In this work, however, we use a bound on the absolute value of curvature (and not an estimate) as a regularizer and show that it results in high certiп¬Ғed robustness. Moreover, previous works have tried to certify robustness by bounding the Lipschitz constant of the neural network (Anil et al., 2018; Hein & Andriushchenko, 2017; Peck et al., 2017; Szegedy et al., 2014; Zhang et al., 2018c). Our approach, however, is based on bounding the Lipschitz constant of the gradient which in turn leads to bound on the eigenvalues of the Hessian of deep neural networks. In summary, we make the following contributions: вҖў We derive a closed-form expression for the Hessian of a deep network with differentiable activation functions (Lemma 1) and derive bounds on the curvature using this closed-form formula (Theorems 3 and 4). вҖў We develop computationally efп¬Ғcient methods for both the robustness certiп¬Ғcation as well as the adversarial attack problems (Theorems 1 and 2). вҖў We provide veriп¬Ғable conditions under which our method is able to compute points on the decision boundary that are provably closest to the input. Empiri- cally, we show that this condition holds for a signiп¬Ғcant fraction of examples (Table 2). вҖў We show that using our proposed curvature bounds as a regularizer during training leads to improved cer- tiп¬Ғed accuracy on 2,3 and 4 layer networks (on the MNIST and Fashion-MNIST datasets) compared to IBP-based adversarial training (Wong & Kolter, 2017; Zhang et al., 2019a) (Tables 3 and 4). Our robustness certiп¬Ғcate (CRC) outperforms CROWNвҖҷs certiп¬Ғcate (Zhang et al., 2018b) signiп¬Ғcantly when trained with our regularizer (Table 5). To the best of our knowledge, this is the п¬Ғrst work that (a) demonstrates the utility of second-order information for provable robustness, (b) derives a framework to п¬Ғnd the exact robustness certiп¬Ғcates in the l2 norm and the exact worst case adversarial perturbation in an l2 ball of given a radius under some conditions, and (c) derives an exact closed form expression for the Hessian and bounds on the curvature values using the same. 2. Related work In the last couple of years, several empirical defenses have been proposed for training classiп¬Ғers to be robust against ad- versarial perturbations (Kurakin et al., 2016b; Madry et al., 2018; Miyato et al., 2017; Papernot et al., 2016; Saman- gouei et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2019b; Zheng et al., 2016) Although these defenses robustify classiп¬Ғers to particular types of attacks, they can be still vulnerable against stronger attacks (Athalye & Carlini, 2018; Athalye et al., 2018; Car- lini & Wagner, 2017; Uesato et al., 2018). For example, (Athalye et al., 2018) showed most of the empirical de- fenses proposed in ICLR 2018 can be broken by developing tailored attacks for each of them. To end the cycle between defenses and attacks, a line of work on certiп¬Ғed defenses has gained attention where the goal is to train classiп¬Ғers whose predictions are provably robust within some given region (Bunel et al., 2017; Carlini et al., 2017; Cheng et al., 2017; Croce et al., 2018; Dutta et al., 2018; Dvijotham et al., 2018a;b; Ehlers, 2017; Fischetti & Jo, 2018; Gehr et al., 2018; Gowal et al., 2018; Huang et al., 2016; Katz et al., 2017; Lomuscio & Maganti, 2017; Mirman et al., 2018; Raghunathan et al., 2018a;b; Singh et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2018a;b; Weng et al., 2018; Wong & Kolter, 2017; Wong et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2018b; 2019a). These methods, however, do not scale to large and practical networks used in solving modern machine learning problems. Another line of defense work focuses on random- ized smoothing where the prediction is robust within some region around the input with a user-chosen probability (Cao & Gong, 2017; Cohen et al., 2019; LВҙecuyer et al., 2018; Li et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2017; Salman et al., 2019). Although these methods can scale to large networks, certifying robust- ness with probability close to 1 often requires generating a large number of noisy samples around the input which leads to high inference-time computational complexity. We discuss existing works in more details in Appendix A.Second-Order Provable Defenses against Adversarial Attacks Table 1.A summary of various primal and dual concepts used in the paper.fdenotes the function of the decision boundary, i.e.z(L) y вҲ’z(L) t where yis the true label and tis the attack target. mand M are lower and upper bounds on the smallest and largest eigenvalues of the Hessian of f, respectively. Certiп¬Ғcate problem (вҲ’) = cert Attack problem (вҲ’) = attack primal problem, pвҲ— (вҲ’) minf(x)=0 1/slash.left2/parallel.alt1x вҲ’x(0)/parallel.alt12 min/parallel.alt1xвҲ’x(0)/parallel.alt1вүӨПҒf(x) dual function, d(вҲ’)(О·) minx 1/slash.left2/parallel.alt1x вҲ’x(0)/parallel.alt12 +О·f(x) minx f(x) +О·/slash.left2(/parallel.alt1x вҲ’x(0)/parallel.alt12 вҲ’ПҒ2) When is dual solvable? вҲ’1/slash.leftM вүӨО·вүӨвҲ’1/slash.leftm вҲ’mвүӨО· dual problem, dвҲ— (вҲ’) maxвҲ’1/slash.leftMвүӨО·вүӨвҲ’1/slash.leftmdcert(О·) maxвҲ’mвүӨО·dattack(О·) When primal =dual? f(x(cert)) =0 /parallel.alt1x(attack) вҲ’x(0)/parallel.alt1=ПҒ 3. Notation Consider a fully connected neural network with Llayers and NI neurons in the Ith layer ( L вүҘ 2 and I вҲҲ [L]) for a multi-label classiп¬Ғcation problem with C classes (NL = C). The corresponding function of the neural net- work is z(L) вҲ¶RD вҶ’ RC where D is the dimension of the input. For an input x, we use z(I)(x) вҲҲ RNI and a(I)(x) вҲҲ RNI to denote the input ( before applying the activation function) and output (after applying the activa- tion function) of neurons in the Ith hidden layer of the network, respectively. To simplify notation and when no confusion arises, we make the dependency of z(I) and a(I) to x implicit. We deп¬Ғne a(0)(x) =x and N0 =D. With a fully connected architecture, each z(I) and a(I) is computed using a transformation matrix W(I) вҲҲRNIГ—NIвҲ’1 , the bias vector b(I) вҲҲRNI and an activation function Пғ(.) as follows: z(I) =W(I)a(IвҲ’1) +b(I), a(I) =Пғ/parenleft.alt1z(I)/parenright.alt1 We use (z(L) i вҲ’z(L) j )(x) as a shorthand for z(L) i (x) вҲ’ z(L) j (x). We use [p]to denote the set {1,...,p }and [p,q], pвүӨqto denote the set {p,p +1,...,q }. We use small letters i,j,k etc to denote the index over a vector or rows of a matrix and capital letters I,J to denote the index over layers of network. The element in the ith position of a vector v is given by vi, the vector in the ith row of a matrix A is Ai while the element in the ith row and jth column of A is Ai,j. We use /parallel.alt1v/parallel.alt1and /parallel.alt1A/parallel.alt1to denote the 2-norm and the operator 2-norm of the vector v and the matrix A, respectively. We use /divides.alt0v/divides.alt0and /divides.alt0A/divides.alt0to denote the vector and matrix constructed by taking the elementwise absolute values. We use О»max(A) and О»min(A) to denote the largest and smallest eigenvalues of a symmetric matrix A. We use diag(v) to denote the diagonal matrix constructed by placing each element of v along the diagonal. We use вҠҷ to denote the Hadamard Product, I to denote the identity matrix. We use /uni227Cand /uni227Dto denote Linear Matrix Inequalities (LMIs) such that given two symmetric matrices A and B where A /uni227DB means A вҲ’B Positive Semi-Deп¬Ғnite (PSD). 4. Using duality to solve the attack and certiп¬Ғcate problems Consider an input x(0) with true label yand attack target t. In the certiп¬Ғcate problem, our goal is to п¬Ғnd a lower bound of minimum l2 distance between x(0) and decision boundary f(x) =0 where f(x) =(z(L) y вҲ’z(L) t )(x). Next, we consider the attack problem. The goal here is to п¬Ғnd an adversarial example inside an l2 ball of radius ПҒsuch that f(x) is minimized. We note that both Theorems 1 and 2 hold for any non-convex function with continuous gradients. Thus they can also be of interest in problems such as optimization of neural nets. Using Theorems 1 and 2, we have the following deп¬Ғnitions for certiп¬Ғcation and attack optimizations: Deп¬Ғnition 1. (Curvature-based Certiп¬Ғcate Optimization ) Given an input x(0) with true label y, false target t, we deп¬Ғne (О·(cert),x(cert)) as the solution of the following max- min optimization: max вҲ’1/slash.leftMвүӨО·вүӨвҲ’1/slash.leftm min x /bracketleft.alt31 2 /parallel.alt2x вҲ’x(0)/parallel.alt2 2 +О·f(x)/bracketright.alt3 We refer to /parallel.alt2x(cert) вҲ’x(0)/parallel.alt2as the Curvature-based Robustness Certiп¬Ғcate (CRC). Deп¬Ғnition 2. (Curvature-based Attack Optimization ) Given input x(0) with label y, false target t, and the l2 ball radius ПҒ, we deп¬Ғne (О·(attack),x(attack)) as the solution of the following optimization: max О·вүҘвҲ’m min x /bracketleft.alt3О· 2 /parenleft.alt3/parallel.alt2x вҲ’x(0)/parallel.alt2 2 вҲ’ПҒ2/parenright.alt3+f(x)/bracketright.alt3 When x(attack) is used for training in an adversarial train- ing framework, we call the method the Curvature-based Robust Training (CRT). A direct implication of Theorems 1 and 2 is that the tight- ness of our robustness certiп¬Ғcate crucially depends on the tightness of our curvature bounds, mand M. If mand M are very large compared to the true eigenvalue bounds of the Hessian of the network, the resulting robustness certiп¬Ғ- cate will be vacuous. In Table 5 (and Figure 1), we show that by adding the derived bound as a regularization term during the training, we can signiп¬Ғcantly decrease curvature bounds of the network, with little or no decrease in its perfor- mance. This leads to high robustness certiп¬Ғcations against adversarial attacks. 5. Adversarial training with curvature regularization Since the term B(I) in Lemma 1 is the Jacobian ofz(I) with respect to x, /parallel.alt1B(I)/parallel.alt1, it is equal to the lipschitz constant of the neural network constructed from the п¬Ғrst I layers of the original network. Finding tight bounds on the lipschitz constant is an active area of research (Fazlyab et al., 2019; Scaman & Virmaux, 2018; Weng et al., 2018) and the prod- uct of the operator norm of weight matrices is known to be a loose bound on the lipschitz constant for deep networks. Since we use the same product to compute the bound for /parallel.alt1B(I)/parallel.alt1in Theorem 4, the resulting curvature bound is likely to be loose for very deep networks. In Figure 1, we observe the same trend: as the depth of the network increases, the upper bound Kub computed us- ing Theorem 4 becomes signiп¬Ғcantly larger than the lowerSecond-Order Provable Defenses against Adversarial Attacks (a) 2 layer network  (b) 3 layer network Figure 1.Illustration of lower (Klb) and upper (Kub) bounds on the curvature of 2 and 3 layer networks with sigmoid activations trained on MNIST. Similarly with Оі = 0, networks adversarially trained with PGD have high curvature as well (note the log scale of the y-axis). However, using our curvature bound as a regularizer, the bound becomes tight and CRC gives high certiп¬Ғcate values (Table 5). However, by regularizing the network to have small curvature during training, the bound becomes signiп¬Ғcantly tighter. Interestingly, using curvature regularization, even with this loose curvature bound for deep nets, we achieve signiп¬Ғcantly higher robust accuracy than the current state of the art methods while enjoying signiп¬Ғcantly higher standard accuracy as well (see Tables 3 and 4). To regularize the network to have small curvature values, we penalize the curvature boundKduring training. However, since verifying the robustness property in an l2 ball around the input is known to be an NP-complete problem for ReLU networks (Katz et al., 2017), it is compu- tationally infeasible to even verify that a given adversarial perturbation is the worst case perturbation in polynomial time unless P=NP. We however show that it is possible to п¬Ғnd (and not just verify) the exact worst case perturbation (and robustness certiп¬Ғcate) for neural networks with smooth activation functions. We believe that these theoretical and empirical results provide evidence that bounding the curva- ture of the network and using smooth activation functions can be critical to achieve high robustness guarantees. 7.2. Results using local curvature bounds From Theorems 1 and 2, we can observe that if the curva- ture is locally bounded within a convex region around the input (we call it the вҖқsafeвҖқ region), then the corresponding dual problems (dвҲ— cert, dвҲ— attack) are again convex optimiza- tion problems provided the optimization trajectory does not escape the safe region. Theorem 3 can be directly extended to compute the local curvature bound using bounds on the second derivatives, i.e. Пғ вҖІвҖІ (z(1)) in the local region. In Table 6, we show signiп¬Ғcant improvements for the CRC certiп¬Ғcate for two-layer sigmoid networks on the MNIST dataset for Оі=0. However, with the curvature regularization, the difference is insigniп¬Ғcant. We also observe that the certiп¬Ғed accuracy for (CRT, 0.0) improves from 95.04% to 95.31% and for standard improves from 54.17% to 58.06%. The certiп¬Ғed accuracy remains the same for other cases. Implementation details are in the Appendix Section C.6. Computing local curvature bounds for deeper networks, however, is more challenging due to the presence of terms involving multiplication of п¬Ғrst and second derivatives. A straightforward extension of Theorem 4 4, wherein we com- pute the upper bound on Пғ вҖІ and ПғвҖІвҖІin a local region around the input across all neurons in all layers does not yield sig- niп¬Ғcant improvements over the global method, therefore we do not include those results in our comparison. Conclusion In this paper, we develop computationally-efп¬Ғcient convex relaxations for robustness certiп¬Ғcation and adversarial at- tack problems given the classiп¬Ғer has a bounded curvature.Second-Order Provable Defenses against Adversarial Attacks We also show that this convex relaxation is tight under some general veriп¬Ғable conditions. To be able to use proposed certiп¬Ғcation and attack convex optimizations, we derive global curvature bounds for deep networks with differen- tiable activation functions. This result is a consequence of a closed-form expression that we derive for the Hessian of a deep network. Adversarial training using our attack method coupled with curvature regularization results in a signif- icantly higher certiп¬Ғed robust accuracy than the existing provable defense methods. Our proposed curvature-based robustness certiп¬Ғcate signiп¬Ғcantly outperforms the CROWN certiп¬Ғcate when trained with our regularizer. Scaling up our proposed curvature-based robustness certiп¬Ғcation and training methods as well as further tightening the derived curvature bounds are among interesting directions for the future work. Appendix A. Related work Many defenses have been proposed to make neural networks robust against adversarial examples. These methods can be classiп¬Ғed into empirical defenses which empirically seem to be robust against known adversarial attacks, and certiп¬Ғed defenses, which are provably robust against such attacks. Empirical defenses The best known empirical defense is adversarial training (Kurakin et al., 2016b; Madry et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2019b). In this method, a neural network is trained to minimize the worst-case loss over a region around the input. Although such defenses seem to work on existing attacks, there is no guarantee that a more powerful attack would not break them. In fact, most such defenses proposed in the literature were later broken by stronger at- tacks (Athalye & Carlini, 2018; Athalye et al., 2018; Carlini & Wagner, 2017; Uesato et al., 2018). To end this arms race between defenses and attacks, a number of works have tried to focus on certiп¬Ғed defenses that have formal robustness guarantees. Certiп¬Ғed defenses A classiп¬Ғer is said to be certiп¬Ғably ro- bust if one can easily obtain a guarantee that a classiп¬ҒerвҖҷs prediction remains constant within some region around the input. Such defenses typically rely on certiп¬Ғcation methods which are either exact or conservative. Exact methods report whether or not there exists a adversarial perturbation inside some lp norm ball. In contrast, conservative methods either certify that no adversarial perturbation exists or decline to make a certiп¬Ғcation; they may decline even when no such perturbation exists. Exact methods are usually based on Satisп¬Ғability Modulo Theories (Carlini et al., 2017; Ehlers, 2017; Huang et al., 2016; Katz et al., 2017) and Mixed In- teger linear programming (Bunel et al., 2017; Cheng et al., 2017; Dutta et al., 2018; Fischetti & Jo, 2018; Lomuscio & Maganti, 2017). Unfortunately, they are computation- ally inefп¬Ғcient and difп¬Ғcult to scale up to even moderately sized neural networks. In contrast, conservative methods are more scalable and efп¬Ғcient which makes them useful for building certiп¬Ғed defenses (Croce et al., 2018; Dvijotham et al., 2018a;b; Gehr et al., 2018; Gowal et al., 2018; Mir- man et al., 2018; Raghunathan et al., 2018a;b; Singh et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2018a;b; Weng et al., 2018; Wong & Kolter, 2017; Wong et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2018b). How- ever, even these methods have not been shown to scale to practical networks that are large and expressive enough to perform well on ImageNet, for example. To scale to such large networks, randomized smoothing has been proposed as a probabilistically certiп¬Ғed defense. Randomized smoothing Randomized smoothing was pre- viously proposed by several works (Cao & Gong, 2017; Liu et al., 2017) as a empirical defense without any formal guar- antees. (LВҙecuyer et al., 2018) п¬Ғrst proved robustness guaran- tees for randomized smoothing classiп¬Ғer using inequalities from differential privacy. (Li et al., 2018) improved upon the same using tools from information theory. Recently, (Cohen et al., 2019) provided a even tighter robustness guarantee for randomized smoothing. (Salman et al., 2019) proposed a method of adversarial training for the randomized smooth- ing classiп¬Ғer giving state of the art results in the l2 norm metric. B.
