The routine to compute the attack example is given in Algorithm 2. Algorithm 2 Attack optimization Require: input x(0), label y, target t, radius ПҒ m,M,K вҶҗcompute bounds(z(L) y вҲ’z(L) t ) О·min вҶҗвҲ’m О·max вҶҗ20(1 вҲ’m) О·вҶҗ1/slash.left2(О·min+О·max) x вҶҗx(0) for iin [1,..., 30]do for jin [1,..., 20]do g вҶҗcompute gradient(z(L) y вҲ’z(L) t ,x) if /parallel.alt1g +О·(x вҲ’x(0))/parallel.alt1<10вҲ’5 then break end if x вҶҗвҲ’(K+О·)вҲ’1 /parenleft.alt1g вҲ’Kx вҲ’О·x(0)/parenright.alt1 end for if /parallel.alt1x вҲ’x(0)/parallel.alt1<ПҒthen О·max вҶҗО· else О·min вҶҗО· end if О·вҶҗ(О·min+О·max)/slash.left2 end for return x C.6. Computing certiп¬Ғcate using local curvature bounds To compute the robustness certiп¬Ғcate in a local region around the input, we п¬Ғrst compute the certiп¬Ғcate using the global bounds on the curvature. Using the same certiп¬Ғcate as the initial l2 radius of the safe region, we can reп¬Ғne our certiп¬Ғcate. Due to the reduction in curvature, this will surely increase the value of the certiп¬Ғcate. We then use the new robustness certiп¬Ғcate as the new l2 radius of the safe region and repeat. We iterate over this process 5 times to compute the local version of our robustness certiп¬Ғcate. To ensure that the optimization trajectory does not escape the safe region, whenever the gradient descent step lies outside the вҖқsafeвҖқ region, we reduce the step size by a factor of two until it lies inside the region. Computing g,h, hU and hL for different activation functions F.1. Softplus activation For softplus activation, we have the following. We useS(x) to denote sigmoid: Пғ(x) =log(1 +exp(x)) Пғ вҖІ (x) =S(x) Пғ вҖІвҖІ (x) =S(x)(1 вҲ’S(x))Second-Order Provable Defenses against Adversarial Attacks To bound S(x)(1 вҲ’S(x)), let Оұdenote S(x). We know that 0 вүӨОұвүӨ1: Оұ(1 вҲ’Оұ) = 1 4 вҲ’/parenleft.alt41 2 вҲ’Оұ/parenright.alt4 2 Thus, S(x)(1 вҲ’S(x)) is maximum at S(x) = 1/slash.left2 and minimum at S(x) =0 and S(x) =1. The maximum value is 0.25 and minimum value is 0. 0 вүӨS(x)(1 вҲ’S(x)) вүӨ0.25 /LeftrightlineвҮ’0 вүӨПғ вҖІвҖІ (x) вүӨ0.25 Thus, hU = 0.25, hL = 0 (for use in Theorem 3) and g = 1, h=0.25 (for use in Theorem 4). F.2. Sigmoid activation For sigmoid activation, we have the following. We useS(x) to denote sigmoid: Пғ(x) =S(x) = 1 1 +exp(вҲ’x) Пғ вҖІ (x) =S(x)(1 вҲ’S(x)) Пғ вҖІвҖІ (x) =S(x)(1 вҲ’S(x))(1 вҲ’2S(x)) The second derivative of sigmoid (Пғ вҖІвҖІ (x)) can be bounded using standard differentiation. Let Оұ denote S(x). We know that 0 вүӨОұвүӨ1: hL вүӨПғ вҖІвҖІ (x) вүӨhU hL = min 0вүӨОұвүӨ1 Оұ(1 вҲ’Оұ)(1 вҲ’2Оұ) hU = max 0вүӨОұвүӨ1 Оұ(1 вҲ’Оұ)(1 вҲ’2Оұ) To solve for both hL and hU, we п¬Ғrst differentiate Оұ(1 вҲ’ Оұ)(1 вҲ’2Оұ) with respect to Оұ: вҲҮОұ(Оұ(1 вҲ’Оұ)(1 вҲ’2Оұ)) =вҲҮОұ/parenleft.alt12Оұ3 вҲ’3Оұ2 +Оұ/parenright.alt1 =/parenleft.alt16Оұ2 вҲ’6Оұ+1/parenright.alt1 Solving for 6Оұ2 вҲ’6Оұ+1 =0, we get the solutions: Оұ=/parenleft.alt43 + вҲҡ 3 6 /parenright.alt4,/parenleft.alt43 вҲ’ вҲҡ 3 6 /parenright.alt4 Since both (3 + вҲҡ 3/slash.left6),(3 вҲ’ вҲҡ 3/slash.left6) lie between 0 and 1, we check for the second derivatives: вҲҮ2 Оұ(Оұ(1 вҲ’Оұ)(1 вҲ’2Оұ)) =вҲҮОұ/parenleft.alt16Оұ2 вҲ’6Оұ+1/parenright.alt1 =12ОұвҲ’6 =6(2ОұвҲ’1) At Оұ=(3 + вҲҡ 3)/slash.left6, вҲҮ2 Оұ =6(2ОұвҲ’1) =2 вҲҡ 3 > 0. At Оұ=(3 вҲ’ вҲҡ 3)/slash.left6, вҲҮ2 Оұ =6(2ОұвҲ’1) =вҲ’2 вҲҡ 3 <0. Thus Оұ=(3 + вҲҡ 3)/slash.left6 is a local minima, Оұ=(3 вҲ’ вҲҡ 3)/slash.left6 is a local maxima. Substituting the two critical points into Оұ(1 вҲ’Оұ)(1 вҲ’2Оұ), we get hU =9.623 Г—10вҲ’2, hL =вҲ’9.623 Г—10вҲ’2. Thus, hU = 9.623 Г—10вҲ’2, hL = вҲ’9.623 Г—10вҲ’2 (for use in Theorem 3) and g=0.25, h=0.09623 (for use in Theorem 4). F.3. Additional experiments Empirical accuracy means the fraction of test samples that were correctly classiп¬Ғed after running a PGD attack (Madry et al., 2018) with an l2 bound on the adversarial perturba- tions. Certiп¬Ғed accuracy means the fraction of test samples that were classiп¬Ғed correctly initially and had the robust- ness certiп¬Ғcate greater than a pre-speciп¬Ғed attack radius ПҒ. Unless otherwise speciп¬Ғed, for both empirical and certiп¬Ғed accuracy, we use ПҒ= 0.5. Unless otherwise speciп¬Ғed, we use the class with the second largest logit as the attack target for the given input (i.e. the class t). Unless speciп¬Ғed, the experiments were run on the MNIST dataset while noting that our results are scalable for more complex datasets. The notation (LГ—[1024], activation) denotes a neural network with Llayers with the speciп¬Ғed activation function, (Оі=c) denotes standard training with Оіset to c, (CRT,c) denotes CRT training with Оі=c. Certiп¬Ғcates CROWN and CRC are computed over 150 correctly classiп¬Ғed images. H.1. Computing Klb and Kub First, note that Kdoes not depend on the input, but on net- work weights W(I), label yand target t. Different images may still have different Kbecause label yand target tmay be different. To compute Klb in the table, п¬Ғrst for each pair yand t, we п¬Ғnd the largest eigenvalue of the Hessian of all test images that have label yand second largest logit of classt. Then we take the max of the largest eigenvalue across all test images. This gives a rough estimate of the largest curvature in the vicinity of test images with label y and target t. We can directly take the mean across all such pairs to compute Klb. However, we п¬Ғnd that some pairs yand twere infrequent (with barely 1,2 test images in them). Thus, for all such pairs we cannot get a good estimate of the largest curvature in vicinity. We select all pairs yand tthat have at least 100 images in them and compute Klb by taking the mean across all such pairs. To compute Kub in the table, we compute Kfor all pairs y and tthat have at least 100 images, i.e at least 100 images should have label yand target t. And then we compute the mean across all Kthat satisfy this condition. This was done to do a fair comparison with Klb. Figure 2 shows a plot of the Kub and Klb with increasing Оі for a sigmoid network (with 4 layers).Second-Order Provable Defenses against Adversarial Attacks Figure 2.Effect of Оіon Kub and Klb for a 4 layer network. llm_extracted_info.main_contributions: Introduces second-order, curvature-based provable defenses for neural networks with differentiable activations. The paper defines Curvature-based Robustness Certificate (CRC) and Curvature-based Robust Training (CRT). Key contributions: (1) dual convex formulations for both certification (minimum l2 distance to the decision boundary) and attack problems when Hessian eigenvalues are bounded (Theorems 1вҖ“2), with verifiable optimality conditions yielding exact certificates/adversarial examples; (2) a closed-form expression for the network Hessian (Lemma 1) and computable global curvature boundsвҖ”tight LMI bounds for 2-layer nets (Theorem 3) and efficient global bounds for deep nets via operator norms (Theorem 4); (3) a curvature regularizer that tightens bounds and boosts certified robustness; (4) numerically, significant gains over IBP-based training on MNIST and Fashion-MNIST, and ability to compute provably closest adversarial examples for a notable fraction of inputs.
llm_extracted_info.methodology:
- Problem setup: For input x(0), define f(x) = z_y(x) - z_t(x). Certification seeks min ||x-x(0)||_2 s.t. f(x)=0; attack seeks min f(x) s.t. ||x-x(0)||_2 вүӨ ПҒ.
- Dual convex programs (Theorems 1вҖ“2): With global Hessian eigenvalue bounds mI вӘҜ вҲҮ^2 f вӘҜ MI, the dual objectives d_cert(О·) = min_x [0.5||x-x(0)||^2 + О· f(x)] are convex for О· вҲҲ [вҲ’1/M, вҲ’1/m]; d_attack(О·) = min_x [f(x) + (О·/2)(||x-x(0)||^2 вҲ’ ПҒ^2)] are convex for О· вүҘ вҲ’m. Under conditions f(x_cert)=0 (cert) or ||x_attackвҲ’x(0)||=ПҒ (attack), primal=dual, giving exact solutions.
- Algorithms: MajorizationвҖ“minimization updates with global bound K on |О»(вҲҮ^2 f)|; bisection over О·. Update rules derived for both certification and attack; linear convergence; implementation details provided.
- Curvature bounds: Closed-form Hessian (Lemma 1) via B(I) (input Jacobians) and F(L,I) (layer-to-output Jacobians). For 2-layer nets with bounded Пғ'' in [h_L, h_U], construct PSD/NSD matrices P,N yielding LMIs N вӘҜ вҲҮ^2 f вӘҜ P and eigenvalue bounds (Theorem 3). For deep nets, bound |О»(вҲҮ^2 z_i)| вүӨ h ОЈ_{I=1}^{LвҲ’1} r(I)^2 max_j S(L,I)_{i,j}, where r(I), S(L,I) are recursively defined using operator norms of W(I) and |Пғ'| (Theorem 4). Local curvature bounds also considered; for ReLU 2-layer, derive quadratic lower bounds and solve via S-procedure.
- Training (CRT): Minimize cross-entropy + Оі K(W,y,t), where K is the curvature bound for the current (y,t). Gradients through spectral norms computed with power iteration and cached singular vectors.
- Additional: Shows randomized smoothing implies bounded curvature (appendix); provides practical pseudocode and implementation details (e.g., GPU, stopping criteria).
llm_extracted_info.experimental_setup:
- Datasets: MNIST and Fashion-MNIST (primary), with supplementary CIFAR-10 results (appendix). 
- Architectures: Fully connected networks with 2, 3, or 4 hidden layers, width 1024 (notation LГ—[1024]); activations: sigmoid, tanh, softplus (required differentiability), with ReLU included only for IBP baselines and a 2-layer ReLU certificate via quadratic bounds.
- Evaluation metrics: Standard accuracy; empirical robust accuracy (PGD l2-bounded attacks); certified robust accuracy (fraction of correctly classified samples with CRC > ПҒ). ПҒ вҲҲ {0.5, 1.58}. Certificates computed on 150 randomly selected correctly classified images. Default target t is runner-up class; also report random and least-confidence targets (appendix). Attack success and certificate success rates reported as fraction where primal=dual holds.
- Baselines: COAP (Wong & Kolter 2017), CROWN-IBP (Zhang et al. 2019a), CROWN-general certificates, Randomized Smoothing (Cohen et al. 2019), PGD and TRADES (for empirical defenses). Hyperparameters for CROWN-IBP tuned over final beta вҲҲ [0.5, 3]; smoothing Пғ вҲҲ {0.25, 0.5, 1.0}. 
- Training: Compare standard training with curvature regularization (Оі) and CRT (adversarial training with curvature regularization). Оі varied; best-performing values include ОівүҲ0.01 for many settings. Hardware: single NVIDIA GeForce RTX 2080 Ti.
- Key results: On MNIST at ПҒ=1.58 with softplus, CRC-certified robust accuracy: 2/3/4-layer nets achieve 69.79%/57.78%/53.19% vs IBP 44.96%/44.74%/44.66%. Fashion-MNIST at ПҒ=1.58: 54.39%/53.4%/49.6% vs ~48вҖ“49%. Provably closest adversarial examples found for 44.17% (2-layer), 22.59% (3-layer), 19.53% (4-layer) with curvature regularization; attack success (primal=dual) reaches 100% with regularization. CRC outperforms CROWN certificates, especially after curvature regularization. Local curvature bounds further improve 2-layer CRC.
llm_extracted_info.limitations:
- Requires differentiable activation functions for Hessian-based bounds; core method not directly applicable to standard ReLU deep networks (only a special 2-layer ReLU variant via quadratic bounds is provided).
- Global curvature bounds for deep networks (Theorem 4) rely on products of operator norms and can be loose, especially as depth increases; without curvature regularization, bounds may be vacuous, yielding weak certificates.
- Demonstrations are on small image datasets (MNIST, Fashion-MNIST) with fully connected architectures; limited CIFAR-10 results and no scaling to large modern architectures (e.g., CNNs on ImageNet).
- Certification and attack exactness (primal=dual) depend on verifiable conditions (boundary or norm constraints) that may not hold for all inputs; fraction decreases with depth without regularization.
- Focuses on l2 norm; extensions to l1/lвҲһ not developed here.
- Local curvature bounds for deep networks provide limited gains due to complexity of mixed derivative terms; approach may involve additional computational overhead (e.g., spectral norm computation via power iteration).
llm_extracted_info.future_research_directions:
- Develop tighter, data-dependent or layerwise curvature bounds for deep networks (e.g., beyond products of operator norms; improved Lipschitz/Jacobian bounding) to reduce conservatism and scale to deeper architectures.
- Extend CRC/CRT to convolutional and residual architectures and to large-scale datasets (CIFAR-10+, ImageNet), including efficient GPU implementations and batching for certification.
- Explore norm-generalization (lвҲһ, l1, hybrid norms) and threat models (untargeted/multi-target) within the duality-based framework.
- Improve local curvature estimation and safe-region tracking for deeper networks (e.g., adaptive region refinement, per-input curvature bounds).
- Combine curvature-based certification with randomized smoothing or other certified defenses for complementary robustness and scalability.
- Analyze and enhance the conditions ensuring primalвҖ“dual tightness; design training procedures that explicitly promote these conditions.
- Investigate curvature-aware architectures or activation functions tailored for certifiable robustness and efficient second-order control.

research_study_list.title: Double Bubble, Toil and Trouble: Enhancing Certified Robustness through Transitivity
research_study_list.abstract:
In response to subtle adversarial examples flipping classifications of neural
network models, recent research has promoted certified robustness as a
solution. There, invariance of predictions to all norm-bounded attacks is
achieved through randomised smoothing of network inputs. Today's
state-of-the-art certifications make optimal use of the class output scores at
the input instance under test: no better radius of certification (under the
$L_2$ norm) is possible given only these score. However, it is an open question
as to whether such lower bounds can be improved using local information around
the instance under test. In this work, we demonstrate how today's "optimal"
certificates can be improved by exploiting both the transitivity of
certifications, and the geometry of the input space, giving rise to what we
term Geometrically-Informed Certified Robustness. By considering the smallest
distance to points on the boundary of a set of certifications this approach
improves certifications for more than $80\%$ of Tiny-Imagenet instances,
yielding an on average $5 \%$ increase in the associated certification. When
incorporating training time processes that enhance the certified radius, our
technique shows even more promising results, with a uniform $4$ percentage
point increase in the achieved certified radius.
research_study_list.full_text: Double Bubble, Toil and Trouble: Enhancing Certiп¬Ғed Robustness through Transitivity Andrew C. Cullen1вҲ— Paul Montague2 Shijie Liu1 Sarah M. Erfani1 Benjamin I.P. Rubinstein1 1School of Computing and Information Systems, University of Melbourne, Parkville, Australia 2Defence Science and Technology Group, Adelaide, Australia andrew.cullen@unimelb.edu.au Abstract In response to subtle adversarial examples п¬Ӯipping classiп¬Ғcations of neural network models, recent research has promoted certiп¬Ғed robustness as a solution. There, in- variance of predictions to all norm-bounded attacks is achieved through randomised smoothing of network inputs. TodayвҖҷs state-of-the-art certiп¬Ғcations make optimal use of the class output scores at the input instance under test: no better radius of certiп¬Ғcation (under the L2 norm) is possible given only these score. However, it is an open question as to whether such lower bounds can be improved using local information around the instance under test. In this work, we demonstrate how todayвҖҷs вҖңoptimalвҖқ certiп¬Ғcates can be improved by exploiting both the transitivity of certiп¬Ғcations, and the geometry of the input space, giving rise to what we term Geometrically-Informed Certiп¬Ғed Robustness. By considering the smallest distance to points on the boundary of a set of certiп¬Ғcations this approach improves certiп¬Ғca- tions for more than 80% of Tiny-Imagenet instances, yielding an on average 5% increase in the associated certiп¬Ғcation. When incorporating training time processes that enhance the certiп¬Ғed radius, our technique shows even more promising results, with a uniform 4 percentage point increase in the achieved certiп¬Ғed radius. 1 Introduction Learned models, including neural networks, are well known to be susceptible having the output changed by crafted perturbations to an input, that preserve the inputs semantic properties [2]. Neural networks not only misclassify these perturbationsвҖ”known as adversarial examplesвҖ”but they also assign high conп¬Ғdence to these incorrect predictions. These behaviours have been observed across a wide range of models and datasets, and appear to be a product of piecewise-linear interactions [13]. Crafting these adversarial examples typically involves gradient-based optimisation to construct small perturbations. These attacks have been applied to both black- and white-box models [31], and can be used to target class changes, to attack all classes [ 10], or even introduce backdoors into model behaviour [5]. To mitigate the inп¬Ӯuence of these attacks, defences have typically been designed to minimise the effect of a speciп¬Ғc attack (or attacks). Such defences are known as best response strategies in a Stackelberg security game where the defender leads the attacker. Best response defences inherently favour the attacker, as deployed mitigations can be defeated by identifying undefended attack frameworks. Moreover, the defender typically has to incorporate the defence at training time, and as such cannot response reactively to newly developed attacks. To circumvent these limitations, certiп¬Ғed guarantees of adversarial robustness can be constructed to identify class-constant regions around an input instance, that guarantee that all instances within a norm- bounded distance (typically L2) are not adversarial examples. Certiп¬Ғcations based on randomised smoothing of classiп¬Ғers around an input point are in a sense optimal [8]: based only on the prediction 36th Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2022). arXiv:2210.06077v1  [cs.LG]  12 Oct 2022class scores at the input point, no better radius is in general possible. Despite this, such certiп¬Ғcations fail to use readily availableвҖ”yet still localвҖ”information: the certiп¬Ғability of points nearby to the input of interest. The key insight of this work is that these neighbourhood points may generate certiп¬Ғed radius large enough to completely enclose that of a sample point, improving the radius of certiп¬Ғcation. This process can be extended to use the intersection of the regions of certiп¬Ғcation of multiple points, and the nature of the input domain itself to generate larger certiп¬Ғcations. This leads to our main contributionвҖ” Geometrically-Informed Certiп¬Ғed RobustnessвҖ”that enjoys certiп¬Ғcations exceeding those of the hitherto best-case guaranteed approach of Cohen et al. (2019) [8]. 2 Background and literature review Bounding mechanisms Conservative bounds upon the impact of norm-bounded perturbations can be constructed by way of either Interval Bound Propagation (IBP) which propagates interval bounds through the model; or Convex Relaxation, which utilise linear relaxation to construct bounding output polytopes over input bounded perturbations [34, 26, 41, 45, 46, 37, 28], in a manner that generally provides tighter bounds than IBP [25]. In contrast to Randomised Smoothing, bounding mechanisms employ augmented loss functions during training, which promote tight output bounds [42] at the cost of decreased applicability. Moreover they both exhibit a time and memory complexity that makes them infeasible for complex model architectures or high-dimensional data [40, 6, 21]. Randomised smoothing Outside of bounding mechanisms, another common framework for de- veloping certiп¬Ғcations leverages randomised smoothing [20], in which noise is applied to input instances to smooth model predictions, subject to a sampling distribution that is tied to the LP-norm of adversarial perturbations being certiп¬Ғed against. In contrast to other robustness mechanisms, this application of the noise is the only architectural change that is required to achieve certiп¬Ғcation. In the case of L2-norm bounded attacks, Gaussian sampling of the form xвҖІ i = x + yi where yi i.i.d. вҲј N(0,Пғ2) вҲҖiвҲҲ{1,...,N } (1) is employed for all test-time instances. These N samples are then used to estimate the expected output of the predicted class of x by way of the Monte-Carlo estimator EY[arg maxfОё(x + Y) = i] вүҲ 1 N NвҲ‘ j=1 1[arg maxfОё(xj) = i] . (2) While this Monte Carlo estimation of output expectations under randomised smoothing is a test-time process, model sensitivity to random perturbations may be decreased by performing adversarial training on such random perturbations. To mitigate the computational expense of large N sample sizes during each training update, training typically employs single draws from the noise distribution. Smoothing-based certiп¬Ғcations Based on randomised smoothing, certiп¬Ғed robustness can guaran- tee classiп¬Ғcation invariance for additive perturbations up to some Lp-norm r, with recent work also considering rotational and/or translational semantic attacks [23, 7]. Lp-norm certiп¬Ғcations were п¬Ғrst demonstrated by way of differential privacy [20, 11], with more recent approaches employing RГ©nyi divergence [22], and parametrising worst-case behaviours [ 8, 33]. By considering the worst-case L2-perturbations, Cohen et al. (2019) purports that the largest achievable pointwise certiп¬Ғcation is r= Пғ 2 ` ОҰвҲ’1 pE0[x]q вҲ’ОҰвҲ’1 pE1[x]q Лҳ . (3) Hhere {E0,E1}are the two largest class expectations (as per Equation (2)), Пғis the noise, and ОҰвҲ’1 is the inverse normal CDF, or Gaussian quantile function. 3 Geometrically-informed certiп¬Ғed robustness While the work contained within this paper can be applied generally, for this work we will focus upon certiп¬Ғcations of robustness about L2-norm bounded adversarial perturbations, for which we assume that the difп¬Ғculty of attacking a model is proportional to the size of the certiп¬Ғcation, based upon the need to evade both human and machine scrutiny [12]. Thus, constructing larger certiп¬Ғcations in such a context is inherently valuable. 2rвҖІ x1 x2 (a) Transitivity rвҖІвҖІ x1 x2x3 (b) Multiple Transitivity ЛҶr x1 x2 (c) Boundary Treatment Figure 1: Transitive certiп¬Ғcation exemplars. Over the remainder of this section we will document how it is possible to improve upon this provably best-case guarantee by exploiting several properties of certiп¬Ғed robustness. 3.1 Exploiting transitivity While it is provably true that Equation (3) is the largest achievable certiп¬Ғcation for any point x, it is possible to exploit the behaviour of points in the neighbourhood of x in order to enhance certiп¬Ғable radius. Such a solver is highly applicable for solving such problems, due to the inherent smoothing nature of randomised smoothing being applicable both to the function space and its derivatives. To implement the multiple transitivity based approach of Section 3.2, Algorithm 1 can trivially be adapted to evaluate derivatives with respect to Equation (12). The boundary treatment of Section 3.3 does not require any additional calculations, but instead is simply the result of applying Equation (16) to the output of Algorithm 1. 4 Extracting gradient information from non-differentiable functions Implementing the aformentioned process requires the ability to evaluate the gradient of the class expectations. This is problematic, as each class expectation is described in terms of a п¬Ғnite sum of non-differentiable indicator functions, as is seen in Equation (2). Within this work we have implemented two mechanisms to circumvent these conditions. The п¬Ғrst substitutes the arg max operation with a Gumbel-Softmax [15]. In doing so, the class expectations are rendered differentiable. The second approach involves recasting the Monte-Carlo estimators as integrals of the form E[arg maxfОё(x) = k] = вҲ« 1[arg maxfОё(y) = k]Пү(y,x)dy , (19) where Пү(y,x) is the multivariate-Normal probability distribution centred around x. (20) While this derivation is novel, the resultant gradient operator reп¬Ӯects those seen in prior works [33]. As such derivatives for the multiple transitivity approach are exclusively considered through autograd for both the Full and Approximate methods. 5 Experiments Conп¬Ғguration To evaluate the performance of our proposed certiп¬Ғcation improvements, we con- sidered the certiп¬Ғed radius produced for MNIST [19], CIFAR-10 [18], and Tiny-Imagenet [16], the latter of these is a 200-class variant of Imagenet [43] which downsamples images to 3 Г—60 Г—60. All datasets were modelled using the Resnet18 architecture in PyTorch [32], with Tiny-Imagenet also utilising 2D adaptive average pooling. For both MNIST and CIFAR-10, our experimentation utilised a single NVIDIA P100 GPU core with 12 GB of GPU RAM, with expectations estimated over 1500 samples. Training employed Cross Entropy loss with a batch size of 128 over 50 epochs. Each epoch involved every training example was perturbed with a single perturbation drawn from N(0,Пғ2), which was added prior to normalisation. Parameter optimisation was performed with Adam [ 17], with the learning rate set as 0.001. Tiny-Imagenet training and evaluation utilised 3 P100 GPUвҖҷs and utilised 1000 samples. Training occurred using SGD over 80 epochs, with a starting learning rate of 0.1, decreasing by a factor of 10 after 30 and 60 epochs, and momentum set to 0.9. The full code to implement our experiments can be found at https://github.com/andrew-cullen/DoubleBubble. Certiп¬Ғed accuracy To explore the performance advantage provided by our technique, we begin by considering the performance of Cohen et al. against the best of our approaches using both the approximate and full derivatives, as seen in Figure 2. While there are only minor differences between the two derivative approaches, there are clear regions of out performance relative to Cohen across all tested datasets. The proportion of this increase appears to be tied to the semantic complexity of the underlying dataset, with decreases in predictive accuracy (visible atR= 0) appearing to elicit greater percentage changes in the achieved certiп¬Ғed radius, as evidenced by the results for Tiny-Imagenet. Semantic complexity drives a decrease in the overall conп¬Ғdence of models, inducing a decrease in the separation between the highest class expectations E0 and E1. While this process shrinks the achievable certiп¬Ғed radius, a higher E1 provides more information to inform our gradient based search process, allowing for the identiп¬Ғcation of larger certiп¬Ғcations. This property would suggest that samples with a larger Rwould exhibit a decreased difference between our techniques and that of Cohen et al. would decrease, as smaller values of E1 provide less search information. This is driven by both an increase in the performance of the certiп¬Ғcations themselves, and in the number of instances able to be certiп¬Ғed in an improved fashion. This later property stems from the smoothing inп¬Ӯuence of Пғ, with larger levels of added noise inducing decreases in the difference between the highest class expectations, improving the ability for our search based mechanisms to identify performance improvements. Here increases in the performance of the boundary treatment are correlated with larger radii of certiп¬Ғcation, due to the multiplicative inп¬Ӯuence of Пғupon Equation (3). Instead it counter-intuitively appears that a smaller rвҖІinduces a search space for rвҖІвҖІwhich is less convex, and more difп¬Ғcult to converge upon. Alternative training routines Recent work has considered the potential for enhancing certiп¬Ғed robustness by modifying the underlying training regime to incentivise maximising the expectation gap between classes [33]. One such approach is MACER [44], which augments the training time behaviour by considering not just the classiп¬Ғcation loss, but also the Пө-robustness loss, which reп¬Ӯects proportion of training samples with robustness above a threshold level. Such a training time modiп¬Ғcation can increase the average certiп¬Ғed radius by 10 вҲ’20%, however doing so does increase the overall training cost by more than an order of magnitude. When applying Geometrically-Informed Certiп¬Ғed Robustness to models trained with MACER, Figure 5 demonstrates that our modiп¬Ғcations yield an even more signiп¬Ғcant improvement than those observed in Figure 2. Under training with MACER, the best performing of our techniques yielded an approximately 4 percentage point increase in the average certiп¬Ғcation. From this it is clear that while MACER does enhance the certiп¬Ғed radii at a sample point, it also induces enough smoothing in the neighbourhood of the sample point to allow transitivity to yield even more signiп¬Ғcant improvements than are present without MACER. However, we must emphasise that while such training time modiп¬Ғcations do producer consistently larger certiп¬Ғcations, doing so requires signiп¬Ғcantly more computational resources, both in terms of training time and GPU memory, as compared to the more traditional certiп¬Ғcation training regime. We also emphasise that training with MACER requires a degree of re-engineering. In contrast the training mechanism used for the remainder of this work only requires the addition of noise to samples prior to being passed through the model, and thus imposes signiп¬Ғcantly fewer engineering considerations. 90.0 0.5 1.0 1.5 2.0 2.5 3.0 3.5 4.0 Пғ 0 5 10 15 20Median Percentage Improvement Approx Approx Double Approx Mod Actual Actual Double Actual Mod Figure 6: Median percentage improvement in the Certiп¬Ғed Radius achieved by each of our approaches relative to Cohen et al. for Tiny-Imagenet across the level of additive noise Пғ. The median was chosen to provide a fair and representative comparison to Cohen et al., that п¬Ғlters out outliers in the percentage improvement when rвүӘ1. Limitations While the principles of our Geometrically Informed Certiп¬Ғed Robustness are extensible to Lp spaces, our experimental work has so far only considered L2-norm bounded perturbations due to the guarantee of best possible certiп¬Ғcation in this space provided by Cohen et al.. Further experimentation could also consider both these general spaces and a broader range of training methods, which have been shown to be able to tighten the achievable radius of certiп¬Ғcation [24]. We note that enhanced robustness certiп¬Ғcation have the potential to counter beneп¬Ғcial applications of adversarial attacks, such as those used to promote stylometric privacy [3]. However, we believe this drawbacks is signiп¬Ғcantly outweighed by the potential for enhanced conп¬Ғdence about models for which adversarial incentives exist. Finally, we also emphasise that our approach requires M evaluations of the certiп¬Ғed robustness, which each require N Monte-Carlo draws, resulting in time- and memory-complexity of O(MN) and O(NS) respectively, where Sis the size of the output logit vector. With respect to the memory- complexity, this is shared by any randomised smoothing based approach, and could be improved by implementing batching across the Monte-Carlo process. While the time cost can be problematic in some contexts, we emphasise that this framework is both requires both fewer adaptations to the training loop and signiп¬Ғcantly less training time relative to bound propagation approaches [21, 36]. We believe these costs can be reduced by performing the optimisation stages withNвҖІ<N model draws, and by potentially reusing model draws across the iterative process to approach the O(N) time-complexity of prior randomised smoothing based certiп¬Ғcations. Even at present, we believe that the increased computational cost is not intractable, especially for human-in-the-loop certiп¬Ғcations. 6 Acknowledgements This research was undertaken using the LIEF HPC-GPGPU Facility hosted at the University of Mel- bourne. This Facility was established with the assistance of LIEF Grant LE170100200. This work was also supported in part by the Australian Department of Defence Next Generation Technologies Fund, as part of the CSIRO/Data61 CRP AMLC project. Sarah Erfani is in part supported by Australian Research Council (ARC) Discovery Early Career Researcher Award (DECRA) DE220100680. 7 Conclusions This work has presented mechanisms that can be exploited to improve achievable levels of certiп¬Ғed robustness, based upon exploiting underlying geometric properties of robust neural networks. In doing so our Geometrically Informed Certiп¬Ғed Robustness approach has been able to generate certiп¬Ғcations that exceed prior guarantees by on average more than 5% at Пғ= 0.5, with the percentage increase improving quasi-linearly with Пғ. Incorporating training time modiп¬Ғcations likes MACER yields more promising results, with the best performing of our approaches yielding a 4 percentage point increase in the certiп¬Ғed proportion at a given radius. Being able to improve upon the size of these guarantees 10inherently increases the cost of constructing adversarial attacks against systems leveraging machine learning, especially in the case where the attacker has no ability to observe the size of the robustness certiп¬Ғcate. A.2 Ramiп¬Ғcations of the dimensionality for n> 3 To improve the achieved certiп¬Ғcation in the case n >3, the added set of hyperspheres must fully enclose the (dвҲ’1)-dimensional manifold that marks the intersection between S2 and S3. In two- dimensionsвҖ”as is used in the examplar Figure 1aвҖ”this intersection takes the form of two points. If LemmaвҖҷs 3.3 and 3.4 are to hold, then encompasingS2 вҲ©S3 will require two additional certiп¬Ғcation hyperspheres to be identiп¬Ғed. In the case where d = 3, the intersection between these two hyperspheres is the boundary of the circle (equivalent to a d= 2 hypersphere) with radius Лңr= d r2 2 вҲ’вҲҘx3 вҲ’x2вҲҘ2 вҲ’r2 3 + r2 2 2 вҲҘx3 вҲ’x2вҲҘ2 . (21) Thus any set of spheres {S2,S3,..., Sn}must uniformly cover all points on the boundary of this surface if we seek to improve the achieved certiп¬Ғcation. To provide an indicative example of how the complexity of the region that must be encircled grows with the underlying dimensionality, we now consider some properties of hyperspheres. In higher 14dimensions, prior work [39] has demonstrated that the volume contained within a d-dimensional hypersphere can be expressed as Vd(Лңr) = ЛңrdПҖd/2 О“ `d 2 + 1 Лҳ , (22) with an associated surface area of Sd(Лңr) = dpVd(r)q dprq ЛҮЛҮЛҮ r=Лңr = dЛңrdвҲ’1ПҖd/2 О“ `d 2 + 1 Лҳ . (23) Thus if S2 and S3 are d-dimensional hyperspheres, then their region of intersection would in turn be a (dвҲ’1)-dimensional hypersphere, the exterior boundary of which scales with ЛңrdвҲ’2. While Лңrmay be less than 1, it should also be true that any additional spheres would likely have an associated radii less than Лңr. As such there would appear to be a power-law proportionality with respect to (dвҲ’1) between the area covered by the intersection manifold and the size of spheres over which we would seek to enclose said manifold. This underscores the complexity of п¬Ғnding a set of hyperspheres to encircle the boundary of S2 вҲ©S3. To give further evidence in the growth of complexity, let us consider a unit-hypersphere inRd that represents the intersection of two hyperspheres in Rd+1. The task of covering such a hypersphere is similar to that of the sphere packing kissing number k(d) [9], which describes the number of touching-but-not-overlapping unit-hyperspheres that can exist upon the surface of a d-dimensional hypersphere. To date, the kissing number has only been solved for the following dimensions outlined in Table 2, however it has been shown to exhibit exponential growth [4]. Table 2: Known Kissing numbers k(d) for d-dimensional hyperspheres d 1 2 3 4 8 24 k(d) 2 6 12 [35] 24 [29] 240 [30] 196,560 [30] Within the context of this work, the kissing number must be considered to be a signiп¬Ғcant under- estimate of the number of boundary spheres that would be required to be found, as we must cover all the space around the central sphere (rather than just maximising the number of hyperspheres without intersection), and it is unlikely that the smallest of the set of encircling hyperspheres has the same radius as the region to be encircled. As such, we can be highly conп¬Ғdent that the growth in complexity of the task of enclosing the boundary of intersection beyond the set of hyperspheres {S1,S2,S3}is exponential. We must also note that even if it were possible to perform such a bounding operation, the gains in certiп¬Ғed radius would be exceedingly minor. If the region of intersection between S2 and S3 was a hypersphere of radius Лҳr, then going from the case where n= 3 to n >3 would only increase the certiп¬Ғed radius from a (rвҖІ)2 + (Лҳr)2 to a (rвҖІ)2 + 9(Лҳr)2, which is trivial relative to the increase in computational complexity. As is shown in Figures 13 and 14, while the Full solver only exhibits sensitivity toОіwhen Оі >0.1, the approximate solvers are far more sensitive, with deleterious performance being observed forОі >0.01 in CIFAR-10, and even earlier for Tiny-Imagenet.
