Local Intrinsic Dimensionality Defense Details. The Local Intrinsic Dimensionality (Amsaleg et al., 2015) вҖңassesses the space-п¬Ғlling capability of the region surrounding a reference example, based on the distance distribution of the example to its neighborsвҖқ (Ma et al., 2018). The authors present evidence that the LID is signiп¬Ғcantly larger for adversarial examples generated by existing attacks than for normal images, and they construct a classiп¬Ғer that can distinguish these adversarial images from normal images. Again, the authors indicate that LID is not intended as a defenseand only should be used to explore properties of adversarial examples. However, it would be natural to wonder whether it would be effective as a defense, so we study its robustness; our results conп¬Ғrm that it is not adequate as a defense. The method used to compute the LID relies on п¬Ғnding the k nearest neighbors, a non- differentiable operation, rendering gradient descent based methods ineffective. Let Sbe a mini-batch of N clean examples. Let ri(x) denote the distance (under metric d(x,y)) between sample xand its i-th nearest neighbor in S(under metric d). Then LID can be approximated by LIDd(x) =вҲ’ ( 1 k kвҲ‘ i=1 log ri(x) rk(x) )вҲ’1 where kis a defense hyperparameter the controls the num- ber of nearest neighbors to consider. The authors use the distance function dj(x,y) = оө№оө№f1..j(x) вҲ’f1..j(y) оө№оө№2 to measure the distance between the jth activation layers. The authors compute a vector of LID values for each sample: вҲ’вҲ’вҶ’LID(x) ={LIDdj (x)}n j=1. Finally, they compute the вҲ’вҲ’вҶ’LID(x) over the training data and adversarial examples generated on the training data, and train a logistic regression classiп¬Ғer to detect adversarial examples. We are grateful to the authors for releasing their complete source code. Discussion. While LID is not a defense itself, the authors assess the ability of LID to detect different types of attacks. Through solving the formulation min. |xвҲ’xвҖІ|2 2 + Оұ(в„“(xвҖІ) +LID-loss(xвҖІ)) the authors attempt to determine if the LID metric is a good metric for detecting adversarial examples. Here, LID-loss(В·) is a function that can be minimized to reduce the LID score. However, the authors report that this modiп¬Ғed attack still achieves 0% success. Because Carlini and WagnerвҖҷs в„“2 attack is unbounded, any time the attack does not reach 100% success indicates that the attack became stuck in a local minima. When this happens, it is often possible to slightly modify the loss function and return to 100% attack success (Carlini & Wagner, 2017b). In this case, we observe the reason that performing this type of adaptive attack fails is that gradient descent does not succeed in optimizing the LID loss, even though the LID computation is differentiable. Computing the LID term involves computing the k-nearest neighbors when comput- ing ri(x). Minimizing the gradient of the distance to the current k-nearest neighbors is not representative of the true direction to travel in for the optimal set of k-nearest neigh- bors. As a consequence, we п¬Ғnd that adversarial examples generated with gradient methods when penalizing for a high LID either (a) are not adversarial; or (b) are detected as adversarial, despite penalizing for the LID loss. Evaluation. We now evaluate what would happen if a defense would directly apply LID to detect adversarial ex- amples. Instead of performing gradient descent over a term that is difп¬Ғcult to differentiate through, we have found that generating high conп¬Ғdence adversarial examples (Carlini & Wagner, 2017a) (completely oblivious to to the detector) is sufп¬Ғcient to fool this detector. We obtain from the authors their detector trained on both the Carlini and WagnerвҖҷsв„“2 attack and train our own on the Fast Gradient Sign attack, both of which were found to be effective at detecting adver- sarial examples generated by other methods. By generating high-conп¬Ғdence adversarial examples minimizing в„“вҲһdis- tortion, we are able to reduce model accuracy to 2% success within Пө= 0.015. LID reports these adversarial examples are benign at a 97% rate (unmodiп¬Ғed test data is п¬Ӯagged as benign with a 98% rate). This evaluation demonstrates that the LID metric can be circumvented, and future work should carefully evaluate if building a detector relying on LID is robust to adversarial examples explicitly targeting such a detector. This work also raises questions whether a large LID is a fundamental characteristic of all adversarial examples, or whether it is a by-product of certain attacks. B. Defense-GAN Defense Details. The defender п¬Ғrst trains a Generative Adversarial Network with a generator G(z) that maps sam- ples from a latent space (typically zвҲјN(0,1)) to images that look like training data. Defense-GAN takes a trained classiп¬Ғer f(В·), and to classify an input x, instead of return- ing f(x), returns f(arg minz|G(z) вҲ’x|). To perform this projection to the manifold, the authors take many steps of gradient descent starting from different random initializa-Obfuscated Gradients Give a False Sense of Security: Circumventing Defenses to Adversarial Examples tions. Defense-GAN was not shown to be effective on CIFAR-10. We therefore evaluate it on MNIST (where it was argued to be secure). Discussion. In Samangouei et al. (2018), the authors con- struct a white-box attack by unrolling the gradient descent used during classiп¬Ғcation. Despite an unbounded в„“2 per- turbation size, Carlini and WagnerвҖҷs attack only reaches 30% misclassiп¬Ғcation rate on the most vulnerable model and under 5% on the strongest. This leads us to believe that unrolling gradient descent breaks gradients. Evaluation. We п¬Ғnd that adversarial examples do exist on the data manifold as described by the generator G(В·). However, Defense-GAN does not completely project to the projection of the generator, and therefore often does not identify these adversarial examples actually on the manifold. We therefore present two evaluations. In the п¬Ғrst, we as- sume that Defense-GAN were to able to perfectly project to the data manifold, and give a construction for generating adversarial examples. In the second, we take the actual im- plementation of Defense-GAN as it is, and perform BPDA to generate adversarial examples with 50% success under reasonable в„“2 bounds. Evaluation A. Performing the manifold projection is non- trivial as an inner optimization step when generating adver- sarial examples. To sidestep this difп¬Ғculty, we show that adversarial examples exist directly onthe projection of the generator. That is, we construct an adversarial example xвҖІ= G(zвҲ—) so that |xвҲ’xвҖІ|is small and c(x) Мё= c(xвҖІ). To do this, we solve the re-parameterized formulation min. вҲҘG(z) вҲ’xвҲҘ2 2 + cВ·в„“(G(z)). We initialize z= arg minz|G(z) вҲ’x|(also found via gradi- ent descent). We train a WGAN using the code the authors provide (Gulrajani et al., 2017), and a MNIST CNN to 99.3% accuracy. We run for 50k iterations of gradient descent for generating each adversarial example; this takes under one minute per instance. The unsecured classiп¬Ғer requires a mean в„“2 distor- tion of 0.0019 (per-pixel normalized, 1.45 un-normalized) to fool. When we mount our attack, we require a mean distortion of 0.0027, an increase in distortion of 1.46Г—; see Figure 2 for examples of adversarial examples. The reason our attacks succeed with 100% success without suffering from vanishing or exploding gradients is that our gradient computation only needs to differentiate through the genera- tor G(В·) once. Figure 2.Images on the MNIST test set. Row 1: Clean images. Row 2: Adversarial examples on an unsecured classiп¬Ғer. Row 3: Adversarial examples on Defense-GAN. Concurrent to our work, Ilyas et al. (2017) also develop a nearly identical approach to Defense-GAN; they also п¬Ғnd it is vulnerable to the attack we outline above, but increase the robustness further with adversarial training. We do not evaluate this extended approach. Evaluation B. The above attack does not succeed on Defense-GAN. While the adversarial examples are directly on the projection of the Generator, the projection process will actually move it off the projection. To mount an attack on the approximate projection process, we use the BPDA attack regularized for в„“2 distortion. Our attack approach is identical to that of PixelDefend, except we replace the manifold projection with a PixelCNN with the manifold projection by gradient descent on the GAN. Under these settings, we succeed at reducing model accu- racy to 55% with a maximum normalized distortion of.0051 for successful attacks.

reference_research_study_list.title: Evasion attacks against machine learning at test time
reference_research_study_list.abstract:
In security-sensitive applications, the success of machine learning depends
on a thorough vetting of their resistance to adversarial data. In one
pertinent, well-motivated attack scenario, an adversary may attempt to evade a
deployed system at test time by carefully manipulating attack samples. In this
work, we present a simple but effective gradient-based approach that can be
exploited to systematically assess the security of several, widely-used
classification algorithms against evasion attacks. Following a recently
proposed framework for security evaluation, we simulate attack scenarios that
exhibit different risk levels for the classifier by increasing the attacker's
knowledge of the system and her ability to manipulate attack samples. This
gives the classifier designer a better picture of the classifier performance
under evasion attacks, and allows him to perform a more informed model
selection (or parameter setting). We evaluate our approach on the relevant
security task of malware detection in PDF files, and show that such systems can
be easily evaded. We also sketch some countermeasures suggested by our
analysis.
reference_research_study_list.full_text: Evasion attacks against machine learning at test time Battista Biggio1, Igino Corona1, Davide Maiorca1, Blaine Nelson2, Nedim ЛҮSrndiВҙ c3, Pavel Laskov3, Giorgio Giacinto1, and Fabio Roli1 1 Dept. of Electrical and Electronic Engineering, University of Cagliari, Piazza dвҖҷArmi, 09123 Cagliari, Italy {battista.biggio, igino.corona, davide.maiorca, giacinto, roli}@diee.unica.it, WWW home page: http://prag.diee.unica.it/ 2 Institut fВЁ ur Informatik, UniversitВЁ at Potsdam, August-Bebel-StraГҹe 89, 14482 Potsdam, Germany bnelson@cs.uni-potsdam.de 3 Wilhelm Schickard Institute for Computer Science, University of TВЁ ubingen, Sand 1, 72076 TВЁ ubingen, Germany {nedim.srndic, pavel.laskov}@uni-tuebingen.de Abstract. In security-sensitive applications, the success of machine learn- ing depends on a thorough vetting of their resistance to adversarial data. In one pertinent, well-motivated attack scenario, an adversary may at- tempt to evade a deployed system at test time by carefully manipulating attack samples. In this work, we present a simple but eп¬Җective gradient- based approach that can be exploited to systematically assess the security of several, widely-used classiп¬Ғcation algorithms against evasion attacks. Following a recently proposed framework for security evaluation, we sim- ulate attack scenarios that exhibit diп¬Җerent risk levels for the classiп¬Ғer by increasing the attackerвҖҷs knowledge of the system and her ability to manipulate attack samples. This gives the classiп¬Ғer designer a better pic- ture of the classiп¬Ғer performance under evasion attacks, and allows him to perform a more informed model selection (or parameter setting). We evaluate our approach on the relevant security task of malware detection in PDF п¬Ғles, and show that such systems can be easily evaded. We also sketch some countermeasures suggested by our analysis. Keywords: adversarial machine learning, evasion attacks, support vec- tor machines, neural networks 1 Introduction Machine learning is being increasingly used in security-sensitive applications such as spam п¬Ғltering, malware detection, and network intrusion detection [3,5,9,11,14,15,16,19,21]. Due to their intrinsic adversarial nature, these applications diп¬Җer from the classi- cal machine learning setting in which the underlying data distribution is assumed to be stationary. To the contrary, in security-sensitive applications, samples (and, arXiv:1708.06131v1  [cs.CR]  21 Aug 2017thus, their distribution) can be actively manipulated by an intelligent, adaptive adversary to confound learning; e.g., to avoid detection, spam emails are often modiп¬Ғed by obfuscating common spam words or inserting words associated with legitimate emails [3,9,16,19]. This has led to an arms race between the designers of learning systems and their adversaries, which is evidenced by the increasing complexity of modern attacks and countermeasures. For these reasons, classical performance evaluation techniques are not suitable to reliably assess the secu- rity of learning algorithms, i.e., the performance degradation caused by carefully crafted attacks [5]. To better understand the security properties of machine learning systems in adversarial settings, paradigms from security engineering and cryptography have been adapted to the machine learning п¬Ғeld [2,5,14]. Following common se- curity protocols, the learning system designer should use proactive protection mechanisms that anticipate and prevent the adversarial impact. This requires (i) п¬Ғnding potential vulnerabilities of learning before they are exploited by the adversary; (ii) investigating the impact of the corresponding attacks ( i.e., eval- uating classiп¬Ғer security); and ( iii) devising appropriate countermeasures if an attack is found to signiп¬Ғcantly degrade the classiп¬ҒerвҖҷs performance. Two approaches have previously addressed security issues in learning. The min-max approach assumes the learner and attackerвҖҷs loss functions are antago- nistic, which yields relatively simple optimization problems [10,12]. A more gen- eral game-theoretic approach applies for non-antagonistic losses; e.g., a spam п¬Ғl- ter wants to accurately identify legitimate email while a spammer seeks to boost his spamвҖҷs appeal. Under certain conditions, such problems can be solved using a Nash equilibrium approach [7,8]. Both approaches provide a secure counterpart to their respective learning problems; i.e., an optimal anticipatory classiп¬Ғer. Realistic constraints, however, are too complex and multi-faceted to be incor- porated into existing game-theoretic approaches. Instead, we investigate the vul- nerabilities of classiп¬Ғcation algorithms by deriving evasion attacks in which the adversary aims to avoid detection by manipulating malicious test samples. 4 We systematically assess classiп¬Ғer security in attack scenarios that exhibit increas- ing risk levels, simulated by increasing the attackerвҖҷs knowledge of the system and her ability to manipulate attack samples. Our analysis allows a classiп¬Ғer designer to understand how the classiп¬Ғcation performance of each considered model degrades under attack, and thus, to make more informed design choices. The problem of evasion at test time was addressed in prior work, but lim- ited to linear and convex-inducing classiп¬Ғers [9,19,22]. In contrast, the methods presented in Sections 2 and 3 can generally evade linear or non-linear classiп¬Ғers using a gradient-descent approach inspired by GollandвҖҷs discriminative direc- tions technique [13]. Although we focus our analysis on widely-used classiп¬Ғers such as Support Vector Machines (SVMs) and neural networks, our approach is applicable to any classiп¬Ғer with a diп¬Җerentiable discriminant function. 4 Note that other kinds of attacks are possible, e.g., if the adversary can manipulate the training data. A comprehensive taxonomy of attacks can be found in [2,14].This paper is organized as follows. We present the evasion problem in Sec- tion 2 and our gradient-descent approach in Section 3. In Section 4 we п¬Ғrst visually demonstrate our attack on the task of handwritten digit recognition, and then show its eп¬Җectiveness on a realistic application related to the detection of PDF malware. Finally in Section 5, we summarize our contributions, discuss possibilities for improving security, and suggest future extensions of this work. 2 Optimal evasion at test time We consider a classiп¬Ғcation algorithm f : X вҶҰвҶ’Ythat assigns samples repre- sented in some feature space x вҲҲX to a label in the set of predeп¬Ғned classes y вҲҲY = {вҲ’1,+1}, where вҲ’1 (+1) represents the legitimate (malicious) class. The classiп¬Ғer f is trained on a dataset D= {xi,yi}n i=1 sampled from an under- lying distribution p(X,Y ). The label yc = f(x) given by a classiп¬Ғer is typically obtained by thresholding a continuous discriminant function g : XвҶҰвҶ’ R. In the sequel, we use yc to refer to the label assigned by the classiп¬Ғer as opposed to the true label y. We further assume that f(x) = вҲ’1 if g(x) <0, and +1 otherwise. 2.1 Adversary model To motivate the optimal attack strategy for evasion, it is necessary to disclose oneвҖҷs assumptions of the adversaryвҖҷs knowledge and ability to manipulate the data. To this end, we exploit a general model of the adversary that elucidates spe- ciп¬Ғc assumptions about adversaryвҖҷs goal, knowledge of the system, and capability to modify the underlying data distribution. The considered model is part of a more general framework investigated in our recent work [5], which subsumes eva- sion and other attack scenarios. This model can incorporate application-speciп¬Ғc constraints in the deп¬Ғnition of the adversaryвҖҷs capability, and can thus be ex- ploited to derive practical guidelines for developing the optimal attack strategy. AdversaryвҖҷs goal. As suggested by Laskov and Kloft [17], the adversaryвҖҷs goal should be deп¬Ғned in terms of a utility (loss) function that the adversary seeks to maximize (minimize). In the evasion setting, the attackerвҖҷs goal is to manipulate a single (without loss of generality, positive) sample that should be misclassiп¬Ғed. Strictly speaking, it would suп¬ғce to п¬Ғnd a sample x such that g(x) < вҲ’Пө for any Пө > 0; i.e., the attack sample only just crosses the decision boundary. 5 Such attacks, however, are easily thwarted by slightly adjusting the decision threshold. A better strategy for an attacker would thus be to create a sample that is misclassiп¬Ғed with high conп¬Ғdence; i.e., a sample minimizing the value of the classiп¬ҒerвҖҷs discriminant function,g(x), subject to some feasibility constraints. AdversaryвҖҷs knowledge. The adversaryвҖҷs knowledge about her targeted learn- ing system may vary signiп¬Ғcantly. Such knowledge may include: вҖ“ the training set or part of it; 5 This is also the setting adopted in previous work [9,19,22].вҖ“ the feature representation of each sample; i.e., how real objects such as emails, network packets are mapped into the classiп¬ҒerвҖҷs feature space; вҖ“ the type of a learning algorithm and the form of its decision function; вҖ“ the (trained) classiп¬Ғer model; e.g., weights of a linear classiп¬Ғer; вҖ“ or feedback from the classiп¬Ғer; e.g., classiп¬Ғer labels for samples chosen by the adversary. AdversaryвҖҷs capability. In the evasion scenario, the adversaryвҖҷs capability is limited to modiп¬Ғcations of test data; i.e.altering the training data is not allowed. However, under this restriction, variations in attackerвҖҷs power may include: вҖ“ modiп¬Ғcations to the input data (limited or unlimited); вҖ“ modiп¬Ғcations to the feature vectors (limited or unlimited); вҖ“ or independent modiп¬Ғcations to speciп¬Ғc features (the semantics of the input data may dictate that certain features are interdependent). Most of the previous work on evasion attacks assumes that the attacker can arbitrarily change every feature [8,10,12], but they constrain the degree of manipulation, e.g., limiting the number of modiп¬Ғcations, or their total cost. However, many real domains impose stricter restrictions. For example, in the task of PDF malware detection [20,24,25], removal of content is not feasible, and content addition may cause correlated changes in the feature vectors. 2.2 Attack scenarios In the sequel, we consider two attack scenarios characterized by diп¬Җerent levels of adversaryвҖҷs knowledge of the attacked system discussed below. Perfect knowledge (PK). In this setting, we assume that the adversaryвҖҷs goal is to minimize g(x), and that she has perfect knowledge of the targeted classiп¬Ғer; i.e., the adversary knows the feature space, the type of the classiп¬Ғer, and the trained model. The adversary can transform attack points in the test data but must remain within a maximum distance ofdmax from the original attack sample. We use dmax as parameter in our evaluation to simulate increasingly pessimistic attack scenarios by giving the adversary greater freedom to alter the data. The choice of a suitable distance measure d : XГ—X вҶҰвҶ’ R+ is application speciп¬Ғc [9,19,22]. Such a distance measure should reп¬Ӯect the adversaryвҖҷs eп¬Җort required to manipulate samples or the cost of these manipulations. For example, in spam п¬Ғltering, the attacker may be bounded by a certain number of words she can manipulate, so as not to lose the semantics of the spam message. Limited knowledge (LK). Here, we again assume that the adversary aims to minimize the discriminant function g(x) under the same constraint that each transformed attack point must remain within a maximum distance of dmax from the corresponding original attack sample. We further assume that the attacker knows the feature representation and the type of the classiп¬Ғer, but does not know either the learned classiп¬Ғer f or its training data D, and hence can not directlycompute g(x). However, we assume that she can collect a surrogate dataset DвҖІ = {ЛҶxi,ЛҶyi}nq i=1 of nq samples drawn from the same underlying distribution p(X,Y ) from which Dwas drawn. This data may be collected by an adversary in several ways; e.g., by sniп¬ғng some network traп¬ғc during the classiп¬Ғer operation, or by collecting legitimate and spam emails from an alternate source. Under this scenario, the adversary proceeds by approximating the discrimi- nant function g(x) as ЛҶg(x), where ЛҶg(x) is the discriminant function of a surrogate classiп¬Ғer ЛҶf learnt on DвҖІ. The amount of the surrogate data, nq, is an attack pa- rameter in our experiments. Since the adversary wants her surrogate ЛҶf to closely approximate the targeted classiп¬Ғer f, it stands to reason that she should learn ЛҶf using the labels assigned by the targeted classiп¬Ғer f, when such feedback is available. In this case, instead of using the true class labels ЛҶ yi to train ЛҶf, the adversary can query f with the samples of DвҖІand subsequently learn using the labels ЛҶyc i = f(ЛҶxi) for each xi. 2.3 Attack strategy Under the above assumptions, for any target malicious sample x0 (the adver- saryвҖҷs desired instance), an optimal attack strategy п¬Ғnds a samplexвҲ—to minimize g(В·) or its estimate ЛҶg(В·), subject to a bound on its distance 6 from x0: xвҲ—= arg min x ЛҶg(x) (1) s.t. d(x,x0) вүӨdmax. Generally, this is a non-linear optimization problem. One may approach it with many well-known techniques, like gradient descent, or quadratic techniques such as NewtonвҖҷs method, BFGS, or L-BFGS. We choose a gradient-descent procedure. However, ЛҶg(x) may be non-convex and descent approaches may not achieve a global optima. Instead, the descent path may lead to a п¬Ӯat region (local minimum) outside of the samplesвҖҷ support ( i.e., where p(x) вүҲ0) where the attack sample may or may not evade depending on the behavior of g in this unsupported region (see left and middle plots in Figure 1). Locally optimizing ЛҶg(x) with gradient descent is particularly susceptible to failure due to the nature of a discriminant function. Besides its shape, for many classiп¬Ғers, g(x) is equivalent to a posterior estimatep(yc = вҲ’1|x); e.g., for neural networks, and SVMs [23]. The discriminant function does not incorporate the evidence we have about the data distribution, p(x), and thus, using gradient descent to optimize Eq. 1 may lead into unsupported regions (p(x) вүҲ0). Because of the insigniп¬Ғcance of these regions, the value of g is relatively unconstrained by criteria such as risk minimization. This problem is compounded by our п¬Ғnite (and possibly small) training set, since it provides little evidence in these regions 6 One can also incorporate additional application-speciп¬Ғc constraints on the attack samples. For instance, the box constraint 0 вүӨxf вүӨ1 can be imposed if the fth feature is normalized in [0,1], or x0 f вүӨxf can be used if the fth feature of the target x0 can be only incremented.g(x) вҲ’ О»  p(x|yc=вҲ’1), О» =0     вҲ’4 вҲ’3 вҲ’2 вҲ’1 0 1 2 3 4вҲ’4 вҲ’2 0 2 4 вҲ’1 вҲ’0.5 0 0.5 1 g(x) вҲ’ О»  p(x|yc=вҲ’1), О» =0     вҲ’4 вҲ’3 вҲ’2 вҲ’1 0 1 2 3 4вҲ’4 вҲ’2 0 2 4 вҲ’1 вҲ’0.5 0 0.5 1 g(x) вҲ’ О»  p(x|yc=вҲ’1), О» =20     вҲ’4 вҲ’3 вҲ’2 вҲ’1 0 1 2 3 4вҲ’4 вҲ’2 0 2 4 вҲ’4.5 вҲ’4 вҲ’3.5 вҲ’3 вҲ’2.5 вҲ’2 вҲ’1.5 вҲ’1 Fig. 1.Diп¬Җerent scenarios for gradient-descent-based evasion procedures. In each, the function g(x) of the learned classiп¬Ғer is plotted with a color map with high values (red-orange-yellow) for the malicious class, and low values (green-cyan-blue) for the legitimate class. The decision boundary is shown in black. For every malicious sample, we plot the gradient descent path against a classiп¬Ғer with a closed boundary around the malicious class ( top-left) and against a classiп¬Ғer with a closed boundary around the benign class (top-right). Finally, we plot the modiп¬Ғed objective function of Eq. (2) and the resulting descent paths against a classiп¬Ғer with a closed boundary around the benign class (bottom). to constrain the shape of g. Thus, when our gradient descent procedure produces an evasion example in these regions, the attacker cannot be conп¬Ғdent that this sample will actually evade the corresponding classiп¬Ғer. Therefore, to increase the probability of successful evasion, the attacker should favor attack points from densely populated regions of legitimate points, where the estimate ЛҶg(x) is more reliable (closer to the real g(x)), and tends to become negative in value. To overcome this shortcoming, we introduce an additional component into our attack objective, which estimates p(x|yc = вҲ’1) using a density estimator. This term acts as a penalizer for x in low density regions and is weighted by a parameter О»вүҘ0 yielding the following modiп¬Ғed optimization problem: arg min x F(x) = ЛҶg(x) вҲ’О» n вҲ‘ i|yc i =вҲ’1 k (xвҲ’xi h ) (2) s.t. d(x,x0) вүӨdmax , (3) where h is a bandwidth parameter for a kernel density estimator (KDE), and n is the number of benign samples ( yc = вҲ’1) available to the adversary. ThisAlgorithm 1 Gradient-descent evasion attack Input: x0, the initial attack point; t, the step size; О», the trade-oп¬Җ parameter; Пө> 0 a small constant. Output: xвҲ—, the п¬Ғnal attack point. 1: mвҶҗ0. 2: repeat 3: mвҶҗm+ 1 4: Set вҲҮF(xmвҲ’1) to a unit vector aligned with вҲҮg(xmвҲ’1) вҲ’О»вҲҮp(xmвҲ’1|yc = вҲ’1). 5: xm вҶҗxmвҲ’1 вҲ’tвҲҮF(xmвҲ’1) 6: if d(xm,x0) >dmax then 7: Project xm onto the boundary of the feasible region. 8: end if 9: until F(xm) вҲ’F ( xmвҲ’1) <Пө 10: return: xвҲ— = xm alternate objective trades oп¬Җ between minimizing ЛҶ g(x) (or p(yc = вҲ’1|x)) and maximizing the estimated density p(x|yc = вҲ’1). The extra component favors attack points that imitate features of known legitimate samples. In doing so, it reshapes the objective function and thereby biases the resulting gradient descent towards regions where the negative class is concentrated (see the bottom plot in Fig. 1). This produces a similar eп¬Җect to that shown by mimicry attacks in network intrusion detection [11].7 For this reason, although our setting is rather diп¬Җerent, in the sequel we refer to this extra term as the mimicry component. Finally, we point out that, when mimicry is used ( О» > 0), our gradient descent clearly follows a suboptimal path compared to the case when only g(x) is minimized (О»= 0). Therefore, more modiп¬Ғcations may be required to reach the same value of g(x) attained when О»= 0. However, as previously discussed, when О»= 0, our descent approach may terminate at a local minimum where g(x) >0, without successfully evading detection. This behavior can thus be qualitatively regarded as a trade-oп¬Җ between the probability of evading the targeted classiп¬Ғer and the number of times that the adversary must modify her samples. 3 Gradient descent attacks Algorithm 1 solves the optimization problem in Eq. 2 via gradient descent. We assume g(x) to be diп¬Җerentiable almost everywhere (subgradients may be used at discontinuities). However, note that if g is non-diп¬Җerentiable or insuп¬ғciently smooth, one may still use the mimicry / KDE term of Eq. (2) as a search heuristic. This investigation is left to future work. 7 Mimicry attacks [11] consist of camouп¬Ӯaging malicious network packets to evade anomaly-based intrusion detection systems by mimicking the characteristics of the legitimate traп¬ғc distribution.x1  xd  Оҙ1  Оҙk  Оҙm  xi g(x)  w1  wk  wm  v11  vmd  vk1  вҖҰ вҖҰ  вҖҰ вҖҰ  Fig. 2.The architecture of a multi-layer perceptron with a single hidden layer. 3.1 Gradients of discriminant functions Linear classiп¬Ғers. Linear discriminant functions are g(x) = вҹЁw,xвҹ©+ b where w вҲҲRd is the feature weights and bвҲҲR is the bias. Its gradient is вҲҮg(x) = w. Support vector machines. For SVMs, g(x) = вҲ‘ iОұiyik(x,xi) +b. The gradi- ent is thusвҲҮg(x) = вҲ‘ iОұiyiвҲҮk(x,xi). In this case, the feasibility of our approach depends on whether the kernel gradient вҲҮk(x,xi) is computable as it is for many numeric kernels. For instance, the gradient of the RBF kernel, k(x,xi) = exp{вҲ’ОівҲҘx вҲ’xiвҲҘ2}, is вҲҮk(x,xi) = вҲ’2Оіexp{вҲ’ОівҲҘx вҲ’xiвҲҘ2}(x вҲ’xi), and for the polynomial kernel, k(x,xi) = (вҹЁx,xiвҹ©+ c)p, it is вҲҮk(x,xi) = p(вҹЁx,xiвҹ©+ c)pвҲ’1xi. Neural networks. For a multi-layer perceptron with a single hidden layer of m neurons and a sigmoidal activation function, we decompose its discriminant function gas follows (see Fig. 2):g(x) = (1+eвҲ’h(x))вҲ’1, h(x) = вҲ‘m k=1 wkОҙk(x)+b, Оҙk(x) = (1 + eвҲ’hk(x))вҲ’1, hk(x) = вҲ‘d j=1 vkjxj + bk. From the chain rule, the ith component of вҲҮg(x) is thus given by: вҲӮg вҲӮxi = вҲӮg вҲӮh вҲ‘m k=1 вҲӮh вҲӮОҙk вҲӮОҙk вҲӮhk вҲӮhk вҲӮxi = g(x)(1 вҲ’g(x)) вҲ‘m k=1 wkОҙk(x)(1 вҲ’Оҙk(x))vki . 3.2 Gradients of kernel density estimators Similarly to SVMs, the gradient of kernel density estimators depends on the kernel gradient. We consider generalized RBF kernels of the form k (xвҲ’xi h ) = exp ( вҲ’d(x,xi) h ) , where d(В·,В·) is any suitable distance function. Here we use the same distance d(В·,В·) deп¬Ғned in Eq. (3), but, in general, they can be diп¬Җerent. For в„“2- and в„“1-norms (i.e., RBF and Laplacian kernels), the KDE (sub)gradients are respectively given by: вҲ’2 nh вҲ‘ i|yc i =вҲ’1 exp ( вҲ’вҲҘxвҲ’xiвҲҘ2 2 h ) (x вҲ’xi) , вҲ’1 nh вҲ‘ i|yc i =вҲ’1 exp ( вҲ’вҲҘxвҲ’xiвҲҘ1 h ) (x вҲ’xi) .Note that the scaling factor here is proportional to O( 1 nh). Therefore, to inп¬Ӯuence gradient descent with a signiп¬Ғcant mimicking eп¬Җect, the value of О» in the objective function should be chosen such that the value of О» nh is comparable with (or higher than) the range of values of the discriminant function ЛҶg(x). 3.3 Descent in discrete spaces In discrete spaces, gradient approaches travel through infeasible portions of the feature space. In such cases, we need to п¬Ғnd a feasible neighborx that maximally decrease F(x). A simple approach to this problem is to probe F at every point in a small neighborhood of x, which would however require a large number of queries. For classiп¬Ғers with a diп¬Җerentiable decision function, we can instead select the neighbor whose change best aligns with вҲҮF(x) and decreases the objective function; i.e., to prevent overshooting a minimum. 4 Experiments In this section, we п¬Ғrst report a toy example from the MNIST handwritten digit classiп¬Ғcation task [18] to visually demonstrate how the proposed algorithm modiп¬Ғes digits to mislead classiп¬Ғcation. We then show the eп¬Җectiveness of the proposed attack on a more realistic and practical scenario: the detection of mal- ware in PDF п¬Ғles. 4.1 A toy example on handwritten digits Similar to Globerson and Roweis [12], we consider discriminating between two distinct digits from the MNIST dataset [18]. Each digit example is represented as a gray-scale image of 28 Г—28 pixels arranged in raster-scan-order to give feature vectors of d= 28 Г—28 = 784 values. We normalized each feature (pixel) x вҲҲ[0,1]d by dividing its value by 255, and we constrained the attack samples to this range. Accordingly, we optimized Eq. (2) subject to 0 вүӨxf вүӨ1 for all f. We only consider the perfect knowledge (PK) attack scenario. We used the Manhattan distance ( в„“1-norm), d, both for the kernel density estimator ( i.e., a Laplacian kernel) and for the constraint d(x,x0) вүӨdmax in Eq. (3), which bounds the total diп¬Җerence between the gray level values of the original image x0 and the attack image x. We used dmax = 5000 255 to limit the total gray-level change to 5000. At each iteration, we increased the в„“1-norm value of x вҲ’x0 by 10 255 , or equivalently, we changed the total gray level by 10. This is eп¬Җectively the gradient step size. The targeted classiп¬Ғer was an SVM with the linear kernel and C = 1. We randomly chose 100 training samples and applied the attacks to a correctly-classiп¬Ғed positive sample. In Fig. 3 we illustrate gradient attacks in which a вҖң3вҖқ is to be misclassiп¬Ғed as a вҖң7вҖқ. The left image shows the initial attack point, the middle image shows the п¬Ғrst attack image misclassiп¬Ғed as legitimate, and the right image shows the attack point after 500 iterations. When О» = 0, the attack images exhibit onlyBefore attack (3 vs 7) 5 10 15 20 25 5 10 15 20 25 After attack, g(x)=0 5 10 15 20 25 5 10 15 20 25 After attack, last iter. 5 10 15 20 25 5 10 15 20 25 0 500 вҲ’2 вҲ’1 0 1 2 g(x) number of iterations Before attack (3 vs 7) 5 10 15 20 25 5 10 15 20 25 After attack, g(x)=0 5 10 15 20 25 5 10 15 20 25 After attack, last iter. 5 10 15 20 25 5 10 15 20 25 0 500 вҲ’2 вҲ’1 0 1 2 g(x) number of iterations Fig. 3.Illustration of the gradient attack on the digit data, for О»= 0 (top row) and О»= 10 (bottom row). Without a mimicry component (О»= 0) gradient descent quickly decreases g but the resulting attack image does not resemble a вҖң7вҖқ. In contrast, the attack minimizes g slower when mimicry is applied ( О»= 0) but the п¬Ғnal attack image closely resembles a mixture between вҖң3вҖқ and вҖң7вҖқ, as the term вҖңmimicryвҖқ suggests. a weak resemblance to the target class вҖң7вҖқ but are, nevertheless, reliably mis- classiп¬Ғed. This is the same eп¬Җect demonstrated in the top-left plot of Fig. 1: the classiп¬Ғer is evaded by making the attack sample suп¬ғciently dissimilar from the malicious class. Conversely, when О» = 10, the attack images strongly resemble the target class because the mimicry term favors samples that are more similar to the target class. This is the same eп¬Җect seen in the bottom plot of Fig. 1. Finally note that, as expected, g(x) tends to decrease more gracefully when mimicry is used, as we follow a suboptimal descent path. Since the targeted classiп¬Ғer can be easily evaded when О» = 0, exploiting the mimicry component would not be the optimal choice in this case. However, in the case of limited knowledge, as discussed at the end of Section 2.3, mimicry may allow us to trade for a higher probability of evading the targeted classiп¬Ғer, at the expense of a higher number of modiп¬Ғcations. 4.2 Malware detection in PDF п¬Ғles We now focus on the task of discriminating between legitimate and malicious PDF п¬Ғles, a popular medium for disseminating malware [26]. PDF п¬Ғles are excel- lent vectors for malicious-code, due to their п¬Ӯexible logical structure, which can described by a hierarchy of interconnected objects. As a result, an attack can be easily hidden in a PDF to circumvent п¬Ғle-type п¬Ғltering. The PDF format fur- ther allows a wide variety of resources to be embedded in the document including JavaScript, Flash, and even binary programs. The type of the embedded ob- ject is speciп¬Ғed by keywords, and its content is in a data stream. Several recentworks proposed machine-learning techniques for detecting malicious PDFs using the п¬ҒleвҖҷs logical structure to accurately identify the malware [20,24,25]. In this case study, we use the feature representation of Maiorca et al.[20] in which each feature corresponds to the tally of occurrences of a given keyword. The PDF structure imposes natural constraints on attacks. Although it is diп¬ғcult to remove an embedded object (and its keywords) from a PDF without corrupting the PDFвҖҷs п¬Ғle structure, it is rather easy to insert new objects (and, thus, keywords) through the addition of a new version to the PDF п¬Ғle [1]. In our feature representation, this is equivalent to allowing only feature increments,i.e., requiring x0 вүӨx as an additional constraint in the optimization problem given by Eq. (2). Further, the total diп¬Җerence in keyword counts between two samples is their Manhattan distance, which we again use for the kernel density estimator and the constraint in Eq. (3). Accordingly, dmax is the maximum number of additional keywords that an attacker can add to the original x0. Experimental setup. For experiments, we used a PDF corpus with 500 mali- cious samples from the Contagio dataset8 and 500 benign samples collected from the web. We randomly split the data into п¬Ғve pairs of training and testing sets with 500 samples each to average the п¬Ғnal results. The features (keywords) were extracted from each training set as described in [20]. On average, 100 keywords were found in each run. Further, we also bounded the maximum value of each feature to 100, as this value was found to be close to the 95 th percentile for each feature. This limited the inп¬Ӯuence of outlying samples. We simulated the perfect knowledge (PK) and the limited knowledge (LK) scenarios described in Section 2.1. In the LK case, we set the number of samples used to learn the surrogate classiп¬Ғer to ng = 100. The reason is to demonstrate that even with a dataset as small as the 20% of the original training set size, the adversary may be able to evade the targeted classiп¬Ғer with high reliability. Further, we assumed that the adversary uses feedback from thetargeted classiп¬Ғer f; i.e., the labels ЛҶyc i = f(ЛҶxi) for each surrogate sample ЛҶxi вҲҲDвҖІ.9 As discussed in Section 3.2, the value of О»is chosen according to the scale of the discriminant function g(x), the bandwidth parameter hof the kernel density estimator, and the number of legitimate samples n in the surrogate training set. For computational reasons, to estimate the value of the KDE at x, we only consider the 50 nearest (legitimate) training samples to x; therefore, nвүӨ50 in our case. The bandwidth parameter was set to h = 10, as this value provided a proper rescaling of the Manhattan distances observed in our dataset for the KDE. We thus set О»= 500 to be comparable with O(nh). For each targeted classiп¬Ғer and training/testing pair, we learned п¬Ғve sur- rogate classiп¬Ғers by randomly selecting ng samples from the test set, and we averaged their results. For SVMs, we sought a surrogate classiп¬Ғer that would correctly match the labels from the targeted classiп¬Ғer; thus, we used parameters C = 100, and Оі = 0.1 (for the RBF kernel) to heavily penalize training errors. 8 http://contagiodump.blogspot.it 9 Similar results were also obtained using the true labels (without relabeling), since the targeted classiп¬Ғers correctly classiп¬Ғed almost all samples in the test set.0 10 20 30 40 500 0.2 0.4 0.6 0.8 1 SVM (linear), О» =0 dmax FN     PK(C=1) LK (C=1) PK(C=100) LK (C=100) 0 10 20 30 40 500 0.2 0.4 0.6 0.8 1 SVM (linear) вҲ’ C=1, О» =500 dmax FN     PK LK 0 10 20 30 40 500 0.2 0.4 0.6 0.8 1 SVM (RBF) вҲ’ C=1, О» =0 dmax FN     PK (Оі =0.1) LK (Оі =0.1) PK (Оі =1) LK (Оі =1) 0 10 20 30 40 500 0.2 0.4 0.6 0.8 1 SVM (RBF) вҲ’ C=1, Оі =1, О» =500 dmax FN     PK LK 0 10 20 30 40 500 0.2 0.4 0.6 0.8 1 Neural Netw., О» =0 dmax FN     PK (m=5) LK (m=5) PK (m=20) LK (m=20) 0 10 20 30 40 500 0.2 0.4 0.6 0.8 1 Neural Netw. вҲ’ m=5, О» =500 dmax FN     PK LK Fig. 4.Experimental results for SVMs with linear and RBF kernel (п¬Ғrst and sec- ond row), and for neural networks (third row). We report the FN values (attained at FP=0.5%) for increasing dmax. For the sake of readability, we report the average FN value Вұhalf standard deviation (shown with error bars). Results for perfect (PK) and limited (LK) knowledge attacks with О» = 0 (without mimicry) are shown in the п¬Ғrst column, while results with О» = 500 (with mimicry) are shown in the second column. In each plot we considered diп¬Җerent values of the classiп¬Ғer parameters, i.e., the regu- larization parameter C for the linear SVM, the kernel parameter Оі for the SVM with RBF kernel, and the number of neurons min the hidden layer for the neural network, as reported in the plot title and legend.Experimental results. We report our results in Figure 4, in terms of the false negative (FN) rate attained by the targeted classiп¬Ғers as a function of the maximum allowable number of modiп¬Ғcations, dmax вҲҲ[0,50]. We compute the FN rate corresponding to a п¬Ғxed false positive (FP) rate of FP= 0 .5%. For dmax = 0, the FN rate corresponds to a standard performance evaluation using unmodiп¬Ғed PDFs. As expected, the FN rate increases with dmax as the PDF is increasingly modiп¬Ғed. Accordingly, a more secure classiп¬Ғer will exhibit a more graceful increase of the FN rate. Results for О» = 0. We п¬Ғrst investigate the eп¬Җect of the proposed attack in the PK case, without considering the mimicry component (Figure 4, п¬Ғrst column), for varying parameters of the considered classiп¬Ғers. The linear SVM (Figure 4, top-left plot) is almost always evaded with as few as 5 to 10 modiп¬Ғcations, in- dependent of the regularization parameter C. It is worth noting that attacking a linear classiп¬Ғer amounts to always incrementing the value of the same highest- weighted feature (corresponding to the /Linearized keyword in the majority of the cases) until it reaches its upper bound. This continues with the next highest weighted non-bounded feature until termination. This occurs simply because the gradient of g(x) does not depend on x for a linear classiп¬Ғer (see Section 3.1). With the RBF kernel (Figure 4, middle-left plot), SVMs exhibit a similar be- havior with C = 1 and various values of its Оі parameter,10 and the RBF SVM provides a higher degree of security compared to linear SVMs ( cf. top-left plot and middle-left plot in Figure 4). Interestingly, compared to SVMs, neural net- works (Figure 4, bottom-left plot) seem to be much more robust against the proposed evasion attack. This behavior can be explained by observing that the decision function of neural networks may be characterized by п¬Ӯat regions ( i.e., regions where the gradient of g(x) is close to zero). Hence, the gradient descent algorithm based solely on g(x) essentially stops after few attack iterations for most of the malicious samples, without being able to п¬Ғnd a suitable attack. In the LK case, without mimicry, classiп¬Ғers are evaded with a probability only slightly lower than that found in the PK case, even when only ng = 100 surrogate samples are used to learn the surrogate classiп¬Ғer. This aspect highlights the threat posed by a skilled adversary with incomplete knowledge: only a small set of samples may be required to successfully attack the target classiп¬Ғer using the proposed algorithm. Results for О» = 500 . When mimicry is used (Figure 4, second column), the success of the evasion of linear SVMs (with C = 1) decreases both in the PK (e.g., compare the blue curve in the top-left plot with the solid blue curve in the top-right plot) and LK case ( e.g., compare the dashed red curve in the top-left plot with the dashed blue curve in the top-right plot). The reason is that the computed direction tends to lead to a slower descent; i.e., a less direct path that often requires more modiп¬Ғcations to evade the classiп¬Ғer. In the non-linear case (Figure 4, middle-right and bottom-right plot), instead, mimicking exhibits some beneп¬Ғcial aspects for the attacker, although the constraint on feature addition 10 We also conducted experiments using C = 0 .1 and C = 100, but did not п¬Ғnd signiп¬Ғcant diп¬Җerences compared to the presented results using C = 1.may make it diп¬ғcult to properly mimic legitimate samples. In particular, note how the targeted SVMs with RBF kernel (with C = 1 and Оі = 1) in the PK case (e.g., compare the solid blue curve in the middle-left plot with the solid blue curve in the middle-right plot) is evaded with a signiп¬Ғcantly higher probability than in the case of О»= 0. The reason is that, as explained at the end of Section 2.3, a pure descent strategy on g(x) may п¬Ғnd local minima ( i.e., attack samples) that do not evade detection, while the mimicry component biases the descent towards regions of the feature space more densely populated by legitimate samples, where g(x) eventually attains lower values. For neural networks, this aspect is even more evident, in both the PK and LK settings (compare the dashed/solid curves in the bottom-left plot with those in the bottom-right plot), sinceg(x) is essentially п¬Ӯat far from the decision boundary, and thus pure gradient descent on g can not even commence for many malicious samples, as previously mentioned. In this case, the mimicry term is thus critical for п¬Ғnding a reasonable descent path to evasion. Discussion. Our attacks raise questions about the feasibility of detecting ma- licious PDFs solely based on logical structure. We found that /Linearized, /OpenAction, /Comment, /Root and /PageLayout were among the most com- monly manipulated keywords. They indeed are found mainly in legitimate PDFs, but can be easily added to malicious PDFs by the versioning mechanism. The attacker can simply insert comments inside the malicious PDF п¬Ғle to augment its /Comment count. Similarly, she can embedlegitimate OpenAction code to add /OpenAction keywords or add new pages to insert /PageLayout keywords. 5 Conclusions, limitations and future work In this work we proposed a simple algorithm for evasion of classiп¬Ғers with dif- ferentiable discriminant functions. We investigated the attack eп¬Җectiveness in the case of perfect and limited knowledge of the attacked system, and empir- ically showed that very popular classiп¬Ғcation algorithms (in particular, SVMs and neural networks) can still be evaded with high probability even if the adver- sary can only learn a copy of the classiп¬Ғer from a small surrogate dataset. Thus, our investigation raises important questions on whether such algorithms can be reliably employed in security-sensitive applications. We believe that the proposed attack formulation can be extended to classiп¬Ғers with non-diп¬Җerentiable discriminant functions as well, such as decision trees and k-nearest neighbors; e.g., by deп¬Ғning suitable search heuristics similar to our mimicry term to minimize g(x). Interestingly our analysis also suggests improvements for classiп¬Ғer security. From Fig. 1, it is clear that a tighter enclosure of the legitimate samples increas- ingly forces the adversary to mimic the legitimate class, which may not always be possible; e.g., malicious network packets or PDF п¬Ғles must contain a valid exploit for the attack to be successful. Accordingly, more secure classiп¬Ғers can be designed by employing regularization terms that promote enclosure of the legit- imate class; e.g., by penalizing вҖңblind spotsвҖқ - regions with low p(x) - classiп¬Ғedas legitimate. Alternatively, one may explicitly model the attack distribution, as in [4]; or add the generated attack samples to the training set. Nevertheless, improving security probably must be balanced with a higher FP rate. In our example applications, the feature representations could be inverted to obtain a corresponding real-world objects (e.g., spam emails, or PDF п¬Ғles);i.e., it is straightforward to manipulate the given real-world object to obtain the desired feature vector xвҲ— of the optimal attack. However, in practice some complex feature mappings can not be easily inverted; e.g., n-gram features [11]. Another idea would be to modify the real-world object at each step of the gradient descent to obtain a sample in the feature space which is as close as possible to the sample that would be obtained at the next attack iteration. A similar technique has been already exploited by [6] to overcome the pre-image problem. Other interesting extensions of our work may be to (i) consider more eп¬Җective strategies such as those proposed by [19,22] to build a small but representative set of surrogate data; and ( ii) improve the classiп¬Ғer estimate ЛҶg(x). To this end, one may exploit ensemble techniques such as bagging or the random subspace method to train several classiп¬Ғers and then average their output. Acknowledgments. This work has been partly supported by the project CRP- 18293, L.R. 7/2007, Bando 2009, and by the project вҖңAdvanced and secure sharing of multimedia data over social networks in the future InternetвҖқ (CUP F71J11000690002), both funded by Regione Autonoma della Sardegna. Davide Maiorca gratefully acknowledges Regione Autonoma della Sardegna for the п¬Ғ- nancial support of his PhD scholarship. Blaine Nelson thanks the Alexander von Humboldt Foundation for providing additional п¬Ғnancial support. The opinions expressed in this paper are solely those of the authors and do not necessarily reп¬Ӯect the opinions of any sponsor. References 1. Adobe: PDF Reference, sixth edition, version 1.7 2. reference_research_study_list.title: Unrestricted adversarial examples
reference_research_study_list.abstract:
We introduce a two-player contest for evaluating the safety and robustness of
machine learning systems, with a large prize pool. Unlike most prior work in ML
robustness, which studies norm-constrained adversaries, we shift our focus to
unconstrained adversaries. Defenders submit machine learning models, and try to
achieve high accuracy and coverage on non-adversarial data while making no
confident mistakes on adversarial inputs. Attackers try to subvert defenses by
finding arbitrary unambiguous inputs where the model assigns an incorrect label
with high confidence. We propose a simple unambiguous dataset ("bird-or-
bicycle") to use as part of this contest. We hope this contest will help to
more comprehensively evaluate the worst-case adversarial risk of machine
learning models.
reference_research_study_list.full_text: Unrestricted Adversarial Examples Tom B. BrownвҲ— Nicholas CarliniвҲ— Chiyuan ZhangвҲ— Catherine OlssonвҲ— Paul ChristianoвҖ  Ian GoodfellowвҲ— Abstract We introduce a two-player contest for evaluating the safety and robustness of machine learning systems, with a large prize pool. Unlike most prior work in ML robustness, which studies norm-constrained adversaries, we shift our focus to unconstrained adversaries. Defenders submit machine learning models, and try to achieve high accuracy and coverage on non-adversarial data while making no conп¬Ғdent mistakes on adversarial inputs. Attackers try to subvert defenses by п¬Ғnding arbitrary unambiguous inputs where the model assigns an incorrect label with high conп¬Ғdence. We propose a simple unambiguous dataset ("bird-or- bicycleвҖқ) to use as part of this contest. We hope this contest will help to more comprehensively evaluate the worst-case adversarial risk of machine learning models. 1 Introduction Deep learning systems are superhuman at image classiп¬Ғcation [He et al., 2016], translation [Wu et al., 2016] and game playing [Mnih et al., 2013]. However, goodexpected performance can be misleading: deep learning systems still make surprising and egregious errors in the worst case [Szegedy et al., 2013]. For example, image classiп¬Ғers have been found to be vulnerable to a range of attacks: вҖў using optimization to make small pixel-wise modiп¬Ғcations to clean inputs [Szegedy et al., 2013]; вҖў applying ordinary spatial transformations to the input (such as translations and rotations on images) [Engstrom et al., 2017]; вҖў and performing simple guess-and-check (trying a variety of inputs that seem likely to be misclassiп¬Ғed) [Gilmer et al., 2018]. Even more troubling, these systems often report high conп¬Ғdence on blatant errors [Nguyen et al., 2015]. As a result, it is not currently possible to protect against these problems simply by trusting the system only when it is highly certain in its prediction. In safety-critical situations, conп¬Ғdent errors could be catastrophic (e.g., the vision system behind a self-driving car conп¬Ғdently believing there is no person in front of it when there is). Even in situations where peoplesвҖҷ lives are not under immediate risk, it is still be important for systems to know when they are uncertain.3 For instance, a home assistant might think that it has heard its owner ask to text a sensitive message to some unintended recipient. If the system is not conп¬Ғdent that it heard the recipient correctly, it could ask for conп¬Ғrmation, but if it is incorrectly conп¬Ғdent then it could send a potentially-sensitive message to someone who it was not intended to receive it ([Chokshi, 2018]). вҲ—Google Brain. Correspondence to: Tom B. Brown <tombrown@google.com>. вҖ OpenAI. 3Indeed, there is a whole sub-п¬Ғeld of machine learning for estimating conп¬Ғdence in predictions. For an introduction, see [Guo et al., 2017]. arXiv:1809.08352v1  [stat.ML]  22 Sep 2018Figure 1: Unrestricted adversarial examples Adversarial examples can be generated in various ways, including small modiп¬Ғcations to the input pixels, spatial transformations, or simple guess-and- check to п¬Ғnd misclassiп¬Ғed inputs. We believe it is important to design machine learning systems that never make conп¬Ғdent mistakes. 4 Such a guarantee would help protect not only against deliberate attacks by adversaries, but also against unintentional accidents. Is the goal of вҖңno conп¬Ғdent mistakesвҖқ even possible? Two aspects seem particularly challenging. First, current models consistently make conп¬Ғdent mistakes even when the model input has been only subtly changed. Second, even if a model is able to resist a certain attack, there is no guarantee that it will be robust to a different attack. For example, defending against an attacker who can make small modiп¬Ғcations to all pixels in an image does not protect against an adversary who can make small rotations to an image [Engstrom et al., 2017]. One might therefore conclude that no progress can be made towards the goal of robustness to arbitrary inputs, especially given the limited progress in the п¬Ғeld of adversarial examples over the last several years. We disagree. In this paper, we present two tools aimed at making this research direction tractable: 1.1 Contest Overview We construct a two-class unambiguous dataset where all images are either an unambiguous image of a bird or an unambiguous image of a bicycle. We describe this dataset in Section 2. Defenders build models that take an image as input, and must return either bird, bicycle or abstain.5 For inputs upon which the model does not abstain, the model must never make a mistake. Every few weeks, we вҖңstakeвҖқ a set of defenses that we believe have a chance of succeeding, and award a small attacker prize to the п¬Ғrst team who can break the defense. To break a defense, an attacker must submit an unambiguous image of either a bird or bicycle that the model labels incorrectly (as decided 4Note that it may only be possible to achieve this on some limited set of tasks which are unambiguous. 5For an introduction to abstaining classiп¬Ғers and the selective classiп¬Ғcation paradigm, see Geifman and El-Yaniv [2017] 2Figure 2: Examples of candidate images for the bird-or-bicycle task. The left two images are valid images for the bird-or-bicycle dataset. The right two images are invalid and not included in the dataset. by an ensemble of human judges). Models are allowed to abstain (by returning low-conп¬Ғdence predictions) on any adversarial input6, but even one conп¬Ғdent error will result in a broken defense. The п¬Ғrst defense to remain un-broken for a 90-day period will win a large defender prize. We will run a one-sided warm-up to the contest to test our infrastructure and to ensure that there are defenses that can soundly defeat our п¬Ғxed attacks. This warm-up is discussed in Section 3. 2 The Unambiguous Bird or Bicycle Task We propose a two-way classiп¬Ғcation task for the purposes of this challenge: вҖңIs this an unambiguous picture of a bird, a bicycle, or is it (ambiguous / not obvious)?вҖқ. We call this the "bird-or-bicycle" task. We selected this task because (a) given an arbitrary image, humans can robustly determine the correct label, (b) modern machine learning techniques can also achieve perfect accuracy on an IID test set, but (c) the task is not so simplistic that it is possible to solve without machine learning (see Appendix F for more detail). We collect our images from the OpenImages [Krasin et al., 2017] dataset; however, we intend the bird-or-bicycle task to be well-deп¬Ғned on arbitrary images. To obtain the ground truth label for an image, we ask multiple human taskers to label the image as either a bird, a bicycle, or as being ambiguous / not obvious. Any images where taskers did not unanimously agree the image is obviously either a bird or bicycle are excluded from the dataset. We provide some examples in Fig. 2 and full instructions provided to the taskers are shown in Appendix G.1. 3 Phase One of the Challenge: The Warm-up Before running the full unrestricted adversarial examples contest, we would like to ensure that it is possible to solve this simple, unambiguous task for a set of state-of-the-art п¬Ғxed attacks. We have selected three attacks for this warm-up, including two black-box pixel-wise optimization attacks [Uesato et al., 2018, Brendel et al., 2017] and a spatial attack [Engstrom et al., 2017]. Because these are п¬Ғxed and restricted attacks, we expect that there will be many вҖңuninteresting defensesвҖқ that are tailored to perform well against these speciп¬Ғc п¬Ғxed attacks, but will not generalize to other attacks.7 Further, we expect that many defenses will unintentionally over-п¬Ғt against these speciп¬Ғc attacks and not generalize to other attacks. This further serves to motivate the full unrestricted two-player challenge. After the research community can conclusively beat these п¬Ғxed attacks, we will begin the two-sided contest phase. Interested parties can learn more about the warm-up on github. 6 Models are prevented from always abstaining through the abstaining mechanism described in Section 4.2 7We give an example of one such uninteresting defense in Appendix E. 34 Phase Two of the Challenge: The Contest 4.1 Differences from prior adversarial examples contests We propose a two-sided adversarial examples contest. Unlike prior contests (e.g., the 2017 NIPS Adversarial Examples Contest [Kurakin et al., 2018]), our contest (a) is ongoing with no п¬Ғxed end-date, (b) is fully white-box, and (c) allows completely unrestricted attacks. Our contest is ongoing, rather than п¬Ғxed length. We release new defenses that have been pro- posed every few weeks, and release attacks at the end of the week that they are submitted. The rapid release cycle allows many rounds of back-and-forth between attackers and defenders, which more closely resembles the dynamics of systems deployed in the real world, and also allows contestants to individually verify that the submitted adversarial images are in fact unambiguous. When assigning the п¬Ғnal defense prize, the organizers will re-evaluate all the attack attempts to verify that they have all failed. Only when a defense has remained undefeated for several months will we award the п¬Ғnal defense prize. Our contest is fully white-box, and does not rely on oblivious adversaries. Because the goal of this contest is to п¬Ғnd models that make no conп¬Ғdent mistakes (and not just for which it is hard for an attacker to п¬Ғnd mistakes) we require that all defenses are open-source and available to the attackers. Thus, attackers have the option to craft adversarial examples that target speciп¬Ғc models. This provides a realistic threat model for evaluating defenses, as recommended by Athalye et al. [2018]. Our contest is unrestricted, and not constrained by any norm-ball. As a consequence of not restricting attacks to a neighborhood around a pre-labeled test point, each image must be evaluated by human judges to obtain a true class. An attack is successful if all the judges agree that it is unambiguously class A and the model mistakenly classiп¬Ғes it as class B with high conп¬Ғdence (instead of class Aor abstaining). 4.2 Abstaining mechanism for the contest In the contest, defenders attempt to create a model that never makes a conп¬Ғdent mistake. Although the task is binary (A vs. B), models are allowed to output three labels: вҖңClass AвҖқ, вҖңClass BвҖқ, or вҖңAbstainвҖқ. We deп¬Ғne a conп¬Ғdent mistake as the model assigning one class label, when a unanimous ensemble of human taskers give the other class label. Abstaining on all adversarial inputs is acceptable, and we allow defenses to choose when to abstain using any mechanism they desire. To prevent models from abstaining on all inputs, models must reach 80% accuracy on a private eligibility dataset of clean bird-or-bicycle images. We reserve the right to perform additional tests to ensure defenders do not over-п¬Ғt against our private held-out data [Markoff, 2015]. 4.3 The contest mechanics are open-sourced We have open-sourced our proposed contest mechanics and are soliciting feedback on them before launching the contest. Readers can п¬Ғnd the most up-to-date contest proposal on GitHub, and we also include a snapshot of the contest proposal in Appendices A, B & C. 5 Conclusion We have presented the unrestricted adversarial examples challenge, consisting of a high-dimensional image classiп¬Ғcation task, and an ongoing contest to evaluate defenses. We hope that this challenge will encourage more researchers to investigate the difп¬Ғcult and conspicuously open problem of building robust classiп¬Ғers. We invite interested parties to test their defenses in the warm-up, and to give feedback on the detailed contest mechanics in our open-source repository. 4Acknowledgments We thank the Hyderabad team for helping to create the bird-or-bicycle dataset. We thank Alexey Kurakin for help in assembling early versions of the dataset.
