reference_research_study_list.title: Certified adversarial robustness via randomized smoothing
reference_research_study_list.abstract:
We show how to turn any classifier that classifies well under Gaussian noise
into a new classifier that is certifiably robust to adversarial perturbations
under the $\ell_2$ norm. This "randomized smoothing" technique has been
proposed recently in the literature, but existing guarantees are loose. We
prove a tight robustness guarantee in $\ell_2$ norm for smoothing with Gaussian
noise. We use randomized smoothing to obtain an ImageNet classifier with e.g. a
certified top-1 accuracy of 49% under adversarial perturbations with $\ell_2$
norm less than 0.5 (=127/255). No certified defense has been shown feasible on
ImageNet except for smoothing. On smaller-scale datasets where competing
approaches to certified $\ell_2$ robustness are viable, smoothing delivers
higher certified accuracies. Our strong empirical results suggest that
randomized smoothing is a promising direction for future research into
adversarially robust classification. Code and models are available at
http://github.com/locuslab/smoothing.
reference_research_study_list.full_text: Certiп¬Ғed Adversarial Robustness via Randomized Smoothing Jeremy Cohen 1 Elan Rosenfeld 1 J. Zico Kolter1 2 Abstract We show how to turn any classiп¬Ғer that classi- п¬Ғes well under Gaussian noise into a new classi- п¬Ғer that is certiп¬Ғably robust to adversarial per- turbations under the в„“2 norm. This вҖңrandom- ized smoothingвҖқ technique has been proposed re- cently in the literature, but existing guarantees are loose. We prove a tight robustness guarantee in в„“2 norm for smoothing with Gaussian noise. We use randomized smoothing to obtain an ImageNet classiп¬Ғer with e.g. a certiп¬Ғed top-1 accuracy of 49% under adversarial perturbations with в„“2 norm less than 0.5 (=127/255). No certiп¬Ғed defense has been shown feasible on ImageNet except for smoothing. On smaller-scale datasets where com- peting approaches to certiп¬Ғed в„“2 robustness are viable, smoothing delivers higher certiп¬Ғed accu- racies. Our strong empirical results suggest that randomized smoothing is a promising direction for future research into adversarially robust classi- п¬Ғcation. Code and models are available at http: //github.com/locuslab/smoothing. 1. Introduction Modern image classiп¬Ғers achieve high accuracy on i.i.d. test sets but are not robust to small, adversarially-chosen perturbations of their inputs (Szegedy et al., 2014; Biggio et al., 2013). Given an image xcorrectly classiп¬Ғed by, say, a neural network, an adversary can usually engineer an ad- versarial perturbation Оҙso small that x+ Оҙlooks just like xto the human eye, yet the network classiп¬Ғes x+ Оҙ as a different, incorrect class. Many works have proposed heuris- tic methods for training classiп¬Ғers intended to be robust to adversarial perturbations. However, most of these heuristics have been subsequently shown to fail against suitably pow- erful adversaries (Carlini & Wagner, 2017; Athalye et al., 2018; Uesato et al., 2018). In response, a line of work on 1Carnegie Mellon University 2Bosch Center for AI. Correspon- dence to: Jeremy Cohen <jeremycohen@cmu.edu>. Proceedings of the 36 th International Conference on Machine Learning, Long Beach, California, PMLR 97, 2019. Copyright 2019 by the author(s). x pA pB Figure 1.Evaluating the smoothed classiп¬Ғer at an input x. Left: the decision regions of the base classiп¬Ғer f are drawn in differ- ent colors. The dotted lines are the level sets of the distribution N(x,Пғ2I). Right: the distribution f(N(x,Пғ2I)). As discussed below, pA is a lower bound on the probability of the top class and pB is an upper bound on the probability of each other class. Here, g(x) is вҖңblue.вҖқ . certiп¬Ғable robustness studies classiп¬Ғers whose prediction at any point xis veriп¬Ғably constant within some set around x (Wong & Kolter, 2018; Raghunathan et al., 2018a, e.g.). In most of these works, the robust classiп¬Ғer takes the form of a neural network. Unfortunately, all existing approaches for certifying the robustness of neural networks have trouble scaling to networks that are large and expressive enough to solve problems like ImageNet. One workaround is to look for robust classiп¬Ғers that are not neural networks. Recently, two papers (Lecuyer et al., 2019; Li et al., 2018) showed that an operation we callrandomized smoothing1 can transform any arbitrary base classiп¬Ғerfinto a new вҖңsmoothed classiп¬ҒerвҖқ g that is certiп¬Ғably robust in в„“2 norm. Let f be an arbitrary classiп¬Ғer which maps inputs Rd to classes Y. For any input x, the smoothed classiп¬ҒerвҖҷs prediction g(x) is deп¬Ғned to be the class which f is most likely to classify the random variable N(x,Пғ2I) as. That is, g(x) returns the most probable prediction by f of random Gaussian corruptions of x. If the base classiп¬Ғer f is most likely to classify N(x,Пғ2I) as xвҖҷs correct class, then the smoothed classiп¬Ғer gwill be 1Smoothing was proposed under the name вҖңPixelDPвҖқ (for dif- ferential privacy). We use a different name since our improved analysis does not involve differential privacy. arXiv:1902.02918v2  [cs.LG]  15 Jun 2019Certiп¬Ғed Adversarial Robustness via Randomized Smoothing correct at x. But the smoothed classiп¬Ғer gwill also possess a desirable property that the base classiп¬Ғer may lack: one can verify that gвҖҷs prediction is constant within an в„“2 ball around any input x, simply by estimating the probabilities with which f classiп¬Ғes N(x,Пғ2I) as each class. The higher the probability with which f classiп¬Ғes N(x,Пғ2I) as the most probable class, the larger the в„“2 radius around x in which gprovably returns that class. Lecuyer et al. (2019) proposed randomized smoothing as a provable adversarial defense, and used it to train the п¬Ғrst certiп¬Ғably robust classiп¬Ғer for ImageNet. Subsequently, Li et al. (2018) proved a stronger robustness guarantee. How- ever, both of these guarantees are loose, in the sense that the smoothed classiп¬Ғer g is provably always more robust than the guarantee indicates. In this paper, we prove the п¬Ғrst tight robustness guarantee for randomized smoothing. Our analysis reveals that smoothing with Gaussian noise naturally induces certiп¬Ғable robustness under the в„“2 norm. We suspect that other, as-yet-unknown noise distributions might induce robustness to other perturbation sets such as general в„“p norm balls. Randomized smoothing has one major drawback. If f is a neural network, it is not possible to exactly compute the probabilities with which f classiп¬Ғes N(x,Пғ2I) as each class. Therefore, it is not possible to exactly evaluate gвҖҷs prediction at any input x, or to exactly compute the radius in which this prediction is certiп¬Ғably robust. Instead, we present Monte Carlo algorithms for both tasks that are guar- anteed to succeed with arbitrarily high probability. Despite this drawback, randomized smoothing enjoys sev- eral compelling advantages over other certiп¬Ғably robust classiп¬Ғers proposed in the literature: it makes no assump- tions about the base classiп¬ҒerвҖҷs architecture, it is simple to implement and understand, and, most importantly, it per- mits the use of arbitrarily large neural networks as the base classiп¬Ғer. In contrast, other certiп¬Ғed defenses do not cur- Table 1.Approximate certiп¬Ғed accuracy on ImageNet. Each row shows a radius r, the best hyperparameter Пғfor that radius, the approximate certiп¬Ғed accuracy at radius rof the corresponding smoothed classiп¬Ғer, and the standard accuracy of the corresponding smoothed classiп¬Ғer. To give a sense of scale, a perturbation with в„“2 radius 1.0 could change one pixel by 255, ten pixels by 80, 100 pixels by 25, or 1000 pixels by 8. Random guessing on ImageNet would attain 0.1% accuracy. в„“2 RADIUS BEST Пғ CERT. ACC (%) S TD. ACC(%) 0.5 0.25 49 67 1.0 0.50 37 57 2.0 0.50 19 57 3.0 1.00 12 44 Figure 2.The smoothed classiп¬ҒerвҖҷs prediction at an inputx(left) is deп¬Ғned as the most likely prediction by the base classiп¬Ғer on random Gaussian corruptions of x(right; Пғ= 0.5). Note that this Gaussian noise is much larger in magnitude than the adversarial perturbations to which gis provably robust. One interpretation of randomized smoothing is that these large random perturbations вҖңdrown outвҖқ small adversarial perturbations. rently scale to large networks. Indeed, smoothing is the only certiп¬Ғed adversarial defense which has been shown feasible on the full-resolution ImageNet classiп¬Ғcation task. We use randomized smoothing to train state-of-the-art certi- п¬Ғably в„“2-robust ImageNet classiп¬Ғers; for example, one of them achieves 49% provable top-1 accuracy under adver- sarial perturbations with в„“2 norm less than 127/255 (Table 1). We also demonstrate that on smaller-scale datasets like CIFAR-10 and SHVN, where competing approaches to cer- tiп¬Ғed в„“2 robustness are feasible, randomized smoothing can deliver better certiп¬Ғed accuracies, both because it enables the use of larger networks and because it does not constrain the expressivity of the base classiп¬Ғer. 2. Related Work Many works have proposed classiп¬Ғers intended to be ro- bust to adversarial perturbations. These approaches can be broadly divided into empirical defenses, which empiri- cally seem robust to known adversarial attacks, andcertiп¬Ғed defenses, which are provably robust to certain kinds of ad- versarial perturbations. Empirical defenses The most successful empirical de- fense to date is adversarial training (Goodfellow et al., 2015; Kurakin et al., 2017; Madry et al., 2018), in which adversarial examples are found during training (often using projected gradient descent) and added to the training set. Unfortunately, it is typically impossible to tell whether a prediction by an empirically robust classiп¬Ғer is truly robust to adversarial perturbations; the most that can be said is that a speciп¬Ғc attack was unable to п¬Ғnd any. In fact, many heuris- tic defenses proposed in the literature were later вҖңbrokenвҖқ by stronger adversaries (Carlini & Wagner, 2017; Athalye et al., 2018; Uesato et al., 2018; Athalye & Carlini, 2018).Certiп¬Ғed Adversarial Robustness via Randomized Smoothing Aiming to escape this cat-and-mouse game, a growing body of work has focused on defenses with formal guarantees. Certiп¬Ғed defenses A classiп¬Ғer is said to be certiп¬Ғably ro- bust if for any input x, one can easily obtain a guarantee that the classiп¬ҒerвҖҷs prediction is constant within some set around x, often an в„“2 or в„“вҲһball. In most work in this area, the certiп¬Ғably robust classiп¬Ғer is a neural network. Some works propose algorithms for certifying the robustness of generi- cally trained networks, while others (Wong & Kolter, 2018; Raghunathan et al., 2018a) propose both a robust training method and a complementary certiп¬Ғcation mechanism. Certiп¬Ғcation methods are either exact (a.k.a вҖңcompleteвҖқ) or conservative (a.k.a вҖңsound but incompleteвҖқ). In the context of в„“p norm-bounded perturbations, exact methods take a classiп¬Ғer g, input x, and radius r, and report whether or not there exists a perturbation Оҙwithin вҲҘОҙвҲҘвүӨ rfor which g(x) Мё= g(x+ Оҙ). In contrast, conservative methods either certify that no such perturbation exists or decline to make a certiп¬Ғcation; they may decline even when it is true that no such perturbation exists. Exact methods are usually based on Satisп¬Ғability Modulo Theories (Katz et al., 2017; Carlini et al., 2017; Ehlers, 2017; Huang et al., 2017) or mixed integer linear programming (Cheng et al., 2017; Lomuscio & Maganti, 2017; Dutta et al., 2017; Fischetti & Jo, 2018; Bunel et al., 2018). Unfortunately, no exact methods have been shown to scale beyond moderate-sized (100,000 acti- vations) networks (Tjeng et al., 2019), and networks of that size can only be veriп¬Ғed when they are trained in a manner that impairs their expressivity. Conservative certiп¬Ғcation is more scalable. Some conser- vative methods bound the global Lipschitz constant of the neural network (Gouk et al., 2018; Tsuzuku et al., 2018; Anil et al., 2019; Cisse et al., 2017), but these approaches tend to be very loose on expressive networks. Others mea- sure the local smoothness of the network in the vicinity of a particular input x. In theory, one could obtain a robustness guarantee via an upper bound on the local Lipschitz con- stant of the network (Hein & Andriushchenko, 2017), but computing this quantity is intractable for general neural net- works. Instead, a panoply of practical solutions have been proposed in the literature (Wong & Kolter, 2018; Wang et al., 2018a;b; Raghunathan et al., 2018a;b; Wong et al., 2018; Dvijotham et al., 2018b;a; Croce et al., 2019; Gehr et al., 2018; Mirman et al., 2018; Singh et al., 2018; Gowal et al., 2018; Weng et al., 2018a; Zhang et al., 2018). Two themes stand out. Some approaches cast veriп¬Ғcation as an opti- mization problem and import tools such as relaxation and duality from the optimization literature to provide conserva- tive guarantees (Wong & Kolter, 2018; Wong et al., 2018; Raghunathan et al., 2018a;b; Dvijotham et al., 2018b;a). Others step through the network layer by layer, maintaining at each layer an outer approximation of the set of activations reachable by a perturbed input (Mirman et al., 2018; Singh et al., 2018; Gowal et al., 2018; Weng et al., 2018a; Zhang et al., 2018). None of these local certiп¬Ғcation methods have been shown to be feasible on networks that are large and expressive enough to solve modern machine learning prob- lems like the ImageNet classiп¬Ғcation task. Also, all either assume speciп¬Ғc network architectures (e.g. ReLU activa- tions or a layered feedforward structure) or require extensive customization for new network architectures. Related work involving noise Prior works have proposed using a networkвҖҷs robustness to Gaussian noise as a proxy for its robustness to adversarial perturbations (Weng et al., 2018b; Ford et al., 2019), and have suggested that Gaussian data augmentation could supplement or replace adversar- ial training (Zantedeschi et al., 2017; Kannan et al., 2018). Smilkov et al. (2017) observed that averaging a classiп¬ҒerвҖҷs input gradients over Gaussian corruptions of an image yields very interpretable saliency maps. The robustness of neural networks to random noise has been analyzed both theo- retically (Fawzi et al., 2016; Franceschi et al., 2018) and empirically (Dodge & Karam, 2017). Finally, Webb et al. (2019) proposed a statistical technique for estimating the noise robustness of a classiп¬Ғer more efп¬Ғciently than naive Monte Carlo simulation; we did not use this technique since it appears to lack formal high-probability guarantees. While these works hypothesized relationships between a neural net- workвҖҷs robustness to random noise andthe same networkвҖҷs robustness to adversarial perturbations, randomized smooth- ing instead uses a classiп¬ҒerвҖҷs robustness to random noiseto create a new classiп¬Ғer robust to adversarial perturbations. Randomized smoothing Randomized smoothing has been studied previously for adversarial robustness. Sev- eral works (Liu et al., 2018; Cao & Gong, 2017) proposed similar techniques as heuristic defenses, but did not prove any guarantees. Lecuyer et al. (2019) used inequalities from the differential privacy literature to prove an в„“2 and в„“1 robustness guarantee for smoothing with Gaussian and Laplace noise, respectively. Subsequently, Li et al. (2018) used tools from information theory to prove a strongerв„“2 ro- bustness guarantee for Gaussian noise. However, all of these robustness guarantees are loose. In contrast, we prove a tight robustness guarantee in в„“2 norm for randomized smoothing with Gaussian noise. 3. Randomized smoothing Consider a classiп¬Ғcation problem from Rd to classes Y. As discussed above, randomized smoothing is a method for constructing a new, вҖңsmoothedвҖқ classiп¬Ғergfrom an arbitrary base classiп¬Ғer f. When queried at x, the smoothed classiп¬Ғer greturns whichever class the base classiп¬Ғer f is most likelyCertiп¬Ғed Adversarial Robustness via Randomized Smoothing to return when xis perturbed by isotropic Gaussian noise: g(x) = arg max cвҲҲY P(f(x+ Оө) = c) (1) where ОөвҲјN(0,Пғ2I) An equivalent deп¬Ғnition is that g(x) returns the class c whose pre-image {xвҖІ вҲҲRd : f(xвҖІ) = c}has the largest probability measure under the distribution N(x,Пғ2I). The noise level Пғis a hyperparameter of the smoothed classiп¬Ғer gwhich controls a robustness/accuracy tradeoff; it does not change with the input x. We leave undeп¬Ғned the behavior of gwhen the argmax is not unique. We will п¬Ғrst present our robustness guarantee for the smoothed classiп¬Ғer g. Then, since it is not possible to exactly evaluate the prediction of gat xor to certify the ro- bustness of garound x, we will give Monte Carlo algorithms for both tasks that succeed with arbitrarily high probability. 3.1. Robustness guarantee Suppose that when the base classiп¬Ғerfclassiп¬Ғes N(x,Пғ2I), the most probable class cA is returned with probability pA, and the вҖңrunner-upвҖқ class is returned with probability pB. Our main result is that smoothed classiп¬Ғergis robust around xwithin the в„“2 radius R= Пғ 2 (ОҰвҲ’1(pA)вҲ’ОҰвҲ’1(pB)), where ОҰвҲ’1 is the inverse of the standard Gaussian CDF. This result also holds if we replace pA with a lower bound pA and we replace pB with an upper bound pB. Theorem 1. Let f : Rd вҶ’ Ybe any deterministic or random function, and let ОөвҲјN(0,Пғ2I). Let gbe deп¬Ғned as in (1). Suppose cA вҲҲY and pA,pB вҲҲ[0,1] satisfy: P(f(x+ Оө) = cA) вүҘpA вүҘpB вүҘmax cМё=cA P(f(x+ Оө) = c) (2) Then g(x+ Оҙ) = cA for all вҲҘОҙвҲҘ2 <R, where R= Пғ 2 (ОҰвҲ’1(pA) вҲ’ОҰвҲ’1(pB)) (3) We now make several observations about Theorem 12 вҖў Theorem 1 assumes nothing about f. This is crucial since it is unclear which well-behavedness assump- tions, if any, are satisп¬Ғed by modern deep architectures. вҖў The certiп¬Ғed radius Ris large when: (1) the noise level Пғis high, (2) the probability of the top class cA is high, and (3) the probability of each other class is low. 2After the dissemination of this work, a more general result was published in Levine et al. (2019); Salman et al. (2019): if h: Rd вҶ’[0,1] is a function and ЛҶhis the вҖңsmoothedвҖқ versionЛҶh(x) = EОөвҲјN(0,Пғ2I)[h(x+ Оө)], then the function x вҶҰвҶ’ОҰвҲ’1(ЛҶh(x)) is 1/Пғ-Lipschitz. Theorem 1 can be proved by applying this result to the functions fc(x) = 1[f(x) = c] for each class c. вҖў The certiп¬Ғed radius R goes to вҲһas pA вҶ’ 1 and pB вҶ’0. This should sound reasonable: the Gaussian distribution is supported on all of Rd, so the only way that f(x+ Оө) = cA with probability 1 is if f = cA almost everywhere. Both Lecuyer et al. (2019) and Li et al. (2018) proved в„“2 robustness guarantees for the same setting as Theorem 1, but with different, smaller expressions for the certiп¬Ғed radius. However, our в„“2 robustness guarantee is tight: if (2) is all that is known about f, then it is impossible to certify an в„“2 ball with radius larger than R. In fact, it is impossible to certify any superset of the в„“2 ball with radius R: Theorem 2. Assume pA + pB вүӨ1. For any perturbation Оҙwith вҲҘОҙвҲҘ2 >R, there exists a base classiп¬Ғer f consistent with the class probabilities (2) for which g(x+ Оҙ) Мё= cA. Theorem 2 shows that Gaussian smoothing naturally in- duces в„“2 robustness: if we make no assumptions on the base classiп¬Ғer beyond the class probabilities (2), then the set of perturbations to which a Gaussian-smoothed classiп¬Ғer is provably robust is exactly an в„“2 ball. The complete proofs of Theorems 1 and 2 are in Appendix A. We now sketch the proofs in the special case when there are only two classes. Theorem 1 (binary case). Suppose pA вҲҲ(1 2 ,1] satisп¬Ғes P(f(x+ Оө) = cA) вүҘpA. Then g(x+ Оҙ) = cA for all вҲҘОҙвҲҘ2 <ПғОҰвҲ’1(pA). Proof sketch. Fix a perturbation Оҙ вҲҲ Rd. To guarantee that g(x+ Оҙ) = cA, we need to show that f classiп¬Ғes the translated Gaussian N(x+ Оҙ,Пғ2I) as cA with probability > 1 2 . However, all we know about f is that f classiп¬Ғes N(x,Пғ2I) as cA with probability вүҘpA. This raises the question: out of all possible base classiп¬Ғers f which classify N(x,Пғ2I) as cA with probability вүҘ pA, which one fвҲ— classiп¬Ғes N(x+Оҙ,Пғ2I) as cAwith the smallest probability? One can show using an argument similar to the Neyman- Pearson lemma (Neyman & Pearson, 1933) that this вҖңworst- caseвҖқ fвҲ—is a linear classiп¬Ғer whose decision boundary is normal to the perturbation Оҙ(Figure 3): fвҲ—(xвҖІ) = { cA if ОҙT(xвҖІвҲ’x) вүӨПғвҲҘОҙвҲҘ2ОҰвҲ’1(pA) cB otherwise (4) This вҖңworst-caseвҖқ fвҲ—classiп¬Ғes N(x+ Оҙ,Пғ2I) as cA with probability ОҰ ( ОҰвҲ’1(pA) вҲ’вҲҘОҙвҲҘ2 Пғ ) . Therefore, to ensure that even the вҖңworst-caseвҖқfвҲ—classiп¬Ғes N(x+Оҙ,Пғ2I) as cAwith probability > 1 2 , we solve for those Оҙfor which ОҰ ( ОҰвҲ’1(pA) вҲ’вҲҘОҙвҲҘ2 Пғ ) > 1 2 which is equivalent to the conditionвҲҘОҙвҲҘ2 <ПғОҰвҲ’1(pA).Certiп¬Ғed Adversarial Robustness via Randomized Smoothing x+ Оҙ x x+ Оҙ x Figure 3.Illustration of fвҲ— in two dimensions. The concentric circles are the density contours of N(x,Пғ2I) and N(x+ Оҙ,Пғ2I). Out of all base classiп¬Ғers f which classify N(x,Пғ2I) as cA (blue) with probability вүҘpA, such as both classiп¬Ғers depicted above, the вҖңworst-caseвҖқ fвҲ—вҖ” the one which classiп¬Ғes N(x+ Оҙ,Пғ2I) as cA with minimal probability вҖ” is depicted on the right: a linear classiп¬Ғer with decision boundary normal to the perturbation Оҙ. Theorem 2 is a simple consequence: for any Оҙwith вҲҘОҙвҲҘ2 > R, the base classiп¬Ғer fвҲ—deп¬Ғned in (4) is consistent with (2); yet if fвҲ—is the base classiп¬Ғer, then g(x+ Оҙ) = cB. Figure 5 (left) plots our в„“2 robustness guarantee against the guarantees derived in prior work. Observe that our R is much larger than that of Lecuyer et al. (2019) and moderately larger than that of Li et al. (2018). Appendix I derives the other two guarantees using this paperвҖҷs notation. Linear base classiп¬Ғer A two-class linear classiп¬Ғer f(x) = sign(wTx + b) is already certiп¬Ғable: the dis- tance from any input xto the decision boundary is |wTx+ b|/вҲҘwвҲҘ2, and no perturbation Оҙwith в„“2 norm less than this distance can possibly change fвҖҷs prediction. In Appendix B we show that if f is linear, then the smoothed classiп¬Ғer gis identical to the base classiп¬Ғerf. Moreover, we show that our bound (3) will certify the true robust radius |wTx+ b|/вҲҘwвҲҘ, rather than a smaller, overconservative radius. Therefore, when f is linear, there always exists a perturbation Оҙ just beyond the certiп¬Ғed radius which changes gвҖҷs prediction. Noise level can scale with image resolution Since our expression (3) for the certiп¬Ғed radius does not depend ex- plicitly on the data dimension d, one might worry that ran- domized smoothing is less effective for images of higher resolution вҖ” certifying a п¬Ғxed в„“2 radius is вҖңless impressiveвҖқ for, say, a 224 Г—224 image than for a 56 Г—56 image. How- ever, as illustrated by Figure 4, images in higher resolution can tolerate higher levels Пғof isotropic Gaussian noise be- fore their class-distinguishing content gets destroyed. As a consequence, in high resolution, smoothing can be per- formed with a larger Пғ, leading to larger certiп¬Ғed radii. See Appendix G for a more rigorous version of this argument. 3.2. Practical algorithms We now present practical Monte Carlo algorithms for eval- uating g(x) and certifying the robustness of g around x. More details can be found in Appendix C. 3.2.1. P REDICTION Evaluating the smoothed classiп¬ҒerвҖҷs prediction g(x) re- quires identifying the class cA with maximal weight in the categorical distribution f(x+ Оө). The procedure described in pseudocode as PREDICT draws nsamples of f(x+ Оө) by running nnoise-corrupted copies of xthrough the base classiп¬Ғer. Let ЛҶcA be the class which appeared the largest number of times. If ЛҶcA appeared much more often than any other class, then PREDICT returns ЛҶcA. Otherwise, it abstains from making a prediction. We use the hypothesis test from Hung & Fithian (2019) to calibrate the abstention threshold so as to bound by Оұthe probability of returning an incorrect answer. PREDICT satisп¬Ғes the following guarantee: Proposition 1. With probability at least 1 вҲ’Оұ over the randomness in PREDICT , PREDICT will either abstain or return g(x). (Equivalently: the probability that PREDICT returns a class other than g(x) is at most Оұ.) The function SAMPLE UNDER NOISE (f, x, num, Пғ) in the pseudocode draws num samples of noise, Оө1 ...Оө num вҲј N(0,Пғ2I), runs each x+ Оөi through the base classiп¬Ғer f, and returns a vector of class counts. BINOM PVALUE (nA, nA+ nB, p) returns the p-value of the two-sided hypothesis test that nA вҲјBinomial(nA + nB,p). Even if the true smoothed classiп¬Ғer gis robust at radius R, PREDICT will be vulnerable in a certain sense to adversarial perturbations with в„“2 norm slightly less than R. By engi- neering a perturbation Оҙfor which f(x+ Оҙ+ Оө) puts mass just over 1 2 on class cA and mass just under 1 2 on class cB, an adversary can force PREDICT to abstain at a high rate. If this scenario is of concern, a variant of Theorem 1 could be proved to certify a radius in which P(f(x+ Оҙ+ Оө) = cA) is larger by some margin than maxcМё=cA P(f(x+ Оҙ+ Оө) = c). 3.2.2. Doing all three of these at the same time in a sta- tistically correct manner requires some care. One simple solution is presented in pseudocode as CERTIFY : п¬Ғrst, use a small number of samples from f(x+ Оө) to take a guess at cA; then use a larger number of samples to estimate pA; then simply take pB = 1 вҲ’pA. Training the base classiп¬Ғer Theorem 1 holds regardless of how the base classiп¬Ғer f is trained. However, in order for gto classify the labeled ex- ample (x,c) correctly and robustly, f needs to consistently classify N(x,Пғ2I) as c. In high dimension, the Gaussian distribution N(x,Пғ2I) places almost no mass near its mode x. As a consequence, when Пғis moderately high, the distri- bution of natural images has virtually disjoint support from the distribution of natural images corrupted by N(0,Пғ2I); see Figure 2 for a visual demonstration. Therefore, if the base classiп¬Ғer f is trained via standard supervised learning on the data distribution, it will see no noisy images during training, and hence will not necessarily learn to classify N(x,Пғ2I) with xвҖҷs true label. Therefore, in this paper we follow Lecuyer et al. (2019) and train the base classiп¬Ғer with Gaussian data augmentation at variance Пғ2. A justiп¬Ғca- tion for this procedure is provided in Appendix F. However, we suspect that there may be room to improve upon this training scheme, perhaps by training the base classiп¬Ғer so as to maximize the smoothed classiп¬ҒerвҖҷs certiп¬Ғed accuracy at some tunable radius r. 4. Experiments In adversarially robust classiп¬Ғcation, one metric of interest is the certiп¬Ғed test set accuracy at radius r, deп¬Ғned as the fraction of the test set whichgclassiп¬Ғes correctly with a pre- diction that is certiп¬Ғably robust within an в„“2 ball of radius r. However, if gis a randomized smoothing classiп¬Ғer, comput- ing this quantity exactly is not possible, so we instead report the approximate certiп¬Ғed test set accuracy, deп¬Ғned as the fraction of the test set which CERTIFY classiп¬Ғes correctly (without abstaining) and certiп¬Ғes robust with a radiusRвүҘr. Comparison to baselines We compared randomized smoothing to three baseline approaches for certiп¬Ғed в„“2 ro- bustness: the duality approach from Wong et al. (2018), the Lipschitz approach from Tsuzuku et al. (2018), and the approach from Weng et al. (2018a); Zhang et al. (2018). The strongest baseline was Wong et al. (2018); we defer the comparison to the other two baselines to Appendix H. In Figure 7, we compare the largest publicly released model from Wong et al. (2018), a small resnet, to two randomized smoothing classiп¬Ғers: one which used the same small resnet architecture for its base classiп¬Ғer, and one which used a larger 110-layer resnet for its base classiп¬Ғer. First, observe that smoothing with the large 110-layer resnet substantially outperforms the baseline (across all hyperparameter set- tings) at all radii. Second, observe that smoothing with the small resnet also outperformed the method of Wong et al. (2018) at all but the smallest radii. We attribute this latter re- sult to the fact that neural networks trained using the method of Wong et al. (2018) are вҖңtypically overregularized to the point that many п¬Ғlters/weights become identically zero,вҖқ per that paper. In contrast, the base classiп¬Ғer in randomized smoothing is a fully expressive neural network. Prediction It is computationally expensive to certify the robustness of g around a point x, since the value of nin CERTIFY must be very large. However, it is far cheaper to evaluate g at x using PREDICT , since n can be small. For example, when we ran PREDICT on ImageNet ( Пғ = 0.25) using n = 100, making each prediction only took 0.15 seconds, and we attained a top-1 test accuracy of 65% (Appendix E). As discussed earlier, an adversary can potentially forcePRE- DICT to abstain with high probability. However, it is rela- tively rare for PREDICT to abstain on the actual data dis- tribution. On ImageNet (Пғ= 0.25), PREDICT with failure probability Оұ= 0.001 abstained 12% of the time when n= 100, 4% when n= 1000, and 1% when n= 10,000. Empirical tightness of bound When f is linear, there al- ways exists a class-changing perturbation just beyond the certiп¬Ғed radius. Since neural networks are not linear, we em- pirically assessed the tightness of our bound by subjecting an ImageNet smoothed classiп¬Ғer (Пғ= 0.25) to a projected gradient descent-style adversarial attack (Appendix J.3). For each example, we ran CERTIFY with Оұ= 0.01, and, if the example was correctly classiп¬Ғed and certiп¬Ғed robust at ra- dius R, we tried п¬Ғnding an adversarial example for gwithin radius 1.5Rand within radius 2R. We succeeded 17% of the time at radius 1.5Rand 53% of the time at radius 2R. 5. Conclusion Theorem 2 establishes that smoothing with Gaussian noise naturally confers adversarial robustness in в„“2 norm: if we have no knowledge about the base classiп¬Ғer beyond the dis- tribution of f(x+ Оө), then the set of perturbations to which the smoothed classiп¬Ғer is provably robust is precisely an в„“2 ball. We suspect that smoothing with other noise distribu- tions may lead to similarly natural robustness guarantees for other perturbation sets such as general в„“p norm balls. Our strong empirical results suggest that randomized smoothing is a promising direction for future research into adversarially robust classiп¬Ғcation. Many empirical approaches have been вҖңbroken,вҖқ and provable approaches based on certifying neural network classiп¬Ғers have not been shown to scale to networks of modern size. It seems to be computationally infeasible to reason in any sophisticated way about the decision boundaries of a large, expressive neu- ral network. Randomized smoothing circumvents this prob- lem: the smoothed classiп¬Ғer is not itself a neural network, though it leverages the discriminative ability of a neural network base classiп¬Ғer. To make the smoothed classiп¬Ғer ro- bust, one need simply make the base classiп¬Ғer classify well under noise. In this way, randomized smoothing reduces the unsolved problem of adversarially robust classiп¬Ғcation to the comparably solved domain of supervised learning.Certiп¬Ғed Adversarial Robustness via Randomized Smoothing 6. We п¬Ғst prove the following lemma, which is essentially a restatement of the Neyman-Pearson lemma (Neyman & Pearson, 1933) from statistical hypothesis testing. Lemma 3 (Neyman-Pearson). Let X and Y be random variables in Rd with densities ВөX and ВөY. Let h: Rd вҶ’{0,1} be a random or deterministic function. Then: 1. The proof for part 2 is virtually identical, except both вҖңвүҘвҖқ become вҖңвүӨ.вҖқ Remark: connection to statistical hypothesis testing. Part 2 of Lemma 3 is known in the п¬Ғeld of statistical hypothesis testing as the Neyman-Pearson Lemma (Neyman & Pearson, 1933). The hypothesis testing problem is this: we are given a sample that comes from one of two distributions over Rd: either the null distribution X or the alternative distribution Y. We would like to identify which distribution the sample came from. It is worse to say вҖңYвҖқ when the true answer is вҖңXвҖқ than to say вҖңXвҖқ when the true answer is вҖңY.вҖқ Therefore we seek a (potentially randomized) procedure h: Rd вҶ’{0,1} which returns вҖңYвҖқ when the sample really came from X with probability no greater than some failure rate Оұ. In particular, out of all such rules h, we would like the uniformly most powerful one hвҲ—, i.e. the rule which is most likely to correctly say вҖңYвҖқ when the sample really came from Y. Neyman & Pearson (1933) showed that hвҲ—is the rule which returns вҖңYвҖқ deterministically on the set SвҲ—= {zвҲҲRd : ВөY (z) ВөX(z) вүҘt}for whichever tmakes P(X вҲҲSвҲ—) = Оұ. In other words, to state this in a form that looks like Part 2 of Lemma 3: if his a different rule with P(h(X) = 1) вүӨОұ, then hвҲ—is more powerful than h, i.e. P(h(Y) = 1) вүӨP(Y вҲҲSвҲ—). Practical algorithms In this appendix, we elaborate on the prediction and certiп¬Ғcation algorithms described in Section 3.2. The pseudocode in Section 3.2 makes use of several helper functions: вҖў SAMPLE UNDER NOISE (f, x, num, Пғ) works as follows: 1. Draw num samples of noise, Оө1 ...Оө num вҲјN(0,Пғ2I). 2. Run the noisy images through the base classiп¬Ғer f to obtain the predictions f(x+ Оө1),...,f (x+ Оөnum). 3. Return the counts for each class, where the count for class cis deп¬Ғned as вҲ‘num i=1 1[f(x+ Оөi) = c]. вҖў BINOM PVALUE (nA, nA+nB, p) returns the p-value of the two-sided hypothesis test thatnA вҲјBinomial(nA+nB,p). Using scipy.stats.binom test, this can be implemented as: binom test(nA, nA + nB, p). вҖў LOWER CONF BOUND (k, n, 1 вҲ’Оұ) returns a one-sided (1 вҲ’Оұ) lower conп¬Ғdence interval for the Binomial pa- rameter p given that k вҲјBinomial(n,p). In other words, it returns some number p for which p вүӨp with prob- ability at least 1 вҲ’Оұ over the sampling of k вҲј Binomial(n,p). Following Lecuyer et al. (2019), we chose to use the Clopper-Pearson conп¬Ғdence interval, which inverts the Binomial CDF (Clopper & Pearson, 1934). Using statsmodels.stats.proportion.proportion confint, this can be implemented as proportion_confint(k, n, alpha=2*alpha, method="beta")[0] C.1. Prediction The randomized algorithm given in pseudocode as PREDICT leverages the hypothesis test given in Hung & Fithian (2019) for identifying the top category of a multinomial distribution. PREDICT has one tunable hyperparameter, Оұ. When Оұis small, PREDICT abstains frequently but rarely returns the wrong class. When Оұis large, PREDICT usually makes a prediction, but may often return the wrong class. We now prove that with high probability, PREDICT will either return g(x) or abstain. Proposition 1 (restated). With probability at least 1 вҲ’Оұover the randomness in PREDICT , PREDICT will either abstain or return g(x). (Equivalently: the probability that PREDICT returns a class other than g(x) is at most Оұ.) Proof. For notational convenience, deп¬Ғne pc = P(f(x+ Оө) = c). Let cA = maxcpc. Notice that by deп¬Ғnition, g(x) = cA. We can describe the randomized procedure PREDICT as follows: 1. Sample a vector of class counts {nc}cвҲҲYfrom Multinomial({pc}cвҲҲY,n). 2. Let ЛҶcA = arg maxcnc be the class whose count is largest. Let nA and nB be the largest count and the second-largest count, respectively. 3. If the p-value of the two-sided hypothesis test that nA is drawn from Binom ( nA + nB,1 2 ) is less than Оұ, then return ЛҶcA. Else, abstain. The quantities cA and the pcвҖҷs are п¬Ғxed but unknown, while the quantitiesЛҶcA, the ncвҖҷs,nA, and nB are random. WeвҖҷd like to prove that the probability thatPREDICT returns a class other than cA is at most Оұ. PREDICT returns a class other than cA if and only if (1) ЛҶcA Мё= cA and (2) PREDICT does not abstain. We have: P(PREDICT returns class Мё= cA) = P(ЛҶcA Мё= cA,PREDICT does not abstain) = P(ЛҶcA Мё= cA) P(PREDICT does not abstain|ЛҶcA Мё= cA) вүӨP(PREDICT does not abstain|ЛҶcA Мё= cA)Certiп¬Ғed Adversarial Robustness via Randomized Smoothing Recall that PREDICT does not abstain if and only if the p-value of the two-sided hypothesis test that nA is drawn from Binom(nA + nB,1 2 ) is less than Оұ. Theorem 1 in Hung & Fithian (2019) proves that the conditional probability that this event occurs given that ЛҶcA Мё= cA is exactly Оұ. That is, P(PREDICT does not abstain|ЛҶcA Мё= cA) = Оұ Therefore, we have: P(PREDICT returns class Мё= cA) вүӨОұ C.2. Certiп¬Ғcation The certiп¬Ғcation task is: given some input xand a randomized smoothing classiп¬Ғer described by (f,Пғ), return both (1) the prediction g(x) and (2) a radius Rin which this prediction is certiп¬Ғed robust. This task requires identifying the class cA with maximal weight in f(x+ Оө), estimating a lower bound pA on pA := P(f(x+ Оө) = cA) and estimating an upper bound pB on pB := maxcМё=cA P(f(x+ Оө) = c) (Figure 1). Suppose for simplicity that we already knew cA and needed to obtain pA. We could collect nsamples of f(x+ Оө), count how many times f(x+ Оө) = cA, and use a Binomial conп¬Ғdence interval to obtain a lower bound on pA that holds with probability at least 1 вҲ’Оұover the nsamples. However, estimating pA and pB while simultaneously identifying the top class cA is a little bit tricky, statistically speaking. We propose a simple two-step procedure. First, use n0 samples from f(x+ Оө) to take a guess ЛҶcA at the identity of the top class cA. In practice we observed that f(x+ Оө) tends to put most of its weight on the top class, so n0 can be set very small. Second, use nsamples from f(x+ Оө) to obtain some pA and pB for which pA вүӨpA and pB вүҘpB with probability at least 1 вҲ’Оұ. We observed that it is much more typical for the mass of f(x+ Оө) not allocated to cA to be allocated entirely to one runner-up class than to be allocated uniformly over all remaining classes. Therefore, the quantity 1 вҲ’pA is a reasonably tight upper bound on pB. Hence, we simply set pB = 1 вҲ’pA, so our bound becomes R= Пғ 2 (ОҰвҲ’1(pA) вҲ’ОҰвҲ’1(1 вҲ’pA)) = Пғ 2 (ОҰвҲ’1(pA) + ОҰвҲ’1(pA)) = ПғОҰвҲ’1(pA) The full procedure is described in pseudocode as CERTIFY . If pA < 1 2 , we abstain from making a certiп¬Ғcation; this can occur especially if ЛҶcA Мё= g(x), i.e. if we misidentify the top class using the п¬Ғrst n0 samples of f(x+ Оө). Proposition 2 (restated). With probability at least 1 вҲ’Оұover the randomness in CERTIFY , if CERTIFY returns a class ЛҶcA and a radius R(i.e. does not abstain), then we have the robustness guarantee g(x+ Оҙ) = ЛҶcA whenever вҲҘОҙвҲҘ2 <R Proof. From the contract of LOWER CONF BOUND , we know that with probability at least 1 вҲ’Оұover the sampling of Оө1 ...Оө n, we have pA вүӨP[f(x+ Оө) = ЛҶcA]. Notice that CERTIFY returns a class and radius only if pA > 1 2 (otherwise it abstains). If pA вүӨP[f(x+ Оө) = ЛҶcA] and 1 2 <pA, then we can invoke Theorem 1 with pB = 1 вҲ’pA to obtain the desired guarantee.Certiп¬Ғed Adversarial Robustness via Randomized Smoothing D. Estimating the certiп¬Ғed test-set accuracy In this appendix, we show how to convert the вҖңapproximate certiп¬Ғed test accuracyвҖқ considered in the main paper into a lower bound on the true certiп¬Ғed test accuracy that holds with high probability over the randomness in CERTIFY . Consider a classiп¬Ғer g, a test set S = {(x1,c1) ... (xm,cm)}, and a radius r. For each example i вҲҲ[m], let zi indicate whether gвҖҷs prediction atxi is both correct and robust at radius r, i.e. zi = 1[g(xi + Оҙ) = ci вҲҖвҲҘОҙвҲҘ2 <r] The certiп¬Ғed test set accuracy of gat radius ris deп¬Ғned as 1 m вҲ‘m i=1 zi. If gis a randomized smoothing classiп¬Ғer, we cannot compute this quantity exactly, but we can estimate a lower bound that holds with arbitrarily high probability over the randomness in CERTIFY . In particular, suppose that we run CERTIFY with failure rate Оұon each example xi in the test set. Let the Bernoulli random variable Yi denote the event that on example i, CERTIFY returns the correct label cA = ci and a certiп¬Ғed radius Rwhich is greater than r. Let Y = вҲ‘m i=1 Yi. In the main paper, we referred to Y/m as the вҖңapproximate certiп¬Ғed accuracy.вҖқ It is вҖңapproximateвҖқ becauseYi = 1 does not mean that zi = 1. We now show how to exploit this fact to construct a one-sided conп¬Ғdence interval for the unobserved quantity 1 m вҲ‘m i=1 zi using the observed quantities Y and m. Theorem 6. For any ПҒ> 0, with probability at least 1 вҲ’ПҒover the randomness in CERTIFY , 1 m mвҲ‘ i=1 zi вүҘ 1 1 вҲ’Оұ ( Y m вҲ’ОұвҲ’ вҲҡ 2Оұ(1 вҲ’Оұ) log(1/ПҒ) m вҲ’log(1/ПҒ) 3m ) (16) Proof. Prediction Table 4 shows the performance of PREDICT as the number of Monte Carlo samples nis varied between 100 and 10,000. Suppose that for some test example (x,c), PREDICT returns the label ЛҶcA. We say that this prediction was correct if ЛҶcA = c and we say that this prediction was accurate if ЛҶcA = g(x). For example, a prediction could be correct but inaccurate if gis wrong at x, yet PREDICT accidentally returns the correct class. Ideally, weвҖҷd likePREDICT to be both correct and accurate. With n= 100 Monte Carlo samples and a failure rate of Оұ= 0.001, PREDICT is cheap to evaluate (0.15 seconds on our hardware) yet it attains relatively high top-1 accuracy of 65% on the ImageNet test set, and only abstains 12% of the time. When we use n= 10,000 Monte Carlo samples, PREDICT takes longer to evaluate (15 seconds), yet only abstains 4% of the time. Interestingly, we observe from Table 4 that most of the abstentions when n= 100 were for examples on which gwas wrong, so in practice we would lose little accuracy by taking nto be as small as 100. Training with Noise As mentioned in section 3.3, in the experiments for this paper, we followed Lecuyer et al. (2019) and trained the base classiп¬Ғer by minimizing the cross-entropy loss with Gaussian data augmentation. We now provide some justiп¬Ғcation for this idea. Let {(x1,c1),..., (xn,cn)} be a training dataset. We assume that the base classiп¬Ғer takes the form f(x) = arg maxcвҲҲYfc(x), where each fc is the scoring function for class c. Suppose that our goal is to maximize the sum of of the log-probabilities that f will classify each xi + Оөas ci: nвҲ‘ i=1 log PОө(f(xi + Оө) = ci) = nвҲ‘ i=1 log EОө 1 [ arg max c fc(xi + Оө) = ci ] (17) Recall that the softmax function can be interpreted as a continuous, differentiable approximation to arg max: 1 [ arg max c fc(xi + Оө) = ci ] вүҲ exp(fci(xi + Оө))вҲ‘ cвҲҲYexp(fc(xi + Оө)) Therefore, our objective is approximately equal to: nвҲ‘ i=1 log EОө [ exp(fci(xi + Оө))вҲ‘ cвҲҲYexp(fc(xi + Оө)) ] (18) By JensenвҖҷs inequality and the concavity oflog, this quantity is lower-bounded by: nвҲ‘ i=1 EОө [ log exp(fci(xi + Оө))вҲ‘ cвҲҲYexp(fc(xi + Оө)) ] which is the negative of the cross-entropy loss under Gaussian data augmentation. Therefore, minimizing the cross-entropy loss under Gaussian data augmentation will maximize (18), which will approxi- mately maximize (17).Certiп¬Ғed Adversarial Robustness via Randomized Smoothing G. Noise Level can Scale with Input Resolution Since our robustness guarantee (3) in Theorem 1 does not explicitly depend on the data dimension d, one might worry that randomized smoothing is less effective for images in high resolution вҖ” certifying a п¬Ғxed в„“2 radius is вҖңless impressiveвҖқ for, say, 224 Г—224 image than for a 56 Г—56 image. However, it turns out that in high resolution, images can be corrupted with larger levels of isotropic Gaussian noise while still preserving their content. This fact is made clear by Figure 12, which shows an image at high and low resolution corrupted by Gaussian noise with the same variance.full The class (вҖңhummingbirdвҖқ) is easy to discern from the high-resolution noisy image, but not from the low-resolution noisy image. As a consequence, in high resolution one can take Пғto be larger while still being able to obtain a base classiп¬Ғer that classiп¬Ғes noisy images accurately. Since our Theorem 1 robustness guarantee scales linearly with Пғ, this means that in high resolution one can certify larger radii. Figure 12.Top: An ImageNet image from class вҖңhummingbirdвҖқ in resolutions 56x56 (left) and 224x224 (right). Bottom: the same images corrupted by isotropic Gaussian noise at Пғ= 0.5. On noiseless images the class is easy to distinguish no matter the resolution, but on noisy data the class is much easier to distinguish when the resolution is high. The argument above can be made rigorous, though we п¬Ғrst need to decide what it means for two images to be high- and low-resolution versions of each other. Here we present one solution: Let Xdenote the space of вҖңhigh-resolutionвҖқ images in dimension2kГ—2kГ—3, and let XвҖІdenote the space of вҖңlow-resolutionвҖқ images in dimension kГ—kГ—3. Let AVGPOOL : XвҶ’X вҖІbe the function which takes as input an image xin dimension 2kГ—2kГ—3, averages together every 2x2 square of pixels, and outputs an image in dimension kГ—kГ—3. Equipped with these deп¬Ғnitions, we can say that(x,xвҖІ) вҲҲXГ—X вҖІare a high/low resolution image pair ifxвҖІ= AVGPOOL (x). Proposition 7. Given any smoothing classiп¬Ғer gвҖІ: XвҖІвҶ’Y, one can construct a smoothing classiп¬Ғer g : XвҶ’Y with the following property: for any xвҲҲX and xвҖІ= AVGPOOL (x), gpredicts the same class at xthat gвҖІpredicts at xвҖІ, but is certiп¬Ғably robust at twice the radius. Proof. Given some smoothing classiп¬Ғer gвҖІ= (fвҖІ,ПғвҖІ) from XвҖІto Y, deп¬Ғne gto be the smoothing classiп¬Ғer (f,Пғ) from X to Ywith noise level Пғ = 2ПғвҖІand base classiп¬Ғer f(x) = fвҖІ(AVGPOOL (x)). Note that the average of four independent copies of N(0,(2Пғ)2) is distributed as N(0,Пғ2). Therefore, for any high/low-resolution image pair xвҖІ= AVGPOOL (x), the random variable AVGPOOL (x+ Оө), where ОөвҲјN(0,(2Пғ)2I2kГ—2kГ—3), is equal in distribution to the random variable xвҖІ+ ОөвҖІ, where ОөвҖІвҲјN(0,Пғ2IkГ—kГ—3). Hence, f(x+ Оө) = fвҖІ(AVGPOOL (x+ Оө)) has the same distribution as fвҖІ(xвҖІ+ ОөвҖІ). By the deп¬Ғnition of g, this means that g(x) = gвҖІ(xвҖІ), Additionally, by Theorem 1, since Пғ = 2ПғвҖІ, this means that gвҖҷs prediction at xis certiп¬Ғably robust at twice the radius as gвҖІвҖҷs prediction atxвҖІ.Certiп¬Ғed Adversarial Robustness via Randomized Smoothing H. A generalization of KL divergence, the Оұ-Renyi divergence is an information theoretic measure of distance between two distributions. It is parameterized by some Оұ> 0. The Оұ-Renyi divergence between two discrete distributions P and Q is deп¬Ғned as: DОұ(P||Q) := 1 ОұвҲ’1 log ( kвҲ‘ i=1 pОұ i qОұвҲ’1 i ) (32) In the continuous case, this sum is replaced with an integral. The divergence is undeп¬Ғned when Оұ= 1 since a division by zero occurs, but the limit of DОұ(P||Q) as ОұвҶ’1 is the KL divergence between P and Q. Li et al. (2018) prove that if P is a discrete distribution for which the highest probability class has probability вүҘpA and all other classes have probability вүӨpB, then for any other discrete distribution Qfor which DОұ(P||Q) <вҲ’log ( 1 вҲ’pA вҲ’pB + 2 (1 2(pA 1вҲ’Оұ + pB 1вҲ’Оұ)1вҲ’Оұ )) (33) the highest-probability class in Qis guaranteed to be the same as the highest-probability class in P. (2018) bound wrtОұwhen pA = 0.999,pB = 0.0001 J. Experiment Details J.1. Comparison to baselines We compared randomized smoothing against three recent approaches for в„“2-robust classiп¬Ғcation (Tsuzuku et al., 2018; Wong et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2018). Tsuzuku et al. (2018) and Wong et al. (2018) propose both a robust training method and a complementary certiп¬Ғcation mechanism, while Zhang et al. (2018) propose a method to certify generically trained networks. In all cases we compared against networks provided by the authors. We compared against Wong et al. (2018) and Zhang et al. (2018) on CIFAR-10, and we compared against Tsuzuku et al. (2018) on SVHN. In image classiп¬Ғcation it is common practice to preprocess a dataset by subtracting from each channel the mean over the dataset, and dividing each channel by the standard deviation over the dataset. However, we wanted to report certiп¬Ғed radii in the original image coordinates rather than in the standardized coordinates. Therefore, throughout most of this work we п¬Ғrst added the Gaussian noise, and then standardized the channels, before feeding the image to the base classiп¬Ғer. (In the practical PyTorch implementation, the п¬Ғrst layer of the base classiп¬Ғer was a layer that standardized the input.) However, all of the baselines we compared against provided pre-trained networks which assumed that the dataset was п¬Ғrst preprocessed in a speciп¬Ғc way. Therefore, when comparing against the baselines we also preprocessed the datasets п¬Ғrst, so that we could report certiп¬Ғed radii that were directly comparable to the radii reported by the baseline methods. Comparison to Wong et al. (2018) Following Wong et al. (2018), the CIFAR-10 dataset was preprocessed by subtracting (0.485,0.456,0.406) and dividing by (0.225,0.225,0.225). While the body of the Wong et al. (2018) paper focuses on в„“вҲһcertiп¬Ғed robustness, their algorithm naturally extends to в„“2 certiп¬Ғed robustness, as developed in the appendix of the paper. We used three в„“2-trained residual networks publicly released by the authors, each trained with a different setting of their hyperparameterПөвҲҲ{0.157,0.628,2.51}. We used code publicly released by the authors athttps://github.com/locuslab/convex_adversarial/blob/master/Certiп¬Ғed Adversarial Robustness via Randomized Smoothing examples/cifar_evaluate.py to compute the robustness radius of test images. The code accepts a radius and returns TRUE (robust) or FALSE (not robust); we incorporated this subroutine into a binary search procedure to п¬Ғnd the largest radius for which the code returned TRUE. For randomized smoothing we usedПғ= 0.6 and a 20-layer residual network base classiп¬Ғer. We ranCERTIFY with n0 = 100, n= 100,000 and Оұ= 0.001. For both methods, we certiп¬Ғed the full CIFAR-10 test set. Comparison to Tsuzuku et al. (2018) Following Tsuzuku et al. (2018), the SVHN dataset was not preprocessed except that pixels were divided by 255 so as to lie within [0, 1]. We compared against a pretrained network provided to us by the authors in which the hyperparameter of their method was set to c= 0.1. The network was a wide residual network with 16 layers and a width factor of 4. We used the authorsвҖҷ code at https://github.com/ytsmiling/lmt to compute the robustness radius of test images. For randomized smoothing we usedПғ= 0.1 and a 20-layer residual network base classiп¬Ғer. We ranCERTIFY with n0 = 100, n= 100,000 and Оұ= 0.001. For both methods, we certiп¬Ғed the whole SVHN test set. Comparison to Zhang et al. (2018) Following Zhang et al. (2018), the CIFAR-10 dataset was preprocessed by subtracting 0.5 from each pixel. We compared against the cifar 7 1024 vanilla network released by the authors, which is a 7-layer MLP. We used the authorsвҖҷ code athttps://github.com/IBM/CROWN-Robustness-Certification to compute the robustness radius of test images. For randomized smoothing we usedПғ= 1.2 and a 20-layer residual network base classiп¬Ғer. We ranCERTIFY with n0 = 100, n= 100,000 and Оұ= 0.001. For randomized smoothing, we certiп¬Ғed the whole CIFAR-10 test set. For Zhang et al. (2018), we certiп¬Ғed every fourth image in the CIFAR-10 test set. J.2. ImageNet and CIFAR-10 Experiments Our code is available at http://github.com/locuslab/smoothing. In order to report certiп¬Ғed radii in the original coordinates, we п¬Ғrst added Gaussian noise, and then standardized the data. Speciп¬Ғcally, in our PyTorch implementation, the п¬Ғrst layer of the base classiп¬Ғer was a normalization layer that performed a channel-wise standardization of its input. For CIFAR-10 we subtracted the dataset mean (0.4914,0.4822,0.4465) and divided by the dataset standard deviation (0.2023,0.1994,0.2010). For ImageNet we subtracted the dataset mean (0.485,0.456,0.406) and divided by the standard deviation (0.229,0.224,0.225). For both ImageNet and CIFAR-10, we trained the base classiп¬Ғer with random horizontal п¬Ӯips and random crops (in addition to the Gaussian data augmentation discussed explicitly in the paper). On ImageNet we trained with synchronous SGD on four NVIDIA RTX 2080 Ti GPUs; training took approximately three days. On ImageNet our base classiп¬Ғer used the ResNet-50 architecture provided in torchvision. On CIFAR-10 we used a 110-layer residual network from https://github.com/bearpaw/pytorch-classification. On ImageNet we certiп¬Ғed every 100-th image in the validation set, for 500 images total. On CIFAR-10 we certiп¬Ғed the whole test set. In Figure 8 (middle) we п¬Ғxed Пғ= 0.25 and Оұ= 0.001 while varying the number of samples n. We did not actually vary the number of samples nthat we simulated: we kept this number п¬Ғxed at 100,000 but varied the number that we fed the Clopper-Pearson conп¬Ғdence interval. In Figure 8 (right), we п¬Ғxed Пғ= 0.25 and n=100,000 while varying Оұ.Certiп¬Ғed Adversarial Robustness via Randomized Smoothing J.3. Adversarial Attacks As discussed in Section 4, we subjected smoothed classiп¬Ғers to a projected gradient descent-style adversarial attack. We now describe the details of this attack. Let f be the base classiп¬Ғer and let Пғbe the noise level. Following Li et al. (2018), given an example (x,c) вҲҲRd Г—Y and a radius r, we used a projected gradient descent style adversarial attack to optimize the objective: arg max Оҙ:вҲҘОҙвҲҘ2<r EОөвҲјN(0,Пғ2I) [в„“(f(x+ Оҙ+ Оө),c)] (39) where в„“is the softmax loss function. (Breaking notation with the rest of the paper in which f returns a class, the function f here refers to the function that maps an image in Rd to a vector of classwise scores.) At each iteration of the attack, we drew ksamples of noise, Оө1 ...Оө k вҲјN(0,Пғ2I), and followed the stochastic gradient gt = вҲ‘k i=1 вҲҮОҙtв„“(f(x+ Оҙt + Оөk),c). As is typical (Kolter & Madry, 2018), we used a вҖңsteepest ascentвҖқ update rule, which, for the в„“2 norm, means that we normalized the gradient before applying the update. The overall PGD update is: Оҙt+1 = projr ( Оҙt + О· gt вҲҘgtвҲҘ ) where the function projr that projects its input onto the ball {z: вҲҘzвҲҘ2 вүӨr}is given by projr(z) = rz max(r,вҲҘzвҲҘ2) . We used a constant step size О·and a п¬Ғxed number T of PGD iterations. In practice, our step size was О· = 0.1, we used T = 20 steps of PGD, and we computed the stochastic gradient using k= 1000 Monte Carlo samples. Unfortunately, the objective we optimize (39) is not actually the attack objective of interest. To force a misclassiп¬Ғcation, an attacker needs to п¬Ғnd some perturbation Оҙwith вҲҘОҙвҲҘ2 <r and some class cB for which PОөвҲјN(0,Пғ2I)(f(x+ Оҙ+ Оө) = cB) вүҘPОөвҲјN(0,Пғ2I)(f(x+ Оҙ+ Оө) = c) Effective adversarial attacks against randomized smoothing are outside the scope of this paper.Certiп¬Ғed Adversarial Robustness via Randomized Smoothing K. Examples of Noisy Images We now show examples of CIFAR-10 and ImageNet images corrupted with varying levels of noise.
